# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Define a fuzzy set. How does it differ from a classical (crisp) set? Give one example.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **fuzzy set** $A$ on a universe of discourse $X$ is defined by a membership function $\mu_A : X \to [0,1]$, where each element $x \in X$ has a *degree of membership* $\mu_A(x)$ in the real interval $[0,1]$.
>
> **Difference from a crisp set:**
> - *Crisp set:* membership is binary — element is either in the set ($\mu = 1$) or not ($\mu = 0$).
> - *Fuzzy set:* membership is gradual — any value in $[0,1]$ is permitted, representing partial belongingness.
>
> **Example:** Let $A$ = "Tall people" on $X = \{150, 160, 170, 180, 190\}$ cm. We may define $\mu_A = \{0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.9, 1.0\}$. A person 170 cm tall is "half-tall" ($\mu = 0.5$), which a crisp set cannot represent. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

**State and explain the three primary operations on fuzzy sets.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Let $A$ and $B$ be fuzzy sets on $X$ with memberships $\mu_A(x)$ and $\mu_B(x)$.
>
> 1. **Union** ($A \cup B$): $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$. Represents the logical **OR**.
> 2. **Intersection** ($A \cap B$): $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$. Represents the logical **AND**.
> 3. **Complement** ($\bar{A}$): $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$. Represents the logical **NOT**.
>
> These operations generalize Boolean algebra to the continuous interval $[0,1]$, enabling approximate reasoning. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for Module 2: each 14-mark question has **internal choice**. Below we present **Question A** and **Question B** as two completely independent alternatives. Sub-parts are split **7 + 7 marks**.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define a fuzzy relation. Explain the max-min composition of two fuzzy relations with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)**. List the steps of the inference process. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Definition of Fuzzy Relation (2 marks):**
A **fuzzy relation** $R$ between two universes $X$ and $Y$ is a fuzzy set defined on the Cartesian product $X \times Y$, characterized by a bivariate membership function:

$$R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \mid (x, y) \in X \times Y\}$$

where $\mu_R : X \times Y \to [0, 1]$. Higher values of $\mu_R(x, y)$ indicate a stronger degree of association between $x$ and $y$.

**Max-Min Composition (4 marks):**
Let $R$ be a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ and $S$ be a fuzzy relation on $Y \times Z$. The **max-min composition** $R \circ S$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$ defined by:

$$\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \big[ \min( \mu_R(x, y), \, \mu_S(y, z) ) \big]$$

**Numerical Example (1 mark):**
Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$, with

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad S = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.8 \\ 0.4 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\mu_{R \circ S}(x_1, z_1) = \max_y \min(\mu_R(x_1, y), \mu_S(y, z_1)) = \max(\min(0.6, 0.5), \min(0.3, 0.4)) = \max(0.5, 0.3) = 0.5$.

The full composition is computed by taking all four $(x, z)$ pairs. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CRISP INPUT] --> A2[FUZZIFICATION]
    A2 --> A3[FUZZY RULE BASE]
    A3 --> A4[INFERENCE ENGINE]
    A4 --> A5[DEFUZZIFICATION]
    A5 --> A6[CRISP OUTPUT]
    A7[DATA BASE: MEMBERSHIP FUNCTIONS] -.-> A3
```

**Architecture Description (2 marks):**
A **Mamdani FIS** (Mamdani & Assilian, 1975) consists of four blocks:
1. **Fuzzification** — maps crisp inputs to fuzzy sets using MFs.
2. **Knowledge Base** — stores fuzzy rules and MF definitions.
3. **Inference Engine** — performs reasoning (firing rule aggregation).
4. **Defuzzification** — converts fuzzy output to a crisp scalar.

**Steps of the Mamdani Inference Process (2 marks):**
1. Compare crisp input variables with the MFs to obtain membership values (fuzzification).
2. For each rule, compute the *firing strength* by combining antecedent memberships using **min** (AND) or **product**.
3. Generate the *qualified consequent* by **clipping** (or **scaling**) the output MF at the firing strength.
4. **Aggregate** all qualified consequents into a single fuzzy output set (using **max**).
5. **Defuzzify** the aggregate set to a crisp output using centroid / bisector / MOM. **(7 marks total)**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain any **three membership functions** used in fuzzy logic with neat diagrams. Discuss the parameters that characterize them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **defuzzification**. Explain the **Centroid of Area (COG)** method and the **Mean of Maxima (MOM)** method with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Three membership functions (6 marks, 2 each):**

**1. Triangular MF:**
Defined by $\{a, b, c\}$ with $a < b < c$:

$$\mu_{tri}(x; a, b, c) = \max\!\left(0, \, \min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, \, \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$$

- **Parameters:** $a$ = left foot, $b$ = peak, $c$ = right foot.
- **Compact representation**; widely used due to simplicity.
- Peak membership of **1.0** at $x = b$; linearly decreasing on both sides.

**2. Trapezoidal MF:**
Defined by $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with $a < b \le c < d$:

$$\mu_{trap}(x; a, b, c, d) = \max\!\left(0, \, \min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, \, 1, \, \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$$

- **Parameters:** $a$ = left foot, $b$ = left shoulder, $c$ = right shoulder, $d$ = right foot.
- Plateau of full membership on $[b, c]$ — useful when a range of values is "fully" in the set.

**3. Gaussian MF:**
Defined by $\{c, \sigma\}$:

$$\mu_{gauss}(x; c, \sigma) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

- **Parameters:** $c$ = center (peak), $\sigma$ = spread (width).
- **Smooth**, infinitely differentiable — preferred in tuning algorithms like ANFIS.
- Symmetric bell shape; never reaches zero, only approaches it asymptotically.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Choice of MF is problem-dependent: triangular for simplicity, trapezoidal for range-based linguistic terms, and Gaussian for smooth, optimization-friendly models. **(7 marks total)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Definition of Defuzzification (2 marks):**
**Defuzzification** is the process of converting a fuzzy output set (aggregate of all qualified rule consequents) into a single crisp value suitable for use by an actuator, display, or downstream controller. It is the inverse of fuzzification.

**Centroid of Area (COG) Method (3 marks):**
Also called the **Center of Gravity (CoG)** method. The crisp output is the $x$-coordinate of the centroid of the area under the aggregated MF:

$$x^*_{COG} = \dfrac{\int_{x} x \cdot \mu_{agg}(x) \, dx}{\int_{x} \mu_{agg}(x) \, dx}$$

**Example:** For a triangular aggregate MF with vertices at $(0, 0), (5, 1), (10, 0)$:
- $\int_0^{10} x \cdot \mu(x) dx = 25$ (split into two right triangles).
- $\int_0^{10} \mu(x) dx = 5$ (area of triangle).
- $x^* = 25/5 = \mathbf{5}$ (matches the geometric center).

**Mean of Maxima (MOM) Method (2 marks):**
The crisp output is the average of all $x$ values at which the aggregated MF attains its maximum height:

$$x^*_{MOM} = \dfrac{1}{|M|} \sum_{x \in M} x, \quad M = \{x : \mu_{agg}(x) = \max \mu_{agg}\}$$

**Example:** If the aggregated MF has $\mu_{max} = 0.8$ achieved on the plateau $x \in [3, 7]$, then $x^*_{MOM} = (3+7)/2 = \mathbf{5}$. **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Zadeh's $\min/\max$ with probabilistic product/sum.** Both are valid (algebraic product/sum), but the *default* in KTU answers is Zadeh's $\min$ (AND) and $\max$ (OR). Always state which convention you adopt.
> 2. **Forgetting the bounded support.** The triangular MF is **zero** outside $[a, c]$ and the trapezoidal is **zero** outside $[a, d]$. Marks are deducted for implying the function is non-zero outside the support.
> 3. **Defuzzification direction.** Some students compute the *area of the aggregate* but forget the **$\int x \mu(x) dx$ weighted moment** in the numerator. The centroid is a *weighted* average.
> 4. **Mamdani vs. Sugeno confusion.** Mamdani has *fuzzy* consequents and needs defuzzification; Sugeno has *functional* consequents and is computed as a weighted average. Mixing them in a single system loses marks.
> 5. **Membership functions must be in $[0, 1]$.** If your $\mu(x)$ ever exceeds 1, the answer is wrong. Always verify the normalization condition.
> 6. **Hedge formulas are often swapped.** "Very" means **concentration** ($\mu^2$); "Somewhat" means **dilation** ($\mu^{0.5}$). Reversing these is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Fuzzy Logic**

- [x] **Fuzzy Set** $A$ on universe $X$ is a mapping $\mu_A : X \to [0, 1]$. **(1-mark definition)**
- [x] **Crisp vs Fuzzy:** Crisp = binary $\{0, 1\}$; Fuzzy = continuous $[0, 1]$.
- [x] **Membership Functions** (Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, Sigmoid, Bell).
- [x] **Triangular MF** parameters $\{a, b, c\}$; peak at $b$, support $[a, c]$.
- [x] **Trapezoidal MF** parameters $\{a, b, c, d\}$; plateau on $[b, c]$, support $[a, d]$.
- [x] **Gaussian MF** parameters $\{c, \sigma\}$; smooth, non-zero everywhere.
- [x] **Union:** $\max(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ — **OR**.
- [x] **Intersection:** $\min(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ — **AND**.
- [x] **Complement:** $1 - \mu_A$ — **NOT**.
- [x] **Algebraic Sum:** $\mu_A + \mu_B - \mu_A \mu_B$ — probabilistic OR.
- [x] **Algebraic Product:** $\mu_A \cdot \mu_B$ — probabilistic AND.
- [x] **De Morgan's Laws** hold for fuzzy sets: $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$.
- [x] **T-Norms** (fuzzy AND): min, product, Łukasiewicz, drastic.
- [x] **S-Norms / T-Conorms** (fuzzy OR): max, sum, Łukasiewicz, drastic.
- [x] **Linguistic Variables** take words as values; **Hedges** ("very", "somewhat") modify them.
- [x] **"Very"** $A$ = $\mu^2$; **"Somewhat"** $A$ = $\mu^{0.5}$; **"Extremely"** $A$ = $\mu^3$.
- [x] **Fuzzy Relation** is a fuzzy set on $X \times Y$.
- [x] **Max-Min Composition:** $\mu_{R \circ S}(x, z) = \max_y \min(\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(y, z))$.
- [x] **FIS Architecture:** Fuzzification → Knowledge Base → Inference Engine → Defuzzification.
- [x] **Mamdani FIS:** fuzzy consequent, requires defuzzification; linguistic.
- [x] **Sugeno (TSK) FIS:** functional (linear/constant) consequent; weighted average output; computationally efficient.
- [x] **Defuzzification Methods:** COG (centroid), BOA (bisector), MOM, SOM, LOM, weighted average.
- [x] **Centroid formula:** $x^* = \int x \mu dx / \int \mu dx$ — the most common KTU answer.
- [x] **Applications:** washing machines, ABS, AC, cameras, medical diagnosis, stock trading, robotics.
- [x] **Originator:** Lotfi A. Zadeh, 1965, UC Berkeley.
- [x] **First industrial application:** Mamdani's steam engine controller, 1974.
- [x] **Soft Computing position:** FL is one of three pillars — alongside **Neural Networks** and **Evolutionary Computation** (Genetic Algorithms).
- [x] **Exam trick:** When asked for a *fuzzy* answer, never give a *crisp* yes/no — always quantify the degree (e.g., "partially true", $\mu = 0.6$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuzzy Sets - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Sets - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fuzzy Set

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Zadeh, 1965):** A **Fuzzy Set** $\tilde{A}$ defined on a universal set (discourse universe) $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ that assigns to every element $x \in X$ a real number in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$, indicating the *degree of membership* (or grade of belongingness) of $x$ in $\tilde{A}$.

$$\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$$

where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \rightarrow [0, 1]$.

The crisp (classical) set is a special case where the membership function is binary:
$$\mu_{A}(x) \in \{0, 1\}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Speed Limit" Story

Imagine you are a traffic police officer judging whether a driver is "speeding." In a classical (crisp) set, you would rigidly say:
- Speed $\leq 60$ km/h → NOT speeding (membership = 0)
- Speed $> 60$ km/h → speeding (membership = 1)

But human judgment is gradual. A fuzzy set recognizes that:
- 60 km/h → membership 0.0 (clearly not speeding)
- 65 km/h → membership 0.2 (slightly over)
- 70 km/h → membership 0.5 (halfway)
- 80 km/h → membership 0.9 (almost certainly speeding)
- 90 km/h → membership 1.0 (definitely speeding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Unlike a classical set (black-and-white), a fuzzy set provides a **gradient of belongingness** (gray-scale), perfectly modelling linguistic, ambiguous, or imprecise concepts like "tall," "hot," "fast," or "expensive."

## 1.3 Need for Fuzzy Sets (Why Not Crisp Sets?)

| Problem with Crisp Sets | Fuzzy Set Solution |
|------------------------|--------------------|
| Binary classification (yes/no) | Continuous degree $[0,1]$ |
| Cannot represent ambiguity | Naturally models vagueness |
| Brittle to small input changes | Smooth, gradual transitions |
| Poor at linguistic variables | Excellent for human-like reasoning |

## 1.4 Terminology Standardization (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

- **Universe of Discourse ($X$):** The complete collection of all possible elements under consideration.
- **Membership Function ($\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$):** The defining function that maps $X \to [0,1]$.
- **Grade (Degree) of Membership:** The numerical value of $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.
- **Fuzzy Singleton:** A fuzzy set whose support contains exactly one element.
- **Empty Fuzzy Set:** A fuzzy set with all memberships equal to **0**.
- **Universal Fuzzy Set:** A fuzzy set with all memberships equal to **1**.

---

## 1.5 Notation Convention (KTU-Standard)

Two equivalent notations are universally accepted:
1. **Zadeh's Notation:**
$$\tilde{A} = \frac{\mu_1}{x_1} + \frac{\mu_2}{x_2} + \cdots + \frac{\mu_n}{x_n}$$
(where $+$ and division are **NOT** arithmetic — they are set-builder separators).

2. **Set-Builder Notation:**
$$\tilde{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_i)}{x_i} \quad \text{(discrete case)}$$

3. **Integral Form (Continuous Universe):**
$$\tilde{A} = \int_X \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{x}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Fuzzy Sets

A fuzzy set is described by several structural properties. The following is the **canonical list** the KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce verbatim.

### 2.1.1 Support of a Fuzzy Set

The **support** of $\tilde{A}$, denoted $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A})$, is the **crisp set** of all elements in $X$ that have **non-zero** membership in $\tilde{A}$.

$$\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The support tells you *where the fuzzy set actually lives* — all elements with any positive presence at all.

### 2.1.2 Core of a Fuzzy Set

The **core** of $\tilde{A}$, denoted $\text{Core}(\tilde{A})$, is the crisp set of all elements that have **full membership** (membership value $= 1$) in $\tilde{A}$.

$$\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$$

### 2.1.3 Height of a Fuzzy Set

The **height** of $\tilde{A}$ is the **largest** membership value attained by any element in $\tilde{A}$.

$$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, the fuzzy set is called **Normal**. If $h(\tilde{A}) < 1$, it is called **Subnormal**.

### 2.1.4 Normality (Normalized Fuzzy Set)

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is **normal** if there exists at least one $x \in X$ such that $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$. Any subnormal fuzzy set can be **normalized** by dividing every membership value by $h(\tilde{A})$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}_{norm}}(x) = \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{h(\tilde{A})}$$

### 2.1.5 α-Cut (Alpha-Cut) — The Bridge Between Fuzzy and Crisp

The **α-cut** (strict) of $\tilde{A}$ is the crisp set of all elements whose membership grade is **at least** $\alpha$, where $\alpha \in (0, 1]$.

$$A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$$

The **strong α-cut** uses strict inequality:

$$A_{\alpha}^{>} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$$

The **level set** is defined as:

$$\Lambda_{\tilde{A}} = \{\alpha \in [0,1] \mid A_{\alpha} \neq \emptyset\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** An α-cut is a horizontal "slice" through the membership function. As α approaches 1, the α-cut shrinks to the core.

### 2.1.6 Convexity of a Fuzzy Set

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is **convex** if and only if **all** of its α-cuts are convex (in the standard sense) for every $\alpha \in (0,1]$. Equivalently:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2))$$

for all $x_1, x_2 \in X$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$.

### 2.1.7 Cardinality and Other Measures

For a finite universal set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$:

- **Scalar (Sigma-Count) Cardinality:**
$$|\tilde{A}| = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_i)$$

- **Relative Cardinality (Proportion):**
$$\|\tilde{A}\| = \frac{|\tilde{A}|}{n}$$

### 2.1.8 Bandwidth / Crossover Points

The **crossover point** of $\tilde{A}$ is the element $x$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5$ (fuzzy boundary).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Mathematical Definition | Remarks |
|---|----------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Support | $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ | Crisp subset of $X$ |
| 2 | Core | $\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ | Subset of Support |
| 3 | Height | $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Single scalar in $[0,1]$ |
| 4 | Normality | $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$ | Exists if any $\mu=1$ |
| 5 | α-Cut | $A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ | Bridges fuzzy to crisp |
| 6 | Strong α-Cut | $A_{\alpha}^{>} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$ | Open level |
| 7 | Convexity | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min(\mu(x_1),\mu(x_2))$ | All α-cuts convex |
| 8 | Sigma-Count | $\vert\tilde{A}\vert = \sum_i \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_i)$ | Sum of memberships |
| 9 | Relative Cardinality | $\vert\vert\tilde{A}\vert\vert = \frac{\vert\tilde{A}\vert}{n}$ | Normalized count |
| 10 | Level Set | $\Lambda_{\tilde{A}} = \{\alpha \mid A_{\alpha} \neq \emptyset\}$ | Set of valid α values |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These properties are the *engineering handles* used to convert fuzzy inputs into crisp decisions in real-world systems such as **fuzzy controllers** (washing machines, air conditioners), **expert systems** (medical diagnosis), and **automotive applications** (anti-lock braking). α-cuts, in particular, form the mathematical bridge enabling **Defuzzification**.

### 2.3 Decomposition Theorem (Resolution Identity)

A fundamental result that allows reconstruction of a fuzzy set from its α-cuts:

$$\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in [0,1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ is a scalar multiplication forming a fuzzy set with membership $\alpha$ on $A_{\alpha}$ and 0 elsewhere. The union uses the max operator.

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the **highest-yield theorems** for KTU Module 2 — expect it in Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 2.4 Set-Theoretic Properties (Algebraic Laws)

For fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C}$ in $X$, the following algebraic identities hold (assuming the standard min-max operators for $\cap$ and $\cup$):

| # | Law | Union ($\cup$) Form | Intersection ($\cap$) Form |
|---|-----|---------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Idempotence | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A} = \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A} = \tilde{A}$ |
| 2 | Commutativity | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \tilde{B} \cup \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \tilde{B} \cap \tilde{A}$ |
| 3 | Associativity | $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cup \tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C})$ | $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cap \tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cap \tilde{C})$ |
| 4 | Distributivity | $\tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cap \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cap (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{C})$ | $\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C})$ |
| 5 | Identity | $\tilde{A} \cup \emptyset = \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap X = \tilde{A}$ |
| 6 | Absorption | $\tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) = \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) = \tilde{A}$ |
| 7 | Involution | $\overline{\overline{\tilde{A}}} = \tilde{A}$ | (Complement of complement) |
| 8 | De Morgan's | $\overline{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}} = \overline{\tilde{A}} \cap \overline{\tilde{B}}$ | $\overline{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}} = \overline{\tilde{A}} \cup \overline{\tilde{B}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** The **Law of Excluded Middle** ($\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}} = X$) and **Law of Non-Contradiction** ($\tilde{A} \cap \overline{\tilde{A}} = \emptyset$) **DO NOT** hold in fuzzy set theory. Examiners *frequently* set trick questions on this.

### 2.5 Complement Operation (Standard Negation)

The **standard fuzzy complement** is defined as:

$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

The complement must satisfy two conditions:
- Boundary: $\mu(0) = 1$ and $\mu(1) = 0$
- Monotonic Non-Increasing: $\mu(a) \geq \mu(b)$ if $a \leq b$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These algebraic laws form the foundation of **Fuzzy Logic** used in programmable logic controllers (PLCs), image processing (fuzzy edge detection), and **fuzzy database queries**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing All Properties of a Fuzzy Set

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question Style]**

### Problem Statement
Consider the universal set $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}$ representing speeds in km/h. Define the fuzzy set "Fast" as:

$$\text{FAST} = \{(10, 0.0), (20, 0.1), (30, 0.3), (40, 0.6), (50, 0.9), (60, 1.0), (70, 1.0)\}$$

Compute: **(a)** Support, **(b)** Core, **(c)** Height, **(d)** Normality check, **(e)** α-cuts for $\alpha = 0.3, 0.6, 0.9$, **(f)** Sigma-count, **(g)** Convexity check.

---

### (a) Support

By definition:
$$\text{Supp}(\text{FAST}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x) > 0\}$$

Scanning each element:
- $\mu(10) = 0.0$ → not included (not $> 0$)
- $\mu(20) = 0.1 > 0$ → included
- $\mu(30) = 0.3 > 0$ → included
- $\mu(40) = 0.6 > 0$ → included
- $\mu(50) = 0.9 > 0$ → included
- $\mu(60) = 1.0 > 0$ → included
- $\mu(70) = 1.0 > 0$ → included

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\text{Supp}(\text{FAST}) = \{20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}}$$

---

### (b) Core

By definition:
$$\text{Core}(\text{FAST}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x) = 1\}$$

Scanning each element:
- $\mu(60) = 1.0$ → included
- $\mu(70) = 1.0$ → included
- All other values are $< 1$ → excluded

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\text{Core}(\text{FAST}) = \{60, 70\}}$$

---

### (c) Height

By definition:
$$h(\text{FAST}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x)$$

The maximum membership value is found by inspecting all elements:
- $\{0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.0, 1.0\}$

The supremum is:
$$\boxed{h(\text{FAST}) = 1.0}$$

---

### (d) Normality Check

Since $h(\text{FAST}) = 1.0$ and there exists at least one element (in fact, two: 60 and 70) with membership equal to 1:
$$\boxed{\text{FAST is a NORMAL fuzzy set}}$$

No normalization is required.

---

### (e) α-Cuts

**For $\alpha = 0.3$:**
$$A_{0.3} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x) \geq 0.3\}$$

Checking each:
- $\mu(10) = 0.0 < 0.3$ → exclude
- $\mu(20) = 0.1 < 0.3$ → exclude
- $\mu(30) = 0.3 \geq 0.3$ → **include**
- $\mu(40) = 0.6 \geq 0.3$ → **include**
- $\mu(50) = 0.9 \geq 0.3$ → **include**
- $\mu(60) = 1.0 \geq 0.3$ → **include**
- $\mu(70) = 1.0 \geq 0.3$ → **include**

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A_{0.3} = \{30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}}$$

**For $\alpha = 0.6$:**
$$A_{0.6} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x) \geq 0.6\}$$

Checking each:
- $\mu(10)=0.0, \mu(20)=0.1, \mu(30)=0.3$ → all $< 0.6$ → exclude
- $\mu(40) = 0.6 \geq 0.6$ → **include**
- $\mu(50) = 0.9 \geq 0.6$ → **include**
- $\mu(60) = 1.0 \geq 0.6$ → **include**
- $\mu(70) = 1.0 \geq 0.6$ → **include**

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A_{0.6} = \{40, 50, 60, 70\}}$$

**For $\alpha = 0.9$:**
$$A_{0.9} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x) \geq 0.9\}$$

Checking:
- $\mu(50) = 0.9 \geq 0.9$ → **include**
- $\mu(60) = 1.0 \geq 0.9$ → **include**
- $\mu(70) = 1.0 \geq 0.9$ → **include**
- All others have memberships $< 0.9$ → exclude

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A_{0.9} = \{50, 60, 70\}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Observation:** As $\alpha$ increases, the α-cut monotonically shrinks:
> $$A_{0.3} \supseteq A_{0.6} \supseteq A_{0.9}$$
> This is a **theorem**: $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2 \implies A_{\alpha_1} \subseteq A_{\alpha_2}$.

---

### (f) Sigma-Count (Cardinality)

$$|\text{FAST}| = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x_i)$$

$$|\text{FAST}| = 0.0 + 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.6 + 0.9 + 1.0 + 1.0$$

Performing the sum step by step:
- $0.0 + 0.1 = 0.1$
- $0.1 + 0.3 = 0.4$
- $0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0$
- $1.0 + 0.9 = 1.9$
- $1.9 + 1.0 = 2.9$
- $2.9 + 1.0 = 3.9$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{|\text{FAST}| = 3.9}$$

The relative cardinality is:
$$\|\text{FAST}\| = \frac{3.9}{7} \approx 0.557$$

---

### (g) Convexity Check

We must verify that for any two elements $x_1, x_2$ in $X$ and any $\lambda \in [0,1]$:
$$\mu_{\text{FAST}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\text{FAST}}(x_1), \mu_{\text{FAST}}(x_2))$$

Equivalent test: **All α-cuts must be convex intervals.**

Inspecting:
- $A_{0.3} = \{30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}$ — contiguous interval ✓
- $A_{0.6} = \{40, 50, 60, 70\}$ — contiguous interval ✓
- $A_{0.9} = \{50, 60, 70\}$ — contiguous interval ✓

All α-cuts form **connected intervals** in $X$ (treated as ordered). Therefore:
$$\boxed{\text{FAST is a CONVEX fuzzy set}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Set Operations and Algebraic Laws

Given:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(a, 0.4), (b, 0.7), (c, 0.2)\}$$
$$\tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.6), (b, 0.3), (c, 0.8)\}$$

Compute (using min for $\cap$ and max for $\cup$):

### Union ($\cup$)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

$$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(a, \max(0.4, 0.6)), (b, \max(0.7, 0.3)), (c, \max(0.2, 0.8))\}$$

Step-by-step:
- $\max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $\max(0.7, 0.3) = 0.7$
- $\max(0.2, 0.8) = 0.8$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.6), (b, 0.7), (c, 0.8)\}}$$

### Intersection ($\cap$)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

Step-by-step:
- $\min(0.4, 0.6) = 0.4$
- $\min(0.7, 0.3) = 0.3$
- $\min(0.2, 0.8) = 0.2$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.4), (b, 0.3), (c, 0.2)\}}$$

### Complement
$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

$$\overline{\tilde{A}} = \{(a, 0.6), (b, 0.3), (c, 0.8)\}$$

### Verifying the Failure of Excluded Middle

Compute $\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}}$:
- $\max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $\max(0.7, 0.3) = 0.7$
- $\max(0.2, 0.8) = 0.8$

$$\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}} = \{(a, 0.6), (b, 0.7), (c, 0.8)\} \neq X$$

This demonstrates that $\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}} \neq X$, confirming the **non-validity of the Law of Excluded Middle** in fuzzy set theory.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Proving an Algebraic Identity

**Prove:** $\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C})$ (Distributive Law).

**Proof:** For any $x \in X$:

LHS:
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C})}(x) = \min\left(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}}(x)\right)$$

By definition of union:
$$\mu_{\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x))$$

So LHS becomes:
$$\text{LHS} = \min\left(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \max(\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x))\right)$$

RHS:
$$\mu_{(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C})}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C}}(x))$$

By definition of intersection:
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x))$$

So RHS becomes:
$$\text{RHS} = \max\left(\min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)), \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x))\right)$$

To show $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, let $a = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), b = \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x), c = \mu_{\tilde{C}}(x) \in [0,1]$. We need:
$$\min(a, \max(b, c)) = \max(\min(a, b), \min(a, c))$$

**Case 1:** If $b \geq c$, then $\max(b, c) = b$.
- LHS: $\min(a, b)$
- RHS: $\max(\min(a, b), \min(a, c))$
- Since $b \geq c$, we have $\min(a, b) \geq \min(a, c)$ (when $a$ is fixed, the min with the larger value is larger).
- So RHS $= \min(a, b)$ = LHS ✓

**Case 2:** If $c \geq b$, by symmetry, LHS = RHS ✓

Thus the identity holds for all $a, b, c \in [0,1]$, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Decomposition Theorem Application

Given the same FAST set from Example 1, reconstruct $\tilde{A}$ using its α-cuts and the Decomposition Theorem.

The level set is $\Lambda = \{0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.0\}$ (the distinct membership values).

For each $\alpha \in \Lambda$:
- $\alpha = 0.1$: $A_{0.1} = \{20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}$
- $\alpha = 0.3$: $A_{0.3} = \{30, 40, 50, 60, 70\}$
- $\alpha = 0.6$: $A_{0.6} = \{40, 50, 60, 70\}$
- $\alpha = 0.9$: $A_{0.9} = \{50, 60, 70\}$
- $\alpha = 1.0$: $A_{1.0} = \{60, 70\}$

By the Decomposition Theorem:
$$\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in \Lambda} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$$

For example, the contribution of $\alpha = 0.6$ is the fuzzy set:
$$0.6 \cdot A_{0.6} = \{(40, 0.6), (50, 0.6), (60, 0.6), (70, 0.6)\}$$

Taking the union (max) over all $\alpha$ values yields the original fuzzy set — confirming the Decomposition Theorem.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Membership Range | Input Form | Output Form | Computational Complexity |
|----------|------------------|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Support | $[0, 1]$ | Fuzzy set | Crisp set | $O(n)$ |
| Core | $\{1\}$ only | Fuzzy set | Crisp set | $O(n)$ |
| Height | $[0, 1]$ | Fuzzy set | Scalar | $O(n)$ |
| α-Cut | Discrete α | Fuzzy set | Crisp set | $O(n)$ per α |
| Sigma-Count | Sum | Fuzzy set | Scalar | $O(n)$ |
| Convexity Check | Pairwise | Fuzzy set | Boolean | $O(n \log n)$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with respect to fuzzy sets:**
**(a) Support of a fuzzy set**
**(b) Height of a fuzzy set**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Support:** The support of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on a universal set $X$ is the crisp set of all elements of $X$ whose membership grade in $\tilde{A}$ is strictly greater than zero. Mathematically:
$$\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$

**(b) Height:** The height $h(\tilde{A})$ of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is the largest membership value attained by any element in $\tilde{A}$. It is the supremum of the membership function over $X$:
$$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

If $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, the fuzzy set is called **normal**; otherwise, it is **subnormal**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is an α-cut? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
An **α-cut** (or alpha-level set) of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is the crisp set of all elements whose membership grade is greater than or equal to a specified threshold $\alpha \in (0, 1]$.

Formally:
$$A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$$

**Example:** Let $\tilde{A} = \{(10, 0.2), (20, 0.5), (30, 0.8), (40, 1.0)\}$ on $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40\}$.

For $\alpha = 0.6$:
$$A_{0.6} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.6\} = \{30, 40\}$$

The α-cut operation **converts fuzzy data into crisp data**, forming the basis for defuzzification and the Decomposition Theorem.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Define a fuzzy set. List and explain any **five properties** of fuzzy sets with suitable mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider two fuzzy sets defined on $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.9), (x_3, 0.1), (x_4, 0.6)\}$$
$$\tilde{B} = \{(x_1, 0.7), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.8), (x_4, 0.5)\}$$
Compute: (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\overline{\tilde{A}}$, (iv) Verify whether the Law of Excluded Middle holds. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A **fuzzy set** $\tilde{A}$ on a universal set $X$ is a collection of ordered pairs:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$$
where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x): X \to [0, 1]$ is the membership function.

**Five properties of fuzzy sets:**

1. **Support:**
$$\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$
This is the crisp subset of $X$ on which the fuzzy set has positive presence.

2. **Core:**
$$\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$$
This is the set of elements that belong fully to the fuzzy set.

3. **Height and Normality:**
$$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$
If $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, the fuzzy set is **normal**; otherwise, it is **subnormal**.

4. **α-Cut (Alpha-Level Set):**
$$A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in (0, 1]$$
This bridges fuzzy sets to crisp sets.

5. **Cardinality (Sigma-Count):**
$$|\tilde{A}| = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_i)$$
This gives a scalar measure of the "size" of a fuzzy set.

*(Other properties that can be mentioned: Convexity, Bandwidth, Crossover Point, Level Set.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Union $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$** (using $\max$):
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \max(0.4, 0.7) = 0.7$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \max(0.9, 0.3) = 0.9$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \max(0.1, 0.8) = 0.8$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x_4) = \max(0.6, 0.5) = 0.6$$
$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(x_1, 0.7), (x_2, 0.9), (x_3, 0.8), (x_4, 0.6)\}}$$

**(ii) Intersection $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$** (using $\min$):
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_1) = \min(0.4, 0.7) = 0.4$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_2) = \min(0.9, 0.3) = 0.3$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_3) = \min(0.1, 0.8) = 0.1$$
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x_4) = \min(0.6, 0.5) = 0.5$$
$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{(x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.1), (x_4, 0.5)\}}$$

**(iii) Complement $\overline{\tilde{A}}$** (using $1 - \mu$):
$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x_1) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$
$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x_2) = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1$$
$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x_3) = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$$
$$\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x_4) = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$
$$\boxed{\overline{\tilde{A}} = \{(x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.1), (x_3, 0.9), (x_4, 0.4)\}}$$

**(iv) Verification of Law of Excluded Middle:**

Compute $\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}}$:
- $\max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6$
- $\max(0.9, 0.1) = 0.9$
- $\max(0.1, 0.9) = 0.9$
- $\max(0.6, 0.4) = 0.6$

$$\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}} = \{(x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.9), (x_3, 0.9), (x_4, 0.6)\} \neq X$$

Since $\tilde{A} \cup \overline{\tilde{A}}$ does **not** equal the universal set $X$ (whose membership would be 1 everywhere), the **Law of Excluded Middle does not hold** in fuzzy set theory. This is a fundamental difference from classical (crisp) set theory.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Define the **α-cut** and **Decomposition Theorem** of a fuzzy set. State and prove the decomposition theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuzzy set is defined on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ as:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.2), (2, 0.5), (3, 0.8), (4, 1.0), (5, 0.6)\}$$
Compute all α-cuts, the support, core, height, and verify the decomposition theorem. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**α-Cut Definition:** The α-cut (or α-level set) of a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ on $X$ is:
$$A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in [0, 1]$$
with $A_0 = X$ by convention.

**Decomposition Theorem Statement:** Every fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ can be uniquely expressed as the union (max) of scaled α-cuts:
$$\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in [0, 1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$$
where $\alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ denotes the fuzzy set with membership $\alpha$ on $A_{\alpha}$ and 0 elsewhere.

**Proof Outline:**

**(Step 1) Forward direction ($\tilde{A} \subseteq \bigcup \alpha \cdot A_\alpha$):** For any $x \in X$ with $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \beta$:
- By definition of α-cut, $x \in A_\beta$ since $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \beta \geq \beta$.
- The scaled set $\beta \cdot A_\beta$ has membership $\beta$ at $x$.
- Therefore, $\mu_{\bigcup \alpha \cdot A_\alpha}(x) \geq \beta = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.

**(Step 2) Reverse direction ($\bigcup \alpha \cdot A_\alpha \subseteq \tilde{A}$):** For any $x \in X$:
- If $x \in A_\alpha$ for some $\alpha$, then $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha$.
- The membership of $x$ in $\alpha \cdot A_\alpha$ is $\alpha \leq \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.
- Taking the union over all $\alpha$ does not exceed $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.

Therefore, equality holds: $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha} \alpha \cdot A_\alpha$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.2), (2, 0.5), (3, 0.8), (4, 1.0), (5, 0.6)\}$

**Distinct membership values (Level Set):**
$$\Lambda = \{0.2, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0\}$$

**α-Cuts:**
- $A_{0.2} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
- $A_{0.5} = \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$
- $A_{0.6} = \{3, 4, 5\}$
- $A_{0.8} = \{3, 4\}$
- $A_{1.0} = \{4\}$

**Support:** $\text{Supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} = X$ (all memberships are positive).

**Core:** $\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{4\}$ (only element with membership = 1).

**Height:** $h(\tilde{A}) = 1.0$ → **Normal fuzzy set**.

**Verification of Decomposition Theorem:**

For each element, the maximum α for which it belongs to $A_\alpha$ equals its membership:
- $\mu(1) = 0.2$: $1 \in A_{0.2}$ but $1 \notin A_{0.5}$ ✓
- $\mu(2) = 0.5$: $2 \in A_{0.5}$ but $2 \notin A_{0.6}$ ✓
- $\mu(3) = 0.8$: $3 \in A_{0.8}$ but $3 \notin A_{1.0}$ ✓
- $\mu(4) = 1.0$: $4 \in A_{1.0}$ ✓
- $\mu(5) = 0.6$: $5 \in A_{0.6}$ but $5 \notin A_{0.8}$ ✓

Taking $\bigcup \alpha \cdot A_\alpha$ recovers $\tilde{A}$ exactly. The Decomposition Theorem is verified.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing support and core** in α-cut computations: Support uses strict $> 0$; Core uses $= 1$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for each mix-up.
> - **Forgetting the formula for normality check**: Students often write $h = 1$ without specifying it means the fuzzy set is normal. Always state the implication.
> - **Using $\cap$ and $\cup$ as arithmetic operators**: In Zadeh's notation $\frac{\mu_1}{x_1} + \frac{\mu_2}{x_2}$, the $+$ is NOT addition. It's a set-builder separator.
> - **Assuming the Law of Excluded Middle holds**: This is a frequent trick question. Always show the counterexample explicitly.
> - **Forgetting to verify convexity through α-cuts** rather than by direct computation: This saves time and earns full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fuzzy Set Definition:** $\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$ with $\mu_{\tilde{A}}: X \to [0, 1]$.
- [x] **Support** = crisp set where $\mu(x) > 0$. **Core** = crisp set where $\mu(x) = 1$.
- [x] **Height** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. If $h = 1$ → **normal**; else **subnormal**.
- [x] **α-Cut** $A_\alpha = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ bridges fuzzy to crisp.
- [x] **Strong α-Cut** uses strict $> \alpha$ instead of $\geq \alpha$.
- [x] **Monotonicity of α-cuts:** $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2 \implies A_{\alpha_1} \subseteq A_{\alpha_2}$.
- [x] **Convex fuzzy set:** All α-cuts are convex intervals.
- [x] **Sigma-Count** $|\tilde{A}| = \sum \mu(x_i)$. **Relative** form divides by $|X|$.
- [x] **Decomposition Theorem:** $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha} \alpha \cdot A_\alpha$ (max-yield identity).
- [x] **Standard operators:** Union = max, Intersection = min, Complement = $1 - \mu$.
- [x] **Algebraic laws that hold:** Commutativity, Associativity, Distributivity, Idempotence, Involution, De Morgan's, Identity, Absorption.
- [x] **Laws that FAIL in fuzzy theory:** Law of Excluded Middle, Law of Non-Contradiction.
- [x] **Convexity test** is equivalent to: $\mu(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min(\mu(x_1), \mu(x_2))$.
- [x] **Normalization formula:** $\mu_{norm}(x) = \mu(x) / h(\tilde{A})$.
- [x] **Zadeh's notation $\frac{\mu}{x}$ uses $+$ as a separator**, not arithmetic addition.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Fuzzy controllers, expert systems, image processing, decision support, automotive ABS, AC climate control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fuzzy Membership Function** is a mathematical function that maps every element **x** of a universe of discourse **X** to a real number in the closed interval **[0, 1]**, quantifying the *degree of belonging* of that element to a fuzzy set. It is formally defined as:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}} : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

where:
- $\tilde{A}$ is a **fuzzy set** defined on the universe of discourse $X$
- $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the **membership grade** (or membership value) of element $x$ in $\tilde{A}$
- The value **1** represents full membership, **0** represents no membership, and values strictly between them represent **partial membership**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike classical (crisp) sets where $\mu(x) \in \{0, 1\}$, a fuzzy membership function allows the full continuum $[0, 1]$, enabling the modelling of **vagueness, ambiguity, and linguistic imprecision** that pervade real-world engineering systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are asked to classify the **height** of a person as "Tall". In classical (crisp) logic, you must draw a sharp line, say, at 180 cm. Anyone above it is "Tall" (membership = 1), anyone below is "Not Tall" (membership = 0). This is unrealistic.

A **Fuzzy Membership Function** smooths this transition. A person of 170 cm might be "slightly tall" (membership = 0.4), a person of 185 cm might be "very tall" (membership = 0.95), and someone of 200 cm is "definitely tall" (membership = 1.0). The function essentially *quantifies the gradual transition* between "Not Tall" and "Definitely Tall".

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A fuzzy membership function is a *bridge* that converts human linguistic reasoning ("hot", "fast", "expensive", "old") into a mathematically tractable numeric form. It does NOT add randomness; it captures **graded belonging** — a deterministic, but non-binary, classification.

### 1.3 Key Distinctions: Crisp vs Fuzzy Membership

| Aspect | Crisp Set | Fuzzy Set |
|---|---|---|
| Function Range | $\{0, 1\}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Transition | Abrupt (step) | Gradual (smooth curve) |
| Information | Lossy (binary) | Information-preserving |
| Suitability | Precise systems | Human-centric, vague systems |
| Set Notation | $A$ | $\tilde{A}$ (tilde denotes fuzziness) |

### 1.4 Core Features of a Membership Function

- **Degree of Compatibility:** Quantifies how well $x$ represents the linguistic label.
- **Subjective but Deterministic:** Two experts may design *different* MFs for the same concept, but each is a precise mathematical function.
- **Order-Preserving:** Typically, $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is monotonically non-decreasing for terms like "Tall", "Hot", "Fast".
- **Universe Restriction:** The MF is non-zero only over a finite region called the **support** of the fuzzy set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Membership functions form the **front-end (Fuzzification block)** of every Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) used in washing machines, air conditioners, autonomous vehicles, medical diagnosis, and stock-market prediction systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Properties of a Membership Function

For a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ with membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ on universe $X$, the following structural regions are universally defined in KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Core (Kernel):** The set of all points $x \in X$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$. Denoted $C(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$.
- **Support:** The set of all points where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0$. Denoted $S(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$.
- **Boundary:** The region where $0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1$. Denoted $B(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\}$.
- **Height:** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. A fuzzy set is **normal** if $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reference for KTU 2024 ESE — commit these equations to memory.**

| # | MF Type | Mathematical Form | Parameters | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Triangular** | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, \frac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | $\{a, b, c\}$ with $a < b < c$ | Simplest, fastest, real-time control |
| 2 | **Trapezoidal** | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, 1, \frac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with $a < b \leq c < d$ | "Comfortable" zones, flat-tops |
| 3 | **Gaussian** | $\mu(x) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Center $c$, spread $\sigma > 0$ | Smooth systems, no sharp edges |
| 4 | **Generalized Bell** | $\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$ | Width $a > 0$, slope $b > 0$, center $c$ | Asymmetric, flexible boundaries |
| 5 | **Sigmoid** | $\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a(x-c)}}$ | Slope $a \neq 0$, inflection $c$ | Open-left / open-right MFs |
| 6 | **Singleton** | $\mu(x) = 1$ if $x = x_0$ else $0$ | Singleton point $x_0$ | Fuzzification of crisp inputs |
| 7 | **S-shaped (Sigmoidal)** | Cubic polynomial: rising from 0 to 1 between $a$ and $b$ | Inflection point $a$, saturation $b$ | "Very High", "Extremely Low" |
| 8 | **Z-shaped / Pi-shaped** | Asymmetric S-curves | Two control points | Boundary transitions |

### 2.3 Notation Notes for KTU

- The expression $\left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert$ uses `\vert` to keep tables safe from markdown pipe conflicts.
- The order of parameters is **standardized in KTU 2024 model solutions** as: parameters $\to$ universe position $\to$ shape description.
- For any normalized MF, $\mu : X \to [0, 1]$.

### 2.4 Why These Specific MFs? (The 'Why' and 'How')

- **Triangular/Trapezoidal:** Composed of straight lines, they are **piecewise-linear**, computationally cheap, and preferred in **embedded real-time controllers** (Mamdani-type FIS).
- **Gaussian/Bell:** Smooth, infinitely differentiable, ideal for **gradient-based learning** (ANFIS, neuro-fuzzy systems), no sharp corners.
- **Sigmoid:** Naturally arises in **neural networks**; ideal for the open-ended sets like "Very High" with no clear upper saturation.
- **Singleton:** A **degenerate MF**; used when input is already crisp but you wish to pass it through a fuzzy pipeline (Sugeno-style systems).
- **S, Z, Pi MFs:** Built from cubic splines; used when **boundary smoothness** is mandatory (e.g., medical diagnostic MFs).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** "Speed" MF in adaptive cruise control (Gaussian).
- **HVAC:** "Comfortable Temperature" in smart ACs (Trapezoidal flat-top).
- **Industrial Process Control:** "Pressure deviation" with Sigmoid MFs.
- **Bio-informatics:** Gene-expression levels with Generalized Bell MFs.
- **Agriculture:** Soil moisture classification with Triangular MFs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of MF is **NOT arbitrary** — it must be justified by the **physical nature** of the variable. The KTU examiner will award 1–2 marks simply for correct MF selection reasoning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Triangular Membership Function

A triangular MF with parameters $\{a, b, c\}$ on the universe $X = \mathbb{R}$ is defined to satisfy three geometric conditions:

1. **Zero** at and below the left anchor $a$.
2. **Linearly increasing** to a **peak of 1** at the centre $b$.
3. **Linearly decreasing** to **zero** at the right anchor $c$.

**Step 1: Rising segment equation.**
We pass through $(a, 0)$ and $(b, 1)$. The slope is:

$$m_{\text{rise}} = \frac{1 - 0}{b - a} = \frac{1}{b - a}$$

Using point-slope form anchored at $(a, 0)$:

$$\mu(x) - 0 = m_{\text{rise}} (x - a)$$

$$\mu_{\text{rise}}(x) = \frac{x - a}{b - a}, \quad \text{for } a \leq x \leq b$$

**Step 2: Falling segment equation.**
We pass through $(b, 1)$ and $(c, 0)$. The slope is:

$$m_{\text{fall}} = \frac{0 - 1}{c - b} = \frac{-1}{c - b}$$

Using point-slope form anchored at $(b, 1)$:

$$\mu(x) - 1 = m_{\text{fall}} (x - b)$$

$$\mu_{\text{fall}}(x) = 1 - \frac{x - b}{c - b} = \frac{c - x}{c - b}, \quad \text{for } b \leq x \leq c$$

**Step 3: Unified piecewise form.**

$$\mu_{\triangle}(x; a, b, c) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \frac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ \frac{c - x}{c - b}, & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

This derivation is foundational because it demonstrates the **principle of linear interpolation between fuzziness anchor points** — a recurring KTU problem-type.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Gaussian Membership Function

A Gaussian MF must satisfy:
1. Peak value of **1** at centre $c$.
2. **Symmetric** spread around $c$.
3. **Smooth, bell-shaped** decay to zero.

**Step 1: Start from the standard normal density.**

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

**Step 2: Discard the constant normalizer** $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}$ because at $x = c$ we require $\mu(c) = 1$ and the exponential term itself is 1 at the centre.

**Step 3: Retain the exponential core** and define:

$$\mu_{\text{Gauss}}(x; c, \sigma) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

**Step 4: Verify normalization.**

At $x = c$:

$$\mu_{\text{Gauss}}(c) = e^{0} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

As $\vert x - c \vert \to \infty$:

$$\mu_{\text{Gauss}}(x) \to e^{-\infty} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The Gaussian MF has a **support of $(-\infty, \infty)$**, i.e., it never truly reaches zero. In practice, a **3-sigma cutoff** at $\mu(x) \approx 0.011$ is treated as the effective boundary.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Generalized Bell Membership Function

**Step 1: Begin with a reciprocal power form.**

To obtain a peak of 1 at $x = c$ and decay to 0 as $x \to \pm\infty$:

$$\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + Q(x)}$$

where $Q(c) = 0$ at the centre and $Q(x) \to \infty$ as $\vert x \vert \to \infty$.

**Step 2: Choose $Q(x)$ as a power law.**

$$Q(x) = \left\vert \frac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}$$

This satisfies both conditions: $Q(c) = 0^{2b} = 0$ and $Q(x) \to \infty$ as $\vert x \vert \to \infty$.

**Step 3: Substitute and verify.**

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(x; a, b, c) = \frac{1}{1 + \left\vert \frac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$$

At $x = c$:

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(c) = \frac{1}{1 + 0} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

As $\vert x \vert \to \infty$:

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(x) \to \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Parameter roles:**
- $a > 0$: controls **half-width** (spread).
- $b > 0$: controls **slope steepness** at the boundaries.
- $c$: the **centre** of the curve.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Fuzzification Example (Triangular MF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problem**

**Problem:** A fuzzy set "Warm" is defined on temperature $X = \{0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$ °C with a triangular MF having parameters $a = 20$, $b = 30$, $c = 40$. Compute the fuzzified output for input $x = 25$ °C.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the region. Since $20 \leq 25 \leq 30$, we are in the rising segment.

Step 2: Apply the rising-segment formula:

$$\mu_{\text{Warm}}(25) = \frac{25 - 20}{30 - 20} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

Step 3: Interpretation. The temperature 25 °C is "half-warm" — neither cold nor truly warm.

This is the **fuzzification** operation: **crisp input → fuzzy membership grade**.

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a fuzzy membership function. How does it differ from a characteristic function of a crisp set?*

**Model Answer:**

A **fuzzy membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ assigns to every element $x$ in the universe of discourse $X$ a real number in the interval $[0, 1]$, representing its **degree of belonging** to a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$.

In contrast, the **characteristic function** $\chi_A(x)$ of a crisp set $A$ maps elements to the discrete set $\{0, 1\}$, allowing only full membership (1) or non-membership (0) — no intermediate values.

**Key differences:**
- Range: $[0, 1]$ (fuzzy) vs $\{0, 1\}$ (crisp).
- Transition: Gradual (fuzzy) vs abrupt (crisp).
- Information: Captures partial truth (fuzzy) vs binary truth (crisp).
- Symbolism: $\tilde{A}$ (fuzzy) vs $A$ (crisp).

**(3 marks: Definition 1.5, Differences 1.5)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*List any THREE standard types of fuzzy membership functions and state one engineering application of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | MF Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Triangular** | Temperature control in a smart AC |
| 2 | **Gaussian** | Noise-robust sensor data smoothing in autonomous vehicles |
| 3 | **Trapezoidal** | "Comfort zone" classification in HVAC systems |

**(3 marks: 1 mark per correct pair)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the parameter roles** (e.g., what $a$, $b$, $\sigma$ represent) — this costs 2–3 marks.
> - Drawing a **rough sketch** of the MF is **MANDATORY** for full marks. Skipping the sketch = −2 marks.
> - Always specify the **universe of discourse** before defining the MF.
> - Do not confuse the **support** with the **core**; the difference between $\mu > 0$ and $\mu = 1$ is critical.
> - For a Gaussian MF, students often write $\mu(x) = e^{-(x-c)^2 / \sigma^2}$ (missing the factor 2 in the denominator) — this is **incorrect**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for a **triangular membership function** with parameters $\{a, b, c\}$. Show that the function is normalized. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Define the geometric constraints — left anchor at $a$, peak at $b$, right anchor at $c$, with $a < b < c$.

Step 2: Derive the rising segment between $(a, 0)$ and $(b, 1)$:

$$\mu_{\text{rise}}(x) = \frac{x - a}{b - a}, \quad a \leq x \leq b$$

Step 3: Derive the falling segment between $(b, 1)$ and $(c, 0)$:

$$\mu_{\text{fall}}(x) = \frac{c - x}{c - b}, \quad b \leq x \leq c$$

Step 4: Combine with zero regions:

$$\mu_{\triangle}(x; a, b, c) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \frac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ \frac{c - x}{c - b}, & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

Step 5: Normalization check.

At $x = b$:

$$\mu_{\triangle}(b) = \frac{b - a}{b - a} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $\sup_{x} \mu_{\triangle}(x) = 1$, the function is **normalised**.

Step 6: Sketch the piecewise linear curve with three labelled segments and a peak at $b$.

**(7 marks: derivation 3, piecewise form 2, normalisation 1, sketch 1)**

---

**(b)** For a temperature control system, the linguistic variable "Temperature" is defined on $X = [0, 50]$ °C with three fuzzy sets: **Cold** (triangular, parameters 0, 10, 20), **Warm** (triangular, parameters 15, 25, 35), **Hot** (triangular, parameters 30, 40, 50). Fuzzify the crisp input $T = 22$ °C. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: For each fuzzy set, identify the appropriate region for $x = 22$:

- **Cold:** $0, 10, 20$. Since $x = 22 > 20$, we are in the right-zero region.
  $$\mu_{\text{Cold}}(22) = 0$$

- **Warm:** $15, 25, 35$. Since $15 \leq 22 \leq 25$, we are in the rising segment.
  $$\mu_{\text{Warm}}(22) = \frac{22 - 15}{25 - 15} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.7$$

- **Hot:** $30, 40, 50$. Since $x = 22 < 30$, we are in the left-zero region.
  $$\mu_{\text{Hot}}(22) = 0$$

Step 2: Tabulate the fuzzified result.

| Fuzzy Set | Membership Grade |
|---|---|
| Cold | 0.0 |
| Warm | 0.7 |
| Hot | 0.0 |

Step 3: Interpretation. The temperature 22 °C is classified as **70% Warm** and 0% Cold and 0% Hot. This is the **fuzzified vector** $[0, 0.7, 0]$ fed into the rule base.

Step 4: Sketch the three triangular MFs and mark the fuzzification point at $T = 22$ °C.

**(7 marks: computation 3, table 1, interpretation 1, sketch 2)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **Gaussian** and **Generalized Bell** membership functions. State the role of each parameter. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Gaussian MF:**

$$\mu_{\text{Gauss}}(x; c, \sigma) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

- **Parameter $c$:** The centre of the bell; the point of maximum membership, $\mu(c) = 1$.
- **Parameter $\sigma$:** Controls the spread/width. Larger $\sigma$ → wider bell; smaller $\sigma$ → narrower bell.

*Sketch:* Symmetric bell curve centred at $c$, with inflection points at $c \pm \sigma$.

**Generalized Bell MF:**

$$\mu_{\text{bell}}(x; a, b, c) = \frac{1}{1 + \left\vert \frac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$$

- **Parameter $a$:** Controls the half-width (spread).
- **Parameter $b$:** Controls the slope steepness at the crossover points. Larger $b$ → steeper.
- **Parameter $c$:** The centre where $\mu = 1$.

*Sketch:* Bell curve with controllable steepness and width.

**(7 marks: 2 per function description + parameters, 1 for sketches combined)**

---

**(b)** A fuzzy set "Fast" on $X = [0, 200]$ km/h is described by a Gaussian MF with $c = 100$ and $\sigma = 25$. Compute the membership grades for $x = 75$, $x = 100$, and $x = 150$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The membership function is:

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(x) = e^{-\frac{(x - 100)^2}{2 \cdot 25^2}} = e^{-\frac{(x - 100)^2}{1250}}$$

**Case 1: $x = 75$**

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(75) = e^{-\frac{(75 - 100)^2}{1250}} = e^{-\frac{625}{1250}} = e^{-0.5}$$

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(75) \approx 0.6065$$

**Case 2: $x = 100$**

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(100) = e^{-\frac{(100 - 100)^2}{1250}} = e^{0} = 1.0000$$

**Case 3: $x = 150$**

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(150) = e^{-\frac{(150 - 100)^2}{1250}} = e^{-\frac{2500}{1250}} = e^{-2}$$

$$\mu_{\text{Fast}}(150) \approx 0.1353$$

**Summary table:**

| Speed (km/h) | Membership Grade | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 75 | 0.6065 | Moderately fast |
| 100 | 1.0000 | Fully fast |
| 150 | 0.1353 | Slightly fast |

**(7 marks: 2 for setting up equation, 4 for three evaluations, 1 for summary table)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A fuzzy membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}} : X \to [0, 1]$ quantifies the **degree of belonging** of $x$ to a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$.
- **Fuzzification:** The process of converting a crisp input $x$ into its membership grade $\mu(x)$ — the entry point of every FIS.
- **Triangular MF:** Simplest, 3 parameters $\{a, b, c\}$; ideal for real-time controllers.
- **Trapezoidal MF:** 4 parameters $\{a, b, c, d\}$; provides a **flat plateau** at $\mu = 1$ between $b$ and $c$.
- **Gaussian MF:** $\mu(x) = e^{-(x-c)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$; smooth, symmetric, no true zero (3-sigma cutoff used).
- **Generalized Bell MF:** $\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \vert (x-c)/a \vert^{2b}}$; flexible boundary slopes.
- **Sigmoid MF:** $\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a(x-c)}}$; ideal for open-left or open-right fuzzy sets.
- **Singleton MF:** $\mu(x_0) = 1$, else $0$; used for crisp-input fuzzification (Sugeno systems).
- **Normalisation:** A fuzzy set is *normal* if $\max \mu(x) = 1$. Triangular, Trapezoidal, Gaussian, and Bell MFs are all normalised by construction.
- **Core:** $\{x \mid \mu(x) = 1\}$. **Support:** $\{x \mid \mu(x) > 0\}$. **Boundary:** $\{x \mid 0 < \mu(x) < 1\}$.
- **Choice Criteria:** MF selection depends on **system dynamics, computational budget, and smoothness requirements** — never arbitrary.
- **Always sketch** the MF for full marks in KTU ESE answers.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing singleton (degenerate) MF with crisp set membership — singletons are still fuzzy-theoretic objects.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Topper's Strategy:** In the 14-mark question, always provide (1) the formula, (2) parameter description, (3) a labelled sketch, and (4) a worked numerical example. This 4-part structure consistently scores 12+ marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
