topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Features of Fuzzy Membership Functions, Operations on Fuzzy Sets, Linguistic Variables ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Fuzzy Set — The Foundation Recap A **Fuzzy Set** $A$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_A(x)$ that maps every element $x \in X$ to a value in the closed interval $[0, 1]$: $$A = \{ (x, \mu_A(x)) \mid x \in X \}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike a classical (crisp) set where $\mu_A(x) \in \{0, 1\}$ (binary), a fuzzy set permits *graded* membership. A value of $\mu_A(x) = 0.8$ means $x$ belongs 80% to the concept represented by $A$. ### 1.2 Fuzzy Membership Function (FMF) — Formal Definition A **Fuzzy Membership Function (FMF)** is a mathematical function $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that assigns to each element $x$ in the universe of discourse $X$ a grade of membership in the fuzzy set $A$. > [!NOTE] > The membership function is the *soul* of a fuzzy set. It is a subjective measure — different experts may construct different FMFs for the same linguistic concept depending on context and convention. ### 1.3 Features of Fuzzy Membership Functions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The five canonical features of any FMF are the *core*, *support*, *boundary*, *crossover points*, and *fuzziness*. These are tested almost every exam cycle. | # | Feature | Formal Definition | Intuitive Meaning | |---|---------|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Core** | $\text{core}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ | The region of *full* membership — elements that **definitely** belong. | | 2 | **Support** | $\text{supp}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ | The region of *non-zero* membership — elements that **partially** belong. | | 3 | **Boundary** | $\text{bnd}(A) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$ | The fuzzy region — elements with **partial** membership. | | 4 | **Crossover Point** | $\mu_A(x) = 0.5$ | The point of *maximum ambiguity* (50-50 belonging). | | 5 | **Fuzziness** | $\text{Fuzziness}(A) = \frac{\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid}{\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid}$ | A measure of how "fuzzy" the set is (between 0 and 1). | ### 1.4 Operations on Fuzzy Sets — Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you describe the weather as "warm" and "humid" simultaneously. How would you combine these two fuzzy perceptions? The fuzzy set operations of **Union (OR)**, **Intersection (AND)**, and **Complement (NOT)** give a mathematically rigorous way to do exactly that. For two fuzzy sets $A$ and $B$ in universe $X$ with membership functions $\mu_A(x)$ and $\mu_B(x)$: - **Union** ($A \cup B$): The "OR" operation — an element belongs to the union if it belongs to *at least one* of the sets. - **Intersection** ($A \cap B$): The "AND" operation — an element belongs to the intersection if it belongs to *both* sets. - **Complement** ($\bar{A}$): The "NOT" operation — the degree of non-membership. ### 1.5 Linguistic Variable — Formal Introduction A **Linguistic Variable** is a variable whose values are *words or sentences* in a natural or artificial language, rather than numerical values (Zadeh, 1975). > [!NOTE] > Example: Instead of saying "Temperature = 35°C", we say "Temperature = *Hot*". Here, "Temperature" is the linguistic variable and "Hot" is a linguistic value. A linguistic variable is fully characterized by the quintuple: $$\langle X, T(X), U, G, M \rangle$$ where: - $X$ = name of the linguistic variable (e.g., "Temperature") - $T(X)$ = term set (set of linguistic values, e.g., {Cold, Warm, Hot}) - $U$ = universe of discourse (e.g., $[0, 50]$ °C) - $G$ = syntactic rule for generating linguistic values - $M$ = semantic rule mapping each linguistic value to a fuzzy set > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** *Hedges* are linguistic modifiers (e.g., "very", "more or less", "slightly", "extremely") that operate on the membership function to shift it. They are central to fuzzy logic controllers. ### 1.6 Intuition Summary > [!TIP] > **One-line memory anchor:** > - **FMF** = the *bridge* between crisp numbers and fuzzy words. > - **Operations** = fuzzy versions of AND, OR, NOT. > - **Linguistic Variable** = a *word* (like "Tall") backed by a *fuzzy set*. > - **Hedges** = *linguistic amplifiers* that reshape the FMF. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Deeper Look at FMF Features #### 2.1.1 Core The set of all elements in $X$ that have a *complete* membership grade of 1. $$\text{core}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$$ - If the FMF is *normalized* (i.e., $\sup_{x \in X} \mu_A(x) = 1$), the core is non-empty. - A non-normalized FMF (e.g., a sigmoid that never reaches 1) has an **empty core**. #### 2.1.2 Support The set of all elements in $X$ that have a *positive* (non-zero) membership grade. $$\text{supp}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$$ - It represents the "extent" of the fuzzy set in the universe. - For a triangular or trapezoidal FMF, the support is the open interval between the *outer* base points. #### 2.1.3 Boundary The set of all elements with strictly partial membership: $$\text{bnd}(A) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$$ - Always: $\text{bnd}(A) = \text{supp}(A) \setminus \text{core}(A)$. - The boundary is the *fuzzy* region of the set; if it is empty, the set reduces to a crisp set. #### 2.1.4 Crossover Points The points where the FMF equals the constant 0.5: $$\text{Crossover}(A) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 0.5\}$$ - These are the points of *maximum fuzziness* for that set. - They are critical in fuzzy set comparison and in defining *alpha-cuts* of intermediate value. #### 2.1.5 Fuzziness A scalar measure of how much a fuzzy set deviates from being crisp: $$f(A) = \frac{\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid}{\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid}$$ - $f(A) = 0 \Rightarrow A$ is a crisp set (no boundary). - $f(A) = 1 \Rightarrow A$ is *maximally fuzzy* (core is a single point of zero measure). ### 2.2 Deeper Look at Fuzzy Set Operations The three primitive operations are defined pointwise for all $x \in X$: $$\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x) \quad \text{(Complement)}$$ $$\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x)) \quad \text{(Union - S-norm)}$$ $$\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x)) \quad \text{(Intersection - T-norm)}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **max-min** system is the *standard* Zadeh formulation. Other families (algebraic, bounded, drastic, Hamacher) exist and are collectively called **S-norms** and **T-norms**. #### 2.2.1 Properties of Fuzzy Set Operations | Property | Holds? | Notes | |----------|--------|-------| | Commutativity | ✅ Yes | $A \cup B = B \cup A$ | | Associativity | ✅ Yes | $(A \cup B) \cup C = A \cup (B \cup C)$ | | Distributivity | ✅ Yes | $A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$ | | Idempotency | ✅ Yes | $A \cup A = A$, $A \cap A = A$ | | Identity | ✅ Yes | $A \cup \emptyset = A$, $A \cap X = A$ | | **Excluded Middle** | ❌ **No** | $A \cup \bar{A} \neq X$ in general | | **Contradiction** | ❌ **No** | $A \cap \bar{A} \neq \emptyset$ in general | | Involution | ✅ Yes | $\overline{\overline{A}} = A$ | | De Morgan's Laws | ✅ Yes | $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Trap:** The *Law of Excluded Middle* ($A \cup \bar{A} = X$) and *Law of Contradiction* ($A \cap \bar{A} = \emptyset$) — which are *always true* in crisp set theory — **fail in fuzzy set theory**. This is a high-frequency 3-mark question. ### 2.3 Deeper Look at Linguistic Variables #### 2.3.1 The Quintuple Definition $$LV = \langle X, T(X), U, G, M \rangle$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Example for "Height" | |--------|---------|----------------------| | $X$ | Variable name | Height | | $T(X)$ | Term set of linguistic values | {Short, Medium, Tall, Very Tall} | | $U$ | Universe of discourse | $[0, 250]$ cm | | $G$ | Syntactic rule (grammar) | Generates "Very Tall" from "Tall" | | $M$ | Semantic rule | Maps "Tall" to FMF $\mu_{Tall}(h)$ | #### 2.3.2 Primary Terms vs. Composite Terms - **Primary terms** (atomic): Short, Medium, Tall — defined directly. - **Composite terms** (derived): Very Tall, More or Less Tall, Slightly Tall — formed by hedges. #### 2.3.3 Standard Hedges and Their Mathematical Effect > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Sheet for Hedges:** | Hedge | Linguistic Meaning | Effect on FMF | Mathematical Operation | |-------|---------------------|---------------|------------------------| | **Very** | Concentration / intensification | Sharper curve, narrower | $\mu_{Very\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$ | | **Extremely** | Strongest intensification | Even sharper | $\mu_{Extremely\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^3$ | | **More or Less** | Dilation / weakening | Flatter, wider | $\mu_{MOL\,A}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)}$ | | **Slightly** | Mild weakening | Slight flattening | $\mu_{Slightly\,A}(x) = \sqrt[\mu_A(x)]{\text{...}}$ or use $\sqrt[3]{\mu_A(x)}$ | | **Not** | Negation | Mirror image | $\mu_{Not\,A}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ | | **Plus** | Faint intensification | Slight sharpening | $\mu_{Plus\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^{1.25}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why squaring?** If $\mu_{Tall}(1.80) = 0.8$, then $\mu_{Very\,Tall}(1.80) = 0.8^2 = 0.64$. The higher the original grade, the more dramatically "Very" shrinks it. This is mathematically consistent with the intuition that "Very" *increases the threshold* of the concept. #### 2.3.4 Real-World Utility - **Fuzzy Logic Controllers (FLCs)** in washing machines, air conditioners, and rice cookers rely entirely on linguistic variables like "Wet, Damp, Dry" or "Cold, Warm, Hot" with hedges like "Slightly Wet" or "Very Hot". - **Automotive engineering**: "Braking force" linguistic variable with values "Light, Medium, Hard, Very Hard". - **Medical diagnosis**: linguistic variables for symptoms ("Severe Pain", "Mild Fever"). - **Stock market analysis**: linguistic values for "Bullish" market modified by "Slightly" or "Very". ### 2.4 Comprehensive Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 2 Topic:** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | Fuzzy set notation | $A = \{(x, \mu_A(x))\}$ | $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0,1]$ | | 2 | Core | $\text{core}(A) = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ | Normalized ⇒ non-empty | | 3 | Support | $\text{supp}(A) = \{x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ | Extent of set | | 4 | Boundary | $\text{bnd}(A) = \{x \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$ | = Support \ Core | | 5 | Crossover | $\mu_A(x) = 0.5$ | Maximum ambiguity | | 6 | Fuzziness | $f(A) = \frac{\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid}{\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid}$ | $0 \leq f(A) \leq 1$ | | 7 | Complement | $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ | Zadeh complement | | 8 | Union | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ | S-norm | | 9 | Intersection | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A, \mu_B)$ | T-norm | | 10 | De Morgan | $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ | Holds in fuzzy | | 11 | Hedge: Very | $[\mu_A(x)]^2$ | Concentration | | 12 | Hedge: Extremely | $[\mu_A(x)]^3$ | Stronger concentration | | 13 | Hedge: More or Less | $\sqrt{\mu_A(x)}$ | Dilation | | 14 | Hedge: Not | $1 - \mu_A(x)$ | Negation | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Identifying FMF Features > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Consider the fuzzy set $A$ = "Comfortable Temperature" defined on the universe $X = \{10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40\}$°C as: > > $A = \{ (10, 0.0), (15, 0.3), (20, 0.7), (25, 1.0), (30, 0.8), (35, 0.4), (40, 0.0) \}$ > > Find the Core, Support, Boundary, Crossover Points, and Fuzziness. #### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the Core.** We need all $x$ such that $\mu_A(x) = 1$. $$\text{core}(A) = \{25\}$$ **Step 2: Identify the Support.** We need all $x$ such that $\mu_A(x) > 0$. $$\text{supp}(A) = \{15, 20, 25, 30, 35\}$$ > The support has 5 elements, so $\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid = 5$. **Step 3: Identify the Boundary.** Boundary = elements with $0 < \mu_A(x) < 1$. $$\text{bnd}(A) = \{15, 20, 30, 35\}$$ > The boundary has 4 elements, so $\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid = 4$. **Step 4: Identify Crossover Points.** Crossover = elements with $\mu_A(x) = 0.5$. $$\text{Crossover}(A) = \emptyset$$ > No element in the universe has a membership grade of exactly 0.5. **Step 5: Compute Fuzziness.** $$f(A) = \frac{\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid}{\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid}$$ $$f(A) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$ > Since $f(A) = 0.8$ is close to 1, the set $A$ is *highly fuzzy* — most of its support lies in the boundary region. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fuzzy Set Operations > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$ with two fuzzy sets: > > $A = \{ (x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.2) \}$ > > $B = \{ (x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.8), (x_4, 0.9) \}$ > > Compute $A \cup B$, $A \cap B$, and $\bar{A}$. #### Step 1: Compute the Union $A \cup B$ (pointwise max) $$\mu_{A \cup B}(x_i) = \max(\mu_A(x_i), \mu_B(x_i))$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{A \cup B}(x_1) &= \max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6 \\ \mu_{A \cup B}(x_2) &= \max(0.7, 0.3) = 0.7 \\ \mu_{A \cup B}(x_3) &= \max(1.0, 0.8) = 1.0 \\ \mu_{A \cup B}(x_4) &= \max(0.2, 0.9) = 0.9 \end{aligned} $$ $$A \cup B = \{ (x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.9) \}$$ #### Step 2: Compute the Intersection $A \cap B$ (pointwise min) $$\mu_{A \cap B}(x_i) = \min(\mu_A(x_i), \mu_B(x_i))$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{A \cap B}(x_1) &= \min(0.4, 0.6) = 0.4 \\ \mu_{A \cap B}(x_2) &= \min(0.7, 0.3) = 0.3 \\ \mu_{A \cap B}(x_3) &= \min(1.0, 0.8) = 0.8 \\ \mu_{A \cap B}(x_4) &= \min(0.2, 0.9) = 0.2 \end{aligned} $$ $$A \cap B = \{ (x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.8), (x_4, 0.2) \}$$ #### Step 3: Compute the Complement $\bar{A}$ $$\mu_{\bar{A}}(x_i) = 1 - \mu_A(x_i)$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{\bar{A}}(x_1) &= 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 \\ \mu_{\bar{A}}(x_2) &= 1 - 0.7 = 0.3 \\ \mu_{\bar{A}}(x_3) &= 1 - 1.0 = 0.0 \\ \mu_{\bar{A}}(x_4) &= 1 - 0.2 = 0.8 \end{aligned} $$ $$\bar{A} = \{ (x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.0), (x_4, 0.8) \}$$ #### Verification of De Morgan's Law $$\overline{A \cup B} \stackrel{?}{=} \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$$ From the union computed above: $A \cup B = \{(x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.9)\}$. Its complement: $$\overline{A \cup B} = \{(x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.0), (x_4, 0.1)\}$$ Now compute $\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ pointwise: $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{\bar{A}}(x_i) &= (0.6, 0.3, 0.0, 0.8) \\ \mu_{\bar{B}}(x_i) &= (0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.1) \\ \mu_{\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}}(x_i) &= (\min(0.6, 0.4), \min(0.3, 0.7), \min(0.0, 0.2), \min(0.8, 0.1)) \\ &= (0.4, 0.3, 0.0, 0.1) \end{aligned} $$ $$\bar{A} \cap \bar{B} = \{(x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.0), (x_4, 0.1)\}$$ > ✅ De Morgan's Law is verified: $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linguistic Variable and Hedges > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Let the linguistic variable "Speed" be defined on $U = [0, 200]$ km/h with the fuzzy set "Fast" given by the triangular membership function: > > $$\mu_{Fast}(v) = \begin{cases} 0 & v \leq 60 \\ \dfrac{v - 60}{40} & 60 < v \leq 100 \\ 1 & v > 100 \end{cases}$$ > > Compute the membership values for: > 1. "Fast" at $v = 80$ km/h > 2. "Very Fast" at $v = 80$ km/h > 3. "More or Less Fast" at $v = 80$ km/h > 4. "Not Fast" at $v = 80$ km/h #### Step 1: Compute $\mu_{Fast}(80)$ Since $60 < 80 \leq 100$, we use the linear branch: $$\mu_{Fast}(80) = \frac{80 - 60}{40} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5$$ #### Step 2: Compute $\mu_{Very\,Fast}(80)$ Hedge "Very" applies the squaring (concentration) operation: $$\mu_{Very\,Fast}(80) = [\mu_{Fast}(80)]^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$$ > Interpretation: At 80 km/h, you are 50% "Fast" but only 25% "Very Fast" — the "Very" raises the bar. #### Step 3: Compute $\mu_{More\,or\,Less\,Fast}(80)$ Hedge "More or Less" applies the square-root (dilation) operation: $$\mu_{MOL\,Fast}(80) = \sqrt{\mu_{Fast}(80)} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.7071$$ > Interpretation: "More or Less Fast" is weaker, so the membership grade increases to ~0.71. #### Step 4: Compute $\mu_{Not\,Fast}(80)$ Hedge "Not" applies the complement: $$\mu_{Not\,Fast}(80) = 1 - \mu_{Fast}(80) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$ > Interpretation: At 80 km/h, the grade of "Not Fast" is also 0.5 — confirming that $x$ is a crossover point of the original FMF. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Verifying Failure of Excluded Middle > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Using the same $A$ from Example 2, compute $A \cup \bar{A}$ and $A \cap \bar{A}$ and verify that the laws of excluded middle and contradiction fail. #### Step 1: Recall $A$ and $\bar{A}$ $$A = \{ (x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.2) \}$$ $$\bar{A} = \{ (x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.0), (x_4, 0.8) \}$$ #### Step 2: Compute $A \cup \bar{A}$ (pointwise max) $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{A \cup \bar{A}}(x_1) &= \max(0.4, 0.6) = 0.6 \neq 1 \\ \mu_{A \cup \bar{A}}(x_2) &= \max(0.7, 0.3) = 0.7 \neq 1 \\ \mu_{A \cup \bar{A}}(x_3) &= \max(1.0, 0.0) = 1.0 = 1 \\ \mu_{A \cup \bar{A}}(x_4) &= \max(0.2, 0.8) = 0.8 \neq 1 \end{aligned} $$ $$A \cup \bar{A} = \{(x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.8)\} \neq X$$ > ❌ Law of Excluded Middle **fails** in fuzzy set theory. #### Step 3: Compute $A \cap \bar{A}$ (pointwise min) $$ \begin{aligned} \mu_{A \cap \bar{A}}(x_1) &= \min(0.4, 0.6) = 0.4 \neq 0 \\ \mu_{A \cap \bar{A}}(x_2) &= \min(0.7, 0.3) = 0.3 \neq 0 \\ \mu_{A \cap \bar{A}}(x_3) &= \min(1.0, 0.0) = 0.0 = 0 \\ \mu_{A \cap \bar{A}}(x_4) &= \min(0.2, 0.8) = 0.2 \neq 0 \end{aligned} $$ $$A \cap \bar{A} = \{(x_1, 0.4), (x_2, 0.3), (x_3, 0.0), (x_4, 0.2)\} \neq \emptyset$$ > ❌ Law of Contradiction **fails** in fuzzy set theory. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture of a Linguistic Variable (Block Diagram) The following block diagram illustrates the internal structure of a linguistic variable and its relationship to fuzzy sets via the semantic rule $M$. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Linguistic Variable LV = X"] node2["Name X, e.g. Temperature"] node3["Term Set T of X
Cold, Warm, Hot, Very Hot"] node4["Universe of Discourse U
e.g. 0 to 50 C"] node5["Syntactic Rule G
Generates composite terms"] node6["Semantic Rule M
Maps each term to a FMF"] node7["Primary Terms
e.g. Warm"] node8["Composite Terms
e.g. Very Warm, Slightly Hot"] node9["Fuzzy Set 1
Cold - FMF mu Cold"] node10["Fuzzy Set 2
Warm - FMF mu Warm"] node11["Fuzzy Set 3
Hot - FMF mu Hot"] node12["Derived FMF
Very Warm = mu Warm squared"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node1 --> node5 node1 --> node6 node3 --> node7 node3 --> node8 node5 -.generates.-> node8 node6 -.maps.-> node9 node6 -.maps.-> node10 node6 -.maps.-> node11 node8 -.uses hedges on.-> node12 ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Map of FMF Features The following diagram maps out the relationships between the five canonical features of a fuzzy membership function and how they partition the universe $X$. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Universe X"] nodeB["Core: mu of x equals 1"] nodeC["Boundary: 0 less than mu of x less than 1"] nodeD["Support: mu of x greater than 0"] nodeE["Outside Set: mu of x equals 0"] nodeF["Crossover: mu of x equals 0.5"] nodeG["Fuzziness Measure f of A"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeC nodeD --> nodeB nodeC --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeG ``` ### 4.3 Flow of Fuzzy Set Operations ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Fuzzy Set A on X"] nodeB["Fuzzy Set B on X"] nodeOp1["Union Operator
max"] nodeOp2["Intersection Operator
min"] nodeOp3["Complement Operator
1 minus mu"] nodeR1["A union B
S-norm result"] nodeR2["A intersection B
T-norm result"] nodeR3["Complement of A"] nodeA --> nodeOp1 nodeB --> nodeOp1 nodeOp1 --> nodeR1 nodeA --> nodeOp2 nodeB --> nodeOp2 nodeOp2 --> nodeR2 nodeA --> nodeOp3 nodeOp3 --> nodeR3 ``` ### 4.4 Hedges Transformation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Original FMF
mu A of x"] node2["Very Hedge
square"] node3["Extremely Hedge
cube"] node4["More or Less Hedge
square root"] node5["Not Hedge
1 minus mu"] node6["Sharpened FMF
mu Very A"] node7["Ultra Sharpened FMF
mu Extremely A"] node8["Dilated FMF
mu MOL A"] node9["Mirrored FMF
mu Not A"] node1 --> node2 --> node6 node1 --> node3 --> node7 node1 --> node4 --> node8 node1 --> node5 --> node9 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **Q1.** Define a **Fuzzy Membership Function**. With a neat diagram, illustrate the following features of an FMF: Core, Support, and Boundary. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A fuzzy membership function $\mu_A(x)$ maps every element $x$ in a universe $X$ to a grade of membership in the fuzzy set $A$, taking values in $[0, 1]$. Formally, $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$. - **Core:** $\{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\}$ — the region of full membership. - **Support:** $\{x \in X \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\}$ — the region of non-zero membership. - **Boundary:** $\{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\}$ — the region of partial membership. It always equals $\text{supp}(A) \setminus \text{core}(A)$. *Visual sketch:* (Triangular FMF diagram with three shaded regions) --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Q2.** State the **Zadeh formulation** of fuzzy set operations. Why do the **Laws of Excluded Middle and Contradiction** fail in fuzzy set theory? **Model Answer (3 marks):** For two fuzzy sets $A$ and $B$ in $X$: - Complement: $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ - Union: $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x)]$ - Intersection: $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x)]$ In crisp sets, $A \cup \bar{A} = X$ (Excluded Middle) and $A \cap \bar{A} = \emptyset$ (Contradiction). In fuzzy sets, this is **not** true in general because an element $x$ with $0 < \mu_A(x) < 1$ belongs *partially* to both $A$ and $\bar{A}$. For example, if $\mu_A(x) = 0.6$, then $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 0.4$, and: - $\max(0.6, 0.4) = 1.0$ only by coincidence; in general $\max(\mu_A, 1 - \mu_A) \neq 1$. - $\min(0.6, 0.4) = 0.4 \neq 0$. Thus both laws fail. --- ### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]** **Q(a)** Consider the universe $X = \{0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60\}$ representing the age (in years) of a population. Two fuzzy sets are defined as: $A$ = "Young" = $\{(0, 1.0), (10, 0.9), (20, 0.7), (30, 0.4), (40, 0.1), (50, 0.0), (60, 0.0)\}$ $B$ = "Old" = $\{(0, 0.0), (10, 0.1), (20, 0.2), (30, 0.4), (40, 0.6), (50, 0.9), (60, 1.0)\}$ Compute the following and verify De Morgan's Law: **(7 marks)** 1. $A \cup B$ 2. $A \cap B$ 3. $\bar{A}$ and $\bar{B}$ 4. $\overline{A \cup B}$ and $\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$ **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(1) $A \cup B$ using pointwise max:** | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\mu_{A \cup B} = \max$ | |-----|---------|---------|-----------------------| | 0 | 1.0 | 0.0 | **1.0** | | 10 | 0.9 | 0.1 | **0.9** | | 20 | 0.7 | 0.2 | **0.7** | | 30 | 0.4 | 0.4 | **0.4** | | 40 | 0.1 | 0.6 | **0.6** | | 50 | 0.0 | 0.9 | **0.9** | | 60 | 0.0 | 1.0 | **1.0** | $$A \cup B = \{(0, 1.0), (10, 0.9), (20, 0.7), (30, 0.4), (40, 0.6), (50, 0.9), (60, 1.0)\}$$ **(2) $A \cap B$ using pointwise min:** $$\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x)]$$ | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\mu_{A \cap B} = \min$ | |-----|---------|---------|-----------------------| | 0 | 1.0 | 0.0 | **0.0** | | 10 | 0.9 | 0.1 | **0.1** | | 20 | 0.7 | 0.2 | **0.2** | | 30 | 0.4 | 0.4 | **0.4** | | 40 | 0.1 | 0.6 | **0.1** | | 50 | 0.0 | 0.9 | **0.0** | | 60 | 0.0 | 1.0 | **0.0** | $$A \cap B = \{(0, 0.0), (10, 0.1), (20, 0.2), (30, 0.4), (40, 0.1), (50, 0.0), (60, 0.0)\}$$ **(3) Complements using $\mu_{\bar{A}} = 1 - \mu_A$:** $$\bar{A} = \{(0, 0.0), (10, 0.1), (20, 0.3), (30, 0.6), (40, 0.9), (50, 1.0), (60, 1.0)\}$$ $$\bar{B} = \{(0, 1.0), (10, 0.9), (20, 0.8), (30, 0.6), (40, 0.4), (50, 0.1), (60, 0.0)\}$$ **(4) Verification of De Morgan's Law $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$:** $$\overline{A \cup B} = \{(0, 0.0), (10, 0.1), (20, 0.3), (30, 0.6), (40, 0.4), (50, 0.1), (60, 0.0)\}$$ $$\bar{A} \cap \bar{B} = \{(0, 0.0), (10, 0.1), (20, 0.3), (30, 0.6), (40, 0.4), (50, 0.1), (60, 0.0)\}$$ ✅ **Verified.** De Morgan's Law holds in fuzzy set theory. --- **Q(b)** Define a **Linguistic Variable** with the formal quintuple representation. Using the linguistic variable "Speed" with universe $U = [0, 200]$ km/h and the term set $T = \{\text{Slow}, \text{Medium}, \text{Fast}\}$, define two membership functions (your choice of shape) and apply the hedges "Very Fast" and "More or Less Fast" to a specific speed value. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** A linguistic variable is a variable whose values are words or sentences in a natural language. Formally, it is a quintuple: $$LV = \langle X, T(X), U, G, M \rangle$$ For "Speed": - $X$ = Speed - $T(X)$ = {Slow, Medium, Fast} - $U = [0, 200]$ km/h - $G$ = syntactic rule generating "Very Fast" from "Fast" - $M$ = semantic rule assigning FMFs to each term **Membership Functions (3 marks):** Let us use triangular FMFs: - $\mu_{Slow}(v) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{60 - v}{30}, 1\right)\right)$ — peak at $v = 30$, drops to 0 at $v = 60$. - $\mu_{Medium}(v) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{v - 50}{20}, \dfrac{130 - v}{20}\right)\right)$ — peak at $v = 90$. - $\mu_{Fast}(v) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{v - 100}{40}, 1\right)\right)$ — rises from $v = 100$, peaks at $v = 140$. **Hedge Application (2 marks):** Evaluate at $v = 120$ km/h: $$\mu_{Fast}(120) = \frac{120 - 100}{40} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5$$ Apply hedges: - "Very Fast": $\mu_{Very\,Fast}(120) = [0.5]^2 = 0.25$ - "More or Less Fast": $\mu_{MOL\,Fast}(120) = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.7071$ Interpretation: At 120 km/h, the speed is moderately "Fast" (0.5) but only weakly "Very Fast" (0.25) and reasonably "More or Less Fast" (0.71). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Q(a)** For a fuzzy set $A$ defined on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ as $A = \{(1, 0.2), (2, 0.5), (3, 1.0), (4, 0.5), (5, 0.1)\}$, determine: **(7 marks)** 1. Core, Support, Boundary 2. Crossover points 3. Fuzziness measure 4. Whether $A$ is normalized **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(1) Core, Support, Boundary (3 marks):** - **Core** $= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 1\} = \{3\}$ - **Support** $= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) > 0\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ ⇒ $\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid = 5$ - **Boundary** $= \{x \mid 0 < \mu_A(x) < 1\} = \{1, 2, 4, 5\}$ ⇒ $\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid = 4$ **(2) Crossover Points (1 mark):** Crossover $= \{x \mid \mu_A(x) = 0.5\} = \{2, 4\}$ **(3) Fuzziness (1 mark):** $$f(A) = \frac{\mid \text{bnd}(A) \mid}{\mid \text{supp}(A) \mid} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$ **(4) Normalization (2 marks):** A fuzzy set is **normalized** if $\sup_{x \in X} \mu_A(x) = 1$. Here, the maximum membership value is 1.0 at $x = 3$. Hence $A$ is **normalized**, and the core is non-empty. --- **Q(b)** Explain the concept of **hedges** in linguistic variables. State the mathematical effect of the hedges "Very", "More or Less", "Not", and "Plus" on a fuzzy membership function $\mu_A(x)$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Introduction (1 mark):** Hedges are linguistic modifiers (e.g., "Very", "Slightly", "Extremely") that operate on fuzzy sets to produce new fuzzy sets. They allow precise tuning of a linguistic variable's term set without redefining the underlying FMF. **Mathematical Effects (6 marks — 1.5 marks each):** For a base fuzzy set $A$ with membership function $\mu_A(x)$: 1. **Very** (Concentration — sharpens the curve, raises the bar): $$\mu_{Very\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$$ 2. **More or Less** (Dilation — flattens the curve, lowers the bar): $$\mu_{MOL\,A}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)} = [\mu_A(x)]^{0.5}$$ 3. **Not** (Negation — mirror reflection about 0.5): $$\mu_{Not\,A}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$$ 4. **Plus** (Mild concentration — slight sharpening): $$\mu_{Plus\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^{1.25}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "Not" with "More or Less"**: Many students incorrectly write $\mu_{Not\,A} = \sqrt{\mu_A}$. The "Not" hedge is *always* the complement, $1 - \mu_A$, not the square root. *"More or Less"* is the square root, *"Not"* is the complement. > 2. **Forgetting to show the pointwise calculation**: When asked to find $A \cup B$ or $A \cap B$ in Part B, the examiner expects a *table* showing $\mu_A$, $\mu_B$, and the result side-by-side. Students who write only the final set without showing step-by-step evaluation lose up to 2 marks. > 3. **Applying the wrong hedge exponent**: The exponent for "Very" is **2**, for "Extremely" is **3**, and for "Slightly" is typically **1/3** (cube root). Mixing these up is a frequent error. > 4. **Mis-stating De Morgan's Law**: Some students write $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$ (incorrect). The correct form is $\overline{A \cup B} = \bar{A} \cap \bar{B}$, and the dual $\overline{A \cap B} = \bar{A} \cup \bar{B}$. The union/intersection symbols *swap* under complementation. > 5. **Ignoring the distinction between primary and composite terms**: A primary term (e.g., "Hot") is defined directly; a composite term (e.g., "Very Hot") is generated by the syntactic rule $G$ via hedges. Examiners often test this distinction in 3-mark questions. > 6. **Stating excluded middle "fails" without explanation**: Saying "$A \cup \bar{A} \neq X$" alone is incomplete. You *must* demonstrate with a numerical example (e.g., $\mu_A(x) = 0.6 \Rightarrow \max(0.6, 0.4) = 0.6 \neq 1$) to earn full marks. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - **FMF** $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ — never exceeds 1, never goes below 0. - **Core** = $\{\mu_A(x) = 1\}$; **Support** = $\{\mu_A(x) > 0\}$; **Boundary** = Support \ Core. - **Crossover** = $\mu_A(x) = 0.5$; **Fuzziness** $f(A) = \frac{|\text{bnd}|}{|\text{supp}|} \in [0, 1]$. - **Zadeh Operations**: Union → max, Intersection → min, Complement → $1 - \mu_A$. - **De Morgan's Laws** still hold in fuzzy sets. - **Excluded Middle** $A \cup \bar{A} = X$ and **Contradiction** $A \cap \bar{A} = \emptyset$ **fail** in fuzzy sets. - **Linguistic Variable** is a quintuple $\langle X, T(X), U, G, M \rangle$. - **Hedges**: - *Very* $\Rightarrow$ $[\mu]^2$ (concentration) - *Extremely* $\Rightarrow$ $[\mu]^3$ (extreme concentration) - *More or Less* $\Rightarrow$ $\sqrt{\mu}$ (dilation) - *Plus* $\Rightarrow$ $[\mu]^{1.25}$ (mild concentration) - *Not* $\Rightarrow$ $1 - \mu$ (negation) - **Primary terms** are atomic (e.g., "Hot"); **Composite terms** use hedges (e.g., "Very Hot"). - **Hedge of "Very"** *raises* the bar; **"More or Less"** *lowers* the bar. - **Fuzziness = 0** ⇒ crisp set; **Fuzziness = 1** ⇒ maximally fuzzy. - A fuzzy set is **normalized** if its FMF attains the value 1 at least once in $X$. - **Practical use**: FLCs, expert systems, decision-support systems, automotive cruise control, image processing, medical diagnosis. > **End of Module 2 — Topic Notes**

topics.title: Linguistic Hedges, Fuzzy Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linguistic Hedges, Fuzzy Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Linguistic Hedges

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Linguistic Hedge** is a linguistic qualifier or modifier (an adverb, adjective, or phrase) that can be applied to a linguistic variable to transform or modify the meaning of a fuzzy set without altering its underlying universe of discourse. Hedges operate mathematically on the **membership function** of an existing fuzzy set to either sharpen, dilate, or shift the meaning, producing a new fuzzy set that is semantically related to the original.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, linguistic hedges are formally classified as **hedge operators** on fuzzy sets, originally introduced by Lofti A. Zadeh in 1972 as a part of his foundational paper *"A Fuzzy-Set-Theoretic Interpretation of Linguistic Hedges."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECST417):** Hedges are treated as the bridge between pure numerical fuzzy logic and **natural language processing (NLP)** in fuzzy systems. They allow the system to interpret human-style adjectives like *"very tall,"* *"slightly hot,"* or *"extremely fast"* mathematically.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you tell a friend, *"The soup is **hot**."* Now consider these variations:

- *"The soup is **very** hot."* — this narrows the meaning; the soup must be even hotter to qualify.
- *"The soup is **more or less** hot."* — this broadens the meaning; even lukewarm soup might fit.
- *"The soup is **extremely** hot."* — this intensifies the meaning dramatically.

Mathematically, each of these adverbs (*very*, *more or less*, *extremely*) **squeezes, stretches, or amplifies** the membership function of the fuzzy set *"hot"*. They are linguistic "lenses" through which we interpret a fuzzy proposition.

### Common Hedges and Their Operations

| Hedge | Linguistic Intent | Mathematical Operation |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Very** | Concentration / Sharpening | $\mu_{VERY\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$ |
| **More or Less** | Dilation / Broadening | $\mu_{MOL\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^{1/2}$ |
| **Slightly** | Mild Reduction | $\mu_{SL\,A}(x) = \int_S [\mu_A(x)]^2 / S$ where threshold applies |
| **Extremely** | Strong Concentration | $\mu_{EXT\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^3$ |
| **Indeed** | Contrast Intensifier | $\mu_{IN\,A}(x) = 2[\mu_A(x)]^2$ if $\mu \le 0.5$, else $1 - 2[1-\mu_A(x)]^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> A linguistic hedge $h$ is a mapping $h: \mathcal{F}(X) \to \mathcal{F}(X)$ that transforms a fuzzy set $A$ into a new fuzzy set $A' = h(A)$, preserving the universe $X$ but altering membership values.

---

## 1.2 Fuzzy Relations

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuzzy Relation** is a generalization of a crisp (binary) relation in which the strength of the relationship between elements of two (or more) sets is expressed as a real-valued **membership grade in the interval [0, 1]**, rather than as a strict binary (0 or 1) association.

Formally, a binary fuzzy relation $R$ from a universe $X$ to a universe $Y$ is a fuzzy set defined on the Cartesian product space $X \times Y$:

$$R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \vert (x, y) \in X \times Y\}$$

where $\mu_R : X \times Y \to [0, 1]$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the crisp relation *"x is taller than y."* In a binary world, for two specific people, either $x$ is taller (1) or not (0). But in reality, the statement *"Ram is somewhat taller than Shyam"* carries a *degree* — Ram is *kind of* taller. A fuzzy relation captures this nuance by assigning values like $\mu_R(\text{Ram, Shyam}) = 0.7$, meaning the relation holds with 70% strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A fuzzy relation is essentially a **two-dimensional fuzzy set** where each pair $(x, y)$ has a membership grade. This allows modeling of approximate, partial, or graded relationships — central to fuzzy databases, fuzzy control, and approximate reasoning.

### Types of Fuzzy Relations

1. **Binary Fuzzy Relation** — between two sets $X$ and $Y$
2. **n-ary Fuzzy Relation** — among $n$ sets $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$
3. **Fuzzy Relation on a Single Set** — $R$ on $X \times X$ (used for similarity, equivalence)

### Operations on Fuzzy Relations

Let $R$ and $S$ be two fuzzy relations on the same space $X \times Y$:

- **Union:** $\mu_{R \cup S}(x, y) = \max[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$
- **Intersection:** $\mu_{R \cap S}(x, y) = \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$
- **Complement:** $\mu_{\bar{R}}(x, y) = 1 - \mu_R(x, y)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> A fuzzy relation $R$ is **reflexive** if $\mu_R(x, x) = 1$ for all $x \in X$; **symmetric** if $\mu_R(x, y) = \mu_R(y, x)$; and **transitive** if $\mu_R(x, z) \ge \max_y \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z)]$ (max-min transitivity).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Linguistic Hedges — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### The Motivation Behind Hedges

Zadeh observed that natural language is **richer** than classical logic. Statements like *"this room is **somewhat** warm"* cannot be represented by a single fuzzy set; the qualifier *"somewhat"* shifts the membership distribution. Hedges formalize this shift mathematically.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Common Hedges

**Hedge 1: VERY (Concentration Operator)**

- **Why:** *"Very tall"* means much taller than *"tall"*. This sharpens the boundary.
- **How:** We square the membership function. High memberships remain high; low memberships become even lower.
- **Effect:** The set becomes *"narrower"* and more selective.

**Hedge 2: MORE OR LESS (Dilation Operator)**

- **Why:** *"More or less tall"* is a softer claim, accepting even moderate heights.
- **How:** We take the square root of the membership function. Low values get boosted.
- **Effect:** The set becomes *"wider"* and more inclusive.

**Hedge 3: EXTREMELY**

- **Why:** *"Extremely tall"* is even stronger than *"very tall."*
- **How:** Cube the membership function.
- **Effect:** Maximum concentration; only the very tallest survive with high membership.

**Hedge 4: INDEED (Contrast Intensifier)**

- **Why:** *"Indeed tall"* sharpens mid-range values.
- **How:** A piecewise function that pulls mid-range memberships towards the extremes (0 or 1).
- **Effect:** Reduces ambiguity in the 0.5 region.

### Linguistic Approximation

Hedges are used to convert a numerical fuzzy output back into a linguistic label. If a fuzzy controller computes a crisp output of $0.83$, we apply **inverse hedges** to identify the linguistic term (e.g., *"very hot"* or *"extremely hot"*).

---

## 2.2 Fuzzy Relations — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Crisp vs. Fuzzy Cartesian Product

For crisp sets $A \subseteq X$ and $B \subseteq Y$, the Cartesian product is:

$$A \times B = \{(x, y) \vert x \in A, y \in B\}$$

For **fuzzy sets** $\tilde{A}$ on $X$ and $\tilde{B}$ on $Y$, the fuzzy Cartesian product uses the min (or product) t-norm:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \times \tilde{B}}(x, y) = \min[\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(y)]$$

### Composition of Fuzzy Relations

Given two fuzzy relations $R_1$ on $X \times Y$ and $R_2$ on $Y \times Z$, the **composition** $R_1 \circ R_2$ is a fuzzy relation on $X \times Z$.

**Max-Min Composition:**

$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

**Max-Product Composition:**

$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} [\mu_{R_1}(x, y) \cdot \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

### Projection

The **projection** of a fuzzy relation $R$ on $X \times Y$ onto $X$ collapses the $Y$-dimension by taking the supremum (max):

$$\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x) = \sup_{y \in Y} \mu_R(x, y)$$

### Cylindrical Extension

The **cylindrical extension** of a fuzzy set $A$ on $X$ into $X \times Y$ is:

$$\mu_{cyl(A)}(x, y) = \mu_A(x) \quad \text{for all } y \in Y$$

This is the inverse operation of projection.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Quick-Reference Table for KTU ESE — Module 2**

| Concept | Formula | Condition / Range |
|---------|---------|-------------------|
| **Very A** | $\mu_{V}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$ | Concentration; $\mu \in [0,1]$ |
| **More or Less A** | $\mu_{MOL}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)}$ | Dilation; $\mu \in [0,1]$ |
| **Extremely A** | $\mu_{E}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^3$ | Strong concentration |
| **Indeed A** | $\mu_{I}(x) = 2[\mu(x)]^2$ if $\mu \le 0.5$, else $1 - 2[1-\mu(x)]^2$ | Contrast intensifier |
| **Slightly A** | $\mu_{S}(x) = \int [\mu_A(x)]^2 / S$ | Threshold-based reduction |
| **Fuzzy Cartesian Product** | $\mu_{A \times B}(x, y) = \min[\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y)]$ | Min t-norm |
| **Fuzzy Relation (general)** | $R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \vert (x, y) \in X \times Y\}$ | $\mu_R : X \times Y \to [0,1]$ |
| **Union of Relations** | $\mu_{R \cup S}(x, y) = \max[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$ | s-norm |
| **Intersection of Relations** | $\mu_{R \cap S}(x, y) = \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_S(x, y)]$ | t-norm |
| **Complement** | $\mu_{\bar{R}}(x, y) = 1 - \mu_R(x, y)$ | Standard negation |
| **Max-Min Composition** | $\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_y \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$ | Most commonly tested |
| **Max-Product Composition** | $\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_y [\mu_{R_1}(x, y) \cdot \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$ | Alternative composition |
| **Projection onto X** | $\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x) = \sup_y \mu_R(x, y)$ | Sup = Max for finite sets |
| **Cylindrical Extension** | $\mu_{cyl(A)}(x, y) = \mu_A(x)$ | Independent of $y$ |
| **Reflexivity** | $\mu_R(x, x) = 1$ for all $x$ | Property of $R$ on $X \times X$ |
| **Symmetry** | $\mu_R(x, y) = \mu_R(y, x)$ | Property of $R$ on $X \times X$ |
| **Max-Min Transitivity** | $\mu_R(x, z) \ge \max_y \min[\mu_R(x, y), \mu_R(y, z)]$ | Fuzzy equivalence |

### Engineering Utility

- **Hedges:** Used in **fuzzy expert systems**, **automotive fuzzy controllers** (e.g., *"very fast acceleration"*), and **sentiment analysis** (e.g., *"slightly positive"* vs. *"extremely positive"*).
- **Fuzzy Relations:** Form the backbone of **Fuzzy Associative Memories (FAM)**, **fuzzy database queries**, **medical diagnosis systems**, and **fuzzy inference engines** (Mamdani/TSK controllers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Application of Linguistic Hedges

**Problem:** Let the fuzzy set $A$ represent *"tall"* on the universe $X = \{150, 160, 170, 180, 190\}$ (in cm):

$$A = \{(150, 0.1), (160, 0.3), (170, 0.6), (180, 0.85), (190, 1.0)\}$$

Compute the hedge-modified sets:
- (i) **Very A**
- (ii) **More or Less A**
- (iii) **Extremely A**
- (iv) **Indeed A**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute VERY A using $\mu_{V}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$**

We square each membership value:

$$\mu_V(150) = (0.1)^2 = 0.01$$

$$\mu_V(160) = (0.3)^2 = 0.09$$

$$\mu_V(170) = (0.6)^2 = 0.36$$

$$\mu_V(180) = (0.85)^2 = 0.7225$$

$$\mu_V(190) = (1.0)^2 = 1.00$$

So, $\text{VERY A} = \{(150, 0.01), (160, 0.09), (170, 0.36), (180, 0.7225), (190, 1.0)\}$.

> **Interpretation:** Values with originally high membership (190 cm) remain at 1.0, but values with lower membership (150 cm) drop from 0.1 to 0.01. The set is now "more selective."

**Step 2: Compute MORE OR LESS A using $\mu_{MOL}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)}$**

We take the square root of each membership:

$$\mu_{MOL}(150) = \sqrt{0.1} \approx 0.3162$$

$$\mu_{MOL}(160) = \sqrt{0.3} \approx 0.5477$$

$$\mu_{MOL}(170) = \sqrt{0.6} \approx 0.7746$$

$$\mu_{MOL}(180) = \sqrt{0.85} \approx 0.9220$$

$$\mu_{MOL}(190) = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.00$$

So, $\text{MOL A} = \{(150, 0.3162), (160, 0.5477), (170, 0.7746), (180, 0.9220), (190, 1.0)\}$.

> **Interpretation:** Even a 150 cm person now has a 31.6% membership in "more or less tall" — the set is broadened.

**Step 3: Compute EXTREMELY A using $\mu_{E}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^3$**

We cube each membership:

$$\mu_E(150) = (0.1)^3 = 0.001$$

$$\mu_E(160) = (0.3)^3 = 0.027$$

$$\mu_E(170) = (0.6)^3 = 0.216$$

$$\mu_E(180) = (0.85)^3 = 0.6141$$

$$\mu_E(190) = (1.0)^3 = 1.00$$

So, $\text{EXTREMELY A} = \{(150, 0.001), (160, 0.027), (170, 0.216), (180, 0.6141), (190, 1.0)\}$.

> **Interpretation:** Cubing has a stronger effect than squaring. Only 190 cm survives with high membership.

**Step 4: Compute INDEED A using the piecewise formula**

We apply:
$$\mu_I(x) = \begin{cases} 2[\mu_A(x)]^2 & \text{if } \mu_A(x) \le 0.5 \\ 1 - 2[1 - \mu_A(x)]^2 & \text{if } \mu_A(x) > 0.5 \end{cases}$$

For $x = 150$, $\mu_A = 0.1 \le 0.5$:

$$\mu_I(150) = 2 \times (0.1)^2 = 2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$$

For $x = 160$, $\mu_A = 0.3 \le 0.5$:

$$\mu_I(160) = 2 \times (0.3)^2 = 2 \times 0.09 = 0.18$$

For $x = 170$, $\mu_A = 0.6 > 0.5$:

$$\mu_I(170) = 1 - 2[1 - 0.6]^2 = 1 - 2(0.4)^2 = 1 - 2(0.16) = 1 - 0.32 = 0.68$$

For $x = 180$, $\mu_A = 0.85 > 0.5$:

$$\mu_I(180) = 1 - 2[1 - 0.85]^2 = 1 - 2(0.15)^2 = 1 - 2(0.0225) = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955$$

For $x = 190$, $\mu_A = 1.0 > 0.5$:

$$\mu_I(190) = 1 - 2[1 - 1.0]^2 = 1 - 0 = 1.00$$

So, $\text{INDEED A} = \{(150, 0.02), (160, 0.18), (170, 0.68), (180, 0.955), (190, 1.0)\}$.

### Comparative Summary Table

| Height (cm) | A | Very A | MOL A | Extremely A | Indeed A |
|-------------|------|---------|--------|-------------|----------|
| 150 | 0.10 | 0.0100 | 0.3162 | 0.0010 | 0.0200 |
| 160 | 0.30 | 0.0900 | 0.5477 | 0.0270 | 0.1800 |
| 170 | 0.60 | 0.3600 | 0.7746 | 0.2160 | 0.6800 |
| 180 | 0.85 | 0.7225 | 0.9220 | 0.6141 | 0.9550 |
| 190 | 1.00 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 |

> **Insight:** Notice how "Very" and "Extremely" suppress mid-range values, "More or Less" elevates them, and "Indeed" pushes everything closer to the extremes (0 or 1).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Max-Min Composition of Fuzzy Relations

**Problem:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2, y_3\}$, and $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$.

Define the fuzzy relations:

$$R_1(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.9 & 0.3 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rows: } x_1, x_2\text{; cols: } y_1, y_2, y_3\text{)}$$

$$R_2(y, z) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rows: } y_1, y_2, y_3\text{; cols: } z_1, z_2\text{)}$$

Compute the max-min composition $R = R_1 \circ R_2$ on $X \times Z$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Set up the composition formula**

$$\mu_{R}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

The result $R$ will be a $2 \times 2$ matrix (rows: $x_1, x_2$; cols: $z_1, z_2$).

**Step 2: Compute $R(x_1, z_1)$**

We compute $\min[\mu_{R_1}(x_1, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z_1)]$ for each $y$, then take the max.

For $y = y_1$:

$$\min[0.4, 0.5] = 0.4$$

For $y = y_2$:

$$\min[0.7, 0.8] = 0.7$$

For $y = y_3$:

$$\min[0.2, 0.1] = 0.1$$

Take the maximum:

$$\mu_R(x_1, z_1) = \max[0.4, 0.7, 0.1] = 0.7$$

**Step 3: Compute $R(x_1, z_2)$**

For $y = y_1$:

$$\min[0.4, 0.6] = 0.4$$

For $y = y_2$:

$$\min[0.7, 0.2] = 0.2$$

For $y = y_3$:

$$\min[0.2, 0.9] = 0.2$$

Take the maximum:

$$\mu_R(x_1, z_2) = \max[0.4, 0.2, 0.2] = 0.4$$

**Step 4: Compute $R(x_2, z_1)$**

For $y = y_1$:

$$\min[0.9, 0.5] = 0.5$$

For $y = y_2$:

$$\min[0.3, 0.8] = 0.3$$

For $y = y_3$:

$$\min[0.8, 0.1] = 0.1$$

Take the maximum:

$$\mu_R(x_2, z_1) = \max[0.5, 0.3, 0.1] = 0.5$$

**Step 5: Compute $R(x_2, z_2)$**

For $y = y_1$:

$$\min[0.9, 0.6] = 0.6$$

For $y = y_2$:

$$\min[0.3, 0.2] = 0.2$$

For $y = y_3$:

$$\min[0.8, 0.9] = 0.8$$

Take the maximum:

$$\mu_R(x_2, z_2) = \max[0.6, 0.2, 0.8] = 0.8$$

**Step 6: Assemble the resulting relation**

$$R = R_1 \circ R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

where rows correspond to $\{x_1, x_2\}$ and columns to $\{z_1, z_2\}$.

### Verification of Method

We can verify the same computation using the matrix "min-multiply" interpretation:

> For each entry $(i, j)$ of the result: take the **elementwise min** of row $i$ of $R_1$ with column $j$ of $R_2$, then take the **max** of the resulting vector.

For example, $R(x_2, z_2)$: row 2 of $R_1$ is $(0.9, 0.3, 0.8)$, column 2 of $R_2$ is $(0.6, 0.2, 0.9)^T$.

Elementwise min: $(\min[0.9, 0.6], \min[0.3, 0.2], \min[0.8, 0.9]) = (0.6, 0.2, 0.8)$.

Max: $\max[0.6, 0.2, 0.8] = 0.8$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Projection and Cylindrical Extension

**Problem:** Let $R$ be a fuzzy relation on $X \times Y$ where $X = \{x_1, x_2\}$ and $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$

- (a) Find the projection of $R$ onto $X$.
- (b) Find the projection of $R$ onto $Y$.
- (c) Compute the cylindrical extension of $A = \{(x_1, 0.7), (x_2, 0.5)\}$ into $X \times Y$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Projection onto $X$**

The projection $R \downarrow X$ is a fuzzy set on $X$ where $\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x) = \max_y \mu_R(x, y)$.

For $x_1$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x_1) = \max[0.6, 0.3] = 0.6$$

For $x_2$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x_2) = \max[0.4, 0.9] = 0.9$$

So, $R \downarrow X = \{(x_1, 0.6), (x_2, 0.9)\}$.

**Part (b): Projection onto $Y$**

For $y_1$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow Y}(y_1) = \max[0.6, 0.4] = 0.6$$

For $y_2$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow Y}(y_2) = \max[0.3, 0.9] = 0.9$$

So, $R \downarrow Y = \{(y_1, 0.6), (y_2, 0.9)\}$.

**Part (c): Cylindrical Extension**

For the fuzzy set $A$ on $X$, the cylindrical extension $cyl(A)$ on $X \times Y$ has membership independent of $y$:

$$\mu_{cyl(A)}(x_1, y_1) = \mu_A(x_1) = 0.7$$

$$\mu_{cyl(A)}(x_1, y_2) = \mu_A(x_1) = 0.7$$

$$\mu_{cyl(A)}(x_2, y_1) = \mu_A(x_2) = 0.5$$

$$\mu_{cyl(A)}(x_2, y_2) = \mu_A(x_2) = 0.5$$

So, the cylindrical extension matrix is:

$$cyl(A) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.7 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Insight:** Notice that the columns of $cyl(A)$ are identical — this is the defining visual signature of a cylindrical extension.

```mermaid
    B --> C2["More or Less A: Dilation n=1/2"]
    B --> C3["Extremely A: Concentration n=3"]
    B --> C4["Indeed A: Contrast Intensifier"]
    C1 --> D1["Sharper boundaries; n-squared"]
    C2 --> D2["Wider boundaries; n-half-power"]
    C3 --> D3["Very sharp; n-cubed"]

    D3 --> E

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Linguistic Hedges. Give two examples with their mathematical representations.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Linguistic Hedge** is a linguistic modifier (such as an adverb or adjective) that operates on a fuzzy set to produce a new fuzzy set with altered membership values, while preserving the universe of discourse. Hedges allow natural language qualifiers to be expressed mathematically.

**Two examples:**

1. **VERY (Concentration):** $\mu_{VERY\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$ — sharpens the fuzzy set.
2. **MORE OR LESS (Dilation):** $\mu_{MOL\,A}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)}$ — broadens the fuzzy set.

> **Marking scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + Example 1 with formula (1 mark) + Example 2 with formula (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: What is a binary fuzzy relation? Define max-min composition.

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **binary fuzzy relation** $R$ from set $X$ to set $Y$ is a fuzzy set defined on the Cartesian product $X \times Y$, expressed as:

$$R = \{(x, y), \mu_R(x, y) \vert x \in X, y \in Y\}$$

where $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$ represents the strength of the relationship between $x$ and $y$.

**Max-min composition** of two fuzzy relations $R_1$ on $X \times Y$ and $R_2$ on $Y \times Z$ is defined as:

$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

> **Marking scheme:** Fuzzy relation definition (1.5 marks) + Max-min composition definition (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define linguistic hedges in fuzzy set theory. Explain the operations for **VERY**, **MORE OR LESS**, and **EXTREMELY** hedges with their mathematical formulations and visual interpretation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a fuzzy set $A$ on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ defined as $A = \{(1, 0.2), (2, 0.5), (3, 0.8), (4, 0.9), (5, 1.0)\}$. Compute the fuzzy sets for **VERY A**, **MORE OR LESS A**, and **EXTREMELY A**. Plot or tabulate all results and comment on how each hedge modifies the original set. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

A **linguistic hedge** is a word or phrase that modifies the meaning of a fuzzy set, transforming its membership function to represent a related but distinct concept. Introduced by **Zadeh (1972)**, hedges serve as the link between numerical fuzzy logic and natural language.

**Operation 1: VERY (Concentration)**
$$\mu_{VERY\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^2$$
This is the *concentration* operator. It squashes low memberships towards 0, sharpening the boundary. The set becomes more selective — only strongly satisfying elements retain high membership.

**Operation 2: MORE OR LESS (Dilation)**
$$\mu_{MOL\,A}(x) = \sqrt{\mu_A(x)} = [\mu_A(x)]^{1/2}$$
This is the *dilation* operator. It elevates low memberships, making the set more inclusive. More elements satisfy the modified predicate.

**Operation 3: EXTREMELY**
$$\mu_{EXT\,A}(x) = [\mu_A(x)]^3$$
This is a higher-order concentration, more aggressive than "Very." Only elements with very high original membership survive with significant grade.

**Visual interpretation:** In all three, the universe $X$ is preserved; only the membership values change. Concentration narrows the "core" of the fuzzy set, while dilation broadens it.

> **(7 marks)**: Definition 2 marks + VERY 1.5 marks + MORE OR LESS 1.5 marks + EXTREMELY 1 mark + visual interpretation 1 mark.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given $A = \{(1, 0.2), (2, 0.5), (3, 0.8), (4, 0.9), (5, 1.0)\}$.

**VERY A:** Square each membership.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu_{VERY\,A}(1) &= (0.2)^2 = 0.04 \\ \mu_{VERY\,A}(2) &= (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \\ \mu_{VERY\,A}(3) &= (0.8)^2 = 0.64 \\ \mu_{VERY\,A}(4) &= (0.9)^2 = 0.81 \\ \mu_{VERY\,A}(5) &= (1.0)^2 = 1.00 \end{aligned}$$

So, $\text{VERY A} = \{(1, 0.04), (2, 0.25), (3, 0.64), (4, 0.81), (5, 1.00)\}$.

**MORE OR LESS A:** Take the square root.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu_{MOL\,A}(1) &= \sqrt{0.2} \approx 0.4472 \\ \mu_{MOL\,A}(2) &= \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.7071 \\ \mu_{MOL\,A}(3) &= \sqrt{0.8} \approx 0.8944 \\ \mu_{MOL\,A}(4) &= \sqrt{0.9} \approx 0.9487 \\ \mu_{MOL\,A}(5) &= \sqrt{1.0} = 1.00 \end{aligned}$$

So, $\text{MOL A} = \{(1, 0.4472), (2, 0.7071), (3, 0.8944), (4, 0.9487), (5, 1.00)\}$.

**EXTREMELY A:** Cube each membership.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu_{EXT\,A}(1) &= (0.2)^3 = 0.008 \\ \mu_{EXT\,A}(2) &= (0.5)^3 = 0.125 \\ \mu_{EXT\,A}(3) &= (0.8)^3 = 0.512 \\ \mu_{EXT\,A}(4) &= (0.9)^3 = 0.729 \\ \mu_{EXT\,A}(5) &= (1.0)^3 = 1.00 \end{aligned}$$

So, $\text{EXT A} = \{(1, 0.008), (2, 0.125), (3, 0.512), (4, 0.729), (5, 1.00)\}$.

**Tabulated Results:**

| x | A | VERY A | MOL A | EXTREME A |
|---|------|---------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | 0.20 | 0.040 | 0.4472 | 0.008 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 0.250 | 0.7071 | 0.125 |
| 3 | 0.80 | 0.640 | 0.8944 | 0.512 |
| 4 | 0.90 | 0.810 | 0.9487 | 0.729 |
| 5 | 1.00 | 1.000 | 1.0000 | 1.000 |

**Comments:**
- "VERY" reduces the membership of element 1 from 0.20 to 0.04 (an 80% reduction), but element 5 remains at 1.0.
- "MORE OR LESS" raises the membership of element 1 from 0.20 to 0.4472, accepting borderline cases.
- "EXTREMELY" is the most aggressive — element 1's membership drops to 0.008, almost zero.

> **(7 marks)**: 2 marks per hedge computation (1 mark for formula application, 1 mark for correct values) + 1 mark for table/comments.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define a **fuzzy relation**. Explain max-min composition and max-product composition with their mathematical formulations. Differentiate between the two. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the fuzzy relations:

$$R_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.8 \\ 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.9 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{on } X \times Y, \quad R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.7 \\ 0.5 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{on } Y \times Z$$

where $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$, $Y = \{y_1, y_2\}$, $Z = \{z_1, z_2\}$. Compute the max-min composition $R = R_1 \circ R_2$. Also compute the projection of $R$ onto $Z$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

A **fuzzy relation** $R$ on $X \times Y$ is a mapping $R: X \times Y \to [0, 1]$ assigning to each pair $(x, y)$ a membership grade $\mu_R(x, y)$ indicating the strength of the relationship.

**Max-Min Composition** of $R_1: X \times Y \to [0, 1]$ and $R_2: Y \times Z \to [0, 1]$ produces $R: X \times Z \to [0, 1]$:

$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

**Max-Product Composition:**

$$\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_{y \in Y} [\mu_{R_1}(x, y) \cdot \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$$

**Difference:** Max-min uses the **minimum (t-norm)** operator to combine values, while max-product uses **multiplication**. Max-min is more conservative (saturates at the smaller value), while max-product preserves the joint strength as a product.

> **(7 marks)**: Definition 1.5 marks + Max-min formula 1.5 marks + Max-product formula 1.5 marks + Differentiation 2.5 marks.

**Part (b) Solution:**

The composition $R = R_1 \circ R_2$ results in a $3 \times 2$ matrix (rows: $x_1, x_2, x_3$; cols: $z_1, z_2$).

**Compute $R(x_1, z_1)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.3, 0.4] = 0.3$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.8, 0.5] = 0.5$
Maximum: $\max[0.3, 0.5] = 0.5$

**Compute $R(x_1, z_2)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.3, 0.7] = 0.3$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.8, 0.9] = 0.8$
Maximum: $\max[0.3, 0.8] = 0.8$

**Compute $R(x_2, z_1)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.6, 0.4] = 0.4$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.2, 0.5] = 0.2$
Maximum: $\max[0.4, 0.2] = 0.4$

**Compute $R(x_2, z_2)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.6, 0.7] = 0.6$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.2, 0.9] = 0.2$
Maximum: $\max[0.6, 0.2] = 0.6$

**Compute $R(x_3, z_1)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.9, 0.4] = 0.4$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.5, 0.5] = 0.5$
Maximum: $\max[0.4, 0.5] = 0.5$

**Compute $R(x_3, z_2)$:**

For $y = y_1$: $\min[0.9, 0.7] = 0.7$
For $y = y_2$: $\min[0.5, 0.9] = 0.5$
Maximum: $\max[0.7, 0.5] = 0.7$

**Resulting Composition Matrix:**

$$R = R_1 \circ R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.8 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Projection onto $Z$:**

$R \downarrow Z$ is a fuzzy set on $Z$ where $\mu_{R \downarrow Z}(z) = \max_x \mu_R(x, z)$.

For $z_1$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow Z}(z_1) = \max[0.5, 0.4, 0.5] = 0.5$$

For $z_2$:

$$\mu_{R \downarrow Z}(z_2) = \max[0.8, 0.6, 0.7] = 0.8$$

So, $R \downarrow Z = \{(z_1, 0.5), (z_2, 0.8)\}$.

> **(7 marks)**: 1 mark per cell (6 cells = 6 marks) + 1 mark for projection computation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** In max-min composition, students often use **multiplication** instead of **min**, or use **max** alone without combining. Always remember: combine with min, then aggregate with max.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** For hedges, students sometimes write the formula for "MORE OR LESS" as the cube or the square — verify: *Very* = square, *More or Less* = square root, *Extremely* = cube.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** For the *Indeed* hedge, students forget the piecewise condition. If $\mu \le 0.5$, use $2\mu^2$; if $\mu > 0.5$, use $1 - 2(1-\mu)^2$. **Forgetting the threshold loses 1 mark easily.**
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In projection, students sometimes sum or average — projection is **max (sup)**, not sum.
> 5. **Mark loss zones:** Always show the **formula before substitution**, and **tabulate** intermediate values for partial marking credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Linguistic Hedges & Fuzzy Relations**

### Linguistic Hedges — Key Takeaways

- **Linguistic Hedges** are qualifiers that modify fuzzy sets mathematically while preserving the universe.
- **VERY** = concentration: $\mu^2$ — sharpens the set.
- **MORE OR LESS** = dilation: $\sqrt{\mu}$ — broadens the set.
- **EXTREMELY** = strong concentration: $\mu^3$ — very sharp.
- **INDEED** = contrast intensifier — piecewise function: $2\mu^2$ for $\mu \le 0.5$, $1 - 2(1-\mu)^2$ for $\mu > 0.5$.
- **SLIGHTLY** uses a threshold-based reduction, similar to a softened concentration.
- Hedges are essential in **fuzzy rule-based systems** and **linguistic approximation**.

### Fuzzy Relations — Key Takeaways

- A **fuzzy relation** $R$ on $X \times Y$ assigns grades $\mu_R(x, y) \in [0, 1]$ to ordered pairs.
- **Operations:** Union (max), Intersection (min), Complement (1 − value).
- **Max-Min Composition:** $\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_y \min[\mu_{R_1}(x, y), \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$.
- **Max-Product Composition:** $\mu_{R_1 \circ R_2}(x, z) = \max_y [\mu_{R_1}(x, y) \cdot \mu_{R_2}(y, z)]$.
- **Projection onto $X$:** $\mu_{R \downarrow X}(x) = \sup_y \mu_R(x, y)$.
- **Cylindrical Extension:** $\mu_{cyl(A)}(x, y) = \mu_A(x)$ — independent of $y$; columns are constant.
- **Properties:** Reflexivity, Symmetry, Max-Min Transitivity — all of these combine to form **fuzzy equivalence relations**.

### Memory Hooks (Mnemonics)

- **V-M-E** for hedges: **V**ery (squared), **M**ore or Less (half-power), **E**xtremely (cubed) — the exponent **2, 0.5, 3** is the "concentration ladder."
- **MIN then MAX** for max-min composition — like a pipeline: filter (min) first, then select (max).
- **PROJECT = MAX over a dimension, CYLINDRICAL = REPLICATE across a dimension** — they are inverse operations.

### Common Application Domains

- **Fuzzy Controllers** (washing machines, air conditioners, cameras)
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (symptom-disease relations)
- **Fuzzy Databases** (graded queries)
- **Linguistic Variables in Expert Systems**
- **Fuzzy Associative Memories (FAMs)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fuzzy If-Then Rule** (also called a **Fuzzy Implication Rule**, **Fuzzy Conditional Rule**, or **Linguistic Rule**) is a conditional statement of the form:

> **IF `<antecedent>` THEN `<consequent>`**

In canonical fuzzy set notation:

$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

where:
- $x$ is the **input variable** (universe of discourse $X$)
- $y$ is the **output variable** (universe of discourse $Y$)
- $A$ is a fuzzy set defined on $X$ with membership function $\mu_A(x)$
- $B$ is a fuzzy set defined on $Y$ with membership function $\mu_B(y)$

The rule represents a fuzzy relation $R$ on the product space $X \times Y$, expressed as $R: A \rightarrow B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** Fuzzy If-Then Rules form the knowledge base of a **Fuzzy Inference System (FIS)**. They translate expert linguistic knowledge into a computable mathematical framework, bridging human reasoning and machine execution.

## 1.2 Types of Fuzzy If-Then Rules

### (a) Simple (Atomic) Form
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

### (b) Compound (Conjunctive) Form — using AND/OR
$$\text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_1) \, \text{AND}\, (x_2 \text{ is } A_2) \, \text{THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

$$\text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_1) \, \text{OR}\, (x_2 \text{ is } A_2) \, \text{THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

### (c) Disjunctive Form — Multiple Rules
$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A_1 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B_1$$
$$\text{ELSE IF } x \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B_2$$
$$\text{ELSE IF } x \text{ is } A_3 \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B_3$$

### (d) Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO) Form
$$\text{IF } (x_1 \text{ is } A_{11}) \, \text{AND}\, (x_2 \text{ is } A_{21}) \, \text{THEN } (y_1 \text{ is } B_{11}), (y_2 \text{ is } B_{21})$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Air-Conditioner Expert System:**
>
> Imagine you are a 70-year-old AC technician. A young apprentice asks: *"When should I set the compressor to HIGH speed?"* You answer with rules based on your experience:
>
> - **Rule 1:** *If* the room is **HOT** *and* the humidity is **HIGH**, *then* set compressor to **HIGH**.
> - **Rule 2:** *If* the room is **WARM**, *then* set compressor to **MEDIUM**.
> - **Rule 3:** *If* the room is **COOL**, *then* set compressor to **LOW**.
>
> The words **HOT, WARM, COOL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW** are *fuzzy* — there is no single sharp temperature defining "HOT." Your brain uses overlapping fuzzy regions. A **Fuzzy If-Then Rule** is precisely this kind of linguistic expert rule, mathematically encoded so a computer can simulate your reasoning.

> **Why it matters:** Unlike classical Boolean logic (TRUE / FALSE), fuzzy rules allow **partial truth** (e.g., 0.7 true), making them perfect for human-like reasoning under imprecision, vagueness, and uncertainty.

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Context

Fuzzy If-Then Rules are deployed in:
- **Consumer electronics:** Washing machines, cameras (autofocus, exposure), vacuum cleaners.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock braking systems (ABS), automatic transmission control.
- **Industrial process control:** Furnace temperature, cement kiln, water treatment plants.
- **Medical diagnosis:** Expert systems for disease classification.
- **Financial forecasting:** Stock market trend prediction.
- **Pattern recognition:** Speech recognition, image segmentation.

The classical foundational metric in fuzzy systems is the **Mamdani Implication** (1974), built upon **Zadeh's (1973)** compositional rule of inference.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Fuzzy If-Then Rule

A Fuzzy If-Then Rule has **two essential parts**:

### (A) Antecedent (Premise / IF-part)
A fuzzy proposition about the input variable(s). May be:
- **Single:** $x$ is $A$
- **Compound (AND):** $x_1$ is $A_1$ **AND** $x_2$ is $A_2$
- **Compound (OR):** $x_1$ is $A_1$ **OR** $x_2$ is $A_2$

**Fuzzy operators used to combine antecedents:**
- AND → **T-norm** (typically **min** or **algebraic product**)
- OR → **S-norm / T-conorm** (typically **max** or **probabilistic sum**)

### (B) Consequent (Conclusion / THEN-part)
The output specification. Type depends on the FIS architecture:
- **Mamdani / Larsen:** Consequent is a fuzzy set $B$
- **Takagi-Sugeno (TSK):** Consequent is a crisp function $y = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$
- **Tsukamoto:** Consequent is a monotonic fuzzy set (singleton output derived from rule firing strength)

## 2.2 Formal Fuzzy Relation $R(x,y)$

A single rule $\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$ is encoded as a fuzzy relation $R$ on $X \times Y$ whose membership function is:

$$\mu_R(x,y) = \mu_{A \rightarrow B}(x,y) = I(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y))$$

where $I(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a **fuzzy implication operator** (see Section 2.4).

## 2.3 Steps of Fuzzy Inference (KTU Board-Standard)

When an input $x_0$ is presented, the FIS executes **5 standard steps**:

| Step | Name | Operation |
|------|------|-----------|
| **1** | **Fuzzification** | Convert crisp input $x_0$ to membership values $\mu_A(x_0)$ for each relevant fuzzy set $A$. |
| **2** | **Antecedent Evaluation** | Apply T-norm (min) or S-norm (max) to combine antecedent membership values → produce **firing strength** $\alpha_i$ for each rule $R_i$. |
| **3** | **Implication** | Apply the rule to obtain output fuzzy set $B_i' = \alpha_i \rightarrow B$ (modifies the consequent). |
| **4** | **Aggregation** | Combine all rule output fuzzy sets into a single aggregated fuzzy set $B^* = \bigcup_i B_i'$. |
| **5** | **Defuzzification** | Convert the aggregated fuzzy set $B^*$ into a crisp output $y^*$. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All fuzzy operators use T-norms and S-norms. KTU expects students to know both Zadeh and algebraic variants.**

### Table 1: T-Norm Operators (Fuzzy AND)

| Name | Formula |
|------|---------|
| **Minimum (Zadeh)** | $T_m(a,b) = \min(a,b)$ |
| **Algebraic Product** | $T_p(a,b) = a \cdot b$ |
| **Bounded Product** | $T_b(a,b) = \max(0, a + b - 1)$ |
| **Drastic Product** | $T_d(a,b) = a$ if $b=1$; $b$ if $a=1$; $0$ otherwise |

### Table 2: S-Norm Operators (Fuzzy OR)

| Name | Formula |
|------|---------|
| **Maximum (Zadeh)** | $S_m(a,b) = \max(a,b)$ |
| **Algebraic Sum** | $S_p(a,b) = a + b - a \cdot b$ |
| **Bounded Sum** | $S_b(a,b) = \min(1, a + b)$ |
| **Drastic Sum** | $S_d(a,b) = a$ if $b=0$; $b$ if $a=0$; $1$ otherwise |

### Table 3: Fuzzy Implication Operators

| Implication Type | Formula $\mu_R(x,y) = I(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(y))$ |
|------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| **Mamdani (min)** | $I_M(a,b) = \min(a,b)$ |
| **Larsen (product)** | $I_L(a,b) = a \cdot b$ |
| **Zadeh (arithmetic)** | $I_Z(a,b) = \max(1-a, \min(a,b))$ |
| **Lukasiewicz** | $I_{Lk}(a,b) = \min(1, 1-a+b)$ |

### Table 4: Defuzzification Methods (Step 5)

| Method | Formula |
|--------|---------|
| **Centroid (COA / COG)** | $y^* = \dfrac{\sum_{i} y_i \cdot \mu_{B^*}(y_i)}{\sum_{i} \mu_{B^*}(y_i)}$ |
| **Bisector** | $y^*$ such that $\int_{-\infty}^{y^*} \mu_{B^*}(y)\,dy = \int_{y^*}^{\infty} \mu_{B^*}(y)\,dy$ |
| **Mean of Maximum (MOM)** | $y^* = \dfrac{\sum y_i \in M}{ \vert M \vert }$, where $M$ is the set of points with $\mu_{B^*}(y)=\max\mu_{B^*}$ |
| **Smallest of Maximum (SOM)** | $y^* = \min \lbrace y : \mu_{B^*}(y) = \max \mu_{B^*} \rbrace$ |
| **Largest of Maximum (LOM)** | $y^* = \max \lbrace y : \mu_{B^*}(y) = \max \mu_{B^*} \rbrace$ |
| **Weighted Average (Sugeno)** | $y^* = \dfrac{\sum_i \alpha_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_i \alpha_i}$ |

> **Note:** In all formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ is rendered as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.5 Why Fuzzy If-Then Rules? — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why:** Classical logic fails to handle linguistic vagueness (e.g., "warm", "high", "fast"). Fuzzy rules mimic human decision-making with partial truths.
- **How:** Each rule contributes a weighted output; the system combines them to produce a smooth, continuous, non-linear mapping. This gives fuzzy systems their hallmark **interpolation property** — any input between rule regions still produces a sensible output.

> **Engineering Utility:** Used in adaptive cruise control, anti-lock braking, autofocus cameras, climate control, industrial kiln controllers, and embedded AI in IoT devices.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mamdani Fuzzy Implication Relation

**Given:** A fuzzy rule $\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$.

**Goal:** Derive the fuzzy relation $R$ encoding this rule.

### Step 1 — Identify the Linguistic Variables
The input space is $X$ with membership function $\mu_A(x)$. The output space is $Y$ with membership function $\mu_B(y)$.

### Step 2 — Define the Implication Operator
Mamdani proposed using the **minimum (T-norm)** as the implication operator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{R}(x,y) &= I_M(\mu_A(x),\,\mu_B(y)) \\[4pt]
&= \min\bigl(\mu_A(x),\,\mu_B(y)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
For any specific pair $(x_0, y_0)$, the truth of the rule's joint statement is the **weaker** of the two antecedent truth and consequent truth. This matches the intuition that a chain is only as strong as its weakest link.

### Step 4 — When Input $x_0$ is Crisp
For a crisp input $x_0$, the rule fires with **firing strength**:

$$
\alpha = \mu_A(x_0)
$$

The output fuzzy set $B'$ is obtained by **clipping** $B$ at level $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{B'}(y) &= \min(\alpha, \mu_B(y)) \\[4pt]
&= \min\bigl(\mu_A(x_0),\, \mu_B(y)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Mamdani clipping** method.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Larsen Implication Relation

Larsen proposed using the **algebraic product** instead of min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{R}(x,y) &= I_L(\mu_A(x),\,\mu_B(y)) \\[4pt]
&= \mu_A(x) \cdot \mu_B(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a crisp input $x_0$, the consequent is **scaled** (not clipped):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{B'}(y) &= \alpha \cdot \mu_B(y) \\[4pt]
&= \mu_A(x_0) \cdot \mu_B(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Larsen scaling** method — preserves the full shape of $B$ scaled by $\alpha$.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Full Fuzzy Inference

**System Setup:**
- Two inputs: $x_1$ (Temperature, ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$), $x_2$ (Humidity, %)
- One output: $y$ (Fan Speed, %)
- Two rules using **Mamdani** and **Centroid defuzzification**:

> **R1:** IF $x_1$ is **Cold** AND $x_2$ is **Dry** THEN $y$ is **Slow**
> **R2:** IF $x_1$ is **Warm** AND $x_2$ is **Humid** THEN $y$ is **Fast**

**Membership functions (given):**

| Variable | Fuzzy Set | Membership Function (discrete) |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| $x_1 = 25$ | Cold | $\mu_{\text{Cold}}(25) = 0.2$ |
| $x_1 = 25$ | Warm | $\mu_{\text{Warm}}(25) = 0.7$ |
| $x_2 = 60$ | Dry | $\mu_{\text{Dry}}(60) = 0.4$ |
| $x_2 = 60$ | Humid | $\mu_{\text{Humid}}(60) = 0.8$ |
| Output $y$ | Slow (centroid at 30) | $\alpha$-clipped to 0.5 |
| Output $y$ | Fast (centroid at 80) | $\alpha$-clipped to 0.6 |

### Step A — Fuzzification (Step 1 of FIS)
At $x_1 = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $x_2 = 60\%$:

$$
\mu_{\text{Cold}}(25) = 0.2, \quad \mu_{\text{Warm}}(25) = 0.7
$$
$$
\mu_{\text{Dry}}(60) = 0.4, \quad \mu_{\text{Humid}}(60) = 0.8
$$

### Step B — Antecedent Evaluation using T-norm MIN (Step 2)

**For Rule R1 (Cold AND Dry):**
$$
\alpha_1 = \min\bigl(\mu_{\text{Cold}}(25),\,\mu_{\text{Dry}}(60)\bigr) = \min(0.2,\,0.4) = 0.2
$$

**For Rule R2 (Warm AND Humid):**
$$
\alpha_2 = \min\bigl(\mu_{\text{Warm}}(25),\,\mu_{\text{Humid}}(60)\bigr) = \min(0.7,\,0.8) = 0.6
$$

(If we had used the **algebraic product** instead: $\alpha_1 = 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08$; $\alpha_2 = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$.)

### Step C — Implication (Step 3)
The Slow and Fast fuzzy sets are clipped by their respective $\alpha$ values (Mamdani min).

**Output fuzzy set from R1** (Slow clipped to 0.2):
$$
\mu_{\text{Slow}'}(y) = \min(0.2,\,\mu_{\text{Slow}}(y)) = 0.2 \text{ for all } y \in \text{support(Slow)}
$$

**Output fuzzy set from R2** (Fast clipped to 0.6):
$$
\mu_{\text{Fast}'}(y) = \min(0.6,\,\mu_{\text{Fast}}(y)) = 0.6 \text{ for all } y \in \text{support(Fast)}
$$

### Step D — Aggregation (Step 4) using S-norm MAX
The aggregated output fuzzy set is the union of clipped outputs:

$$
\mu_{B^*}(y) = \max\bigl(\mu_{\text{Slow}'}(y),\,\mu_{\text{Fast}'}(y)\bigr)
$$

For each $y$, we take the maximum of the two rule contributions.

### Step E — Defuzzification using Centroid (Step 5)

Assume Slow is supported on $y \in [10, 50]$ with centroid $y_{\text{Slow}} = 30$ and Fast is supported on $y \in [60, 100]$ with centroid $y_{\text{Fast}} = 80$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y^* &= \frac{\sum_i \alpha_i \cdot y_{\text{centroid}_i}}{\sum_i \alpha_i} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(0.2)(30) + (0.6)(80)}{0.2 + 0.6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6.0 + 48.0}{0.8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{54.0}{0.8} \\[6pt]
&= 67.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Final Result
**Crisp Fan Speed Output: $y^* = 67.5\%$**

The fan should run at approximately **67.5% speed** for inputs of $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ temperature and $60\%$ humidity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Implications of T-norm / T-conorm Choice

| Rule Architecture | Antecedent Operator | Implication Operator | Aggregation | Best For |
|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Mamdani** | min | min (clipping) | max | Interpretable, control apps |
| **Larsen** | min or product | product (scaling) | max | Smooth output surfaces |
| **Tsukamoto** | min | monotonic $B$ | weighted sum | Each rule gives a crisp output |
| **Takagi-Sugeno** | min or product | linear function $y=ax+b$ | weighted avg | Adaptive systems, ANFIS, optimization |
| **Standard Additive (SAM)** | product | product | sum | Hardware-friendly, real-time |

## 3.5 Conversion Logic: T-norm to Firing Strength Algebra

For a compound rule with $n$ antecedents connected by AND:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= T\bigl(\mu_{A_1}(x_1),\,\mu_{A_2}(x_2),\,\ldots,\,\mu_{A_n}(x_n)\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \min_{i}\bigl(\mu_{A_i}(x_i)\bigr) \quad \text{(Zadeh MIN)} \\[4pt]
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mu_{A_i}(x_i) \quad \text{(Algebraic product)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a compound rule with $n$ antecedents connected by OR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= S\bigl(\mu_{A_1}(x_1),\,\mu_{A_2}(x_2),\,\ldots,\,\mu_{A_n}(x_n)\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= \max_{i}\bigl(\mu_{A_i}(x_i)\bigr) \quad \text{(Zadeh MAX)} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(1-\mu_{A_i}(x_i)\bigr) \quad \text{(Algebraic sum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a Fuzzy If-Then Rule with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

A **Fuzzy If-Then Rule** is a linguistic conditional statement of the form:

$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

where $A$ and $B$ are fuzzy sets defined on universes of discourse $X$ and $Y$ respectively. The IF-part is called the **antecedent** and the THEN-part is the **consequent**.

**Example:**
> *IF temperature is HIGH THEN fan_speed is FAST.*

Here, "HIGH" and "FAST" are fuzzy linguistic terms with associated membership functions. The rule maps input "temperature" to output "fan speed" using a fuzzy relation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] List any three common fuzzy implication operators used in Mamdani-style Fuzzy If-Then Rules.

**Model Answer:**

Three common fuzzy implication operators are:

1. **Mamdani Minimum Implication:** $\mu_R(x,y) = \min(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(y))$
2. **Larsen Product Implication:** $\mu_R(x,y) = \mu_A(x) \cdot \mu_B(y)$
3. **Zadeh Arithmetic Implication:** $\mu_R(x,y) = \max(1 - \mu_A(x),\, \min(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(y)))$

All three map the antecedent fuzzy set $A$ and consequent fuzzy set $B$ to a fuzzy relation $R$ on $X \times Y$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (KTU Internal Choice Format)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> *"Explain the canonical form of Fuzzy If-Then Rules. Differentiate between Mamdani and Takagi-Sugeno (TSK) Fuzzy If-Then Rules with examples."*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Canonical Form of Fuzzy If-Then Rules:**

The canonical form expresses a single rule as:

$$\text{IF } x \text{ is } A \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B$$

where:
- $x, y$ are linguistic variables
- $A \subseteq X$ and $B \subseteq Y$ are fuzzy sets
- The rule defines a fuzzy relation $R(x,y) = I(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(y))$

A **compound** rule combines multiple antecedents using AND (T-norm) or OR (T-conorm). A **rule base** is a collection of such rules that jointly cover the input-output space.

**2. Mamdani vs. Takagi-Sugeno (TSK):**

| Feature | Mamdani | Takagi-Sugeno (TSK) |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| **Consequent** | Fuzzy set $B$ | Linear/Crisp function $y = f(x_1,\ldots,x_n)$ |
| **Implication** | $\min(\alpha, \mu_B(y))$ (clipping) | $y_i = f_i(\mathbf{x})$ (no defuzzification per rule) |
| **Aggregation** | Max of all clipped sets | Weighted average: $y^* = \frac{\sum \alpha_i y_i}{\sum \alpha_i}$ |
| **Defuzzification** | Required at end (centroid, MOM) | Not needed (output is already crisp) |
| **Interpretability** | High (linguistic) | Low (mathematical) |
| **Example** | IF temp is HIGH THEN fan is FAST | IF temp is HIGH THEN fan = 0.8·temp + 10 |
| **Computational cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Best for** | Expert systems, control | Adaptive systems, optimization (ANFIS) |

**3. Example — Mamdani Rule:**
> *IF (temperature is Hot) AND (humidity is High) THEN (fan_speed is Fast).*
> The consequent "Fast" is a fuzzy set with its own membership function.

**4. Example — TSK Rule:**
> *IF (temperature is Hot) AND (humidity is High) THEN (fan_speed = 0.6·temp + 0.4·humidity).*
> The consequent is a linear crisp function of the inputs.

**5. Conclusion:**
Mamdani is preferred for **interpretability and human-like reasoning**, while TSK is preferred for **computational efficiency and integration with adaptive learning algorithms** like ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System).

#### Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

> *"A two-input, single-output fuzzy system has the following two rules:*
