Therefore: $$y^* = 3.0$$ --- **Comparative Results:** - Centroid: $3.107$ - Bisector: $2.60$ - MOM: $3.00$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to use absolute values** in MOM/SOM/LOM — students sometimes average ALL $y$ values instead of only those at the peak. > 2. **Mixing up Sugeno and Mamdani formulas** — Sugeno uses weighted average (algebraic), Mamdani requires integral-based defuzzification. > 3. **Skipping the integration limits** in continuous Centroid problems — always specify $y_{min}$ and $y_{max}$. > 4. **Not normalizing the denominator** in weighted average — if $\sum w_i \neq 1$, the denominator $\sum w_i$ MUST appear. > 5. **Confusing Center of Sums with Centroid** — Center of Sums sums all output sets BEFORE integration, while Centroid integrates the combined final set. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Defuzzification** converts a fuzzy output set to a single crisp value; it is the **final stage** of a Mamdani FIS. - **Seven key defuzzification methods** for KTU: Centroid, Bisector, MOM, SOM, LOM, Weighted Average, Center of Sums. - **Centroid formula:** $y^* = \dfrac{\int y \mu(y) \, dy}{\int \mu(y) \, dy}$ — *most frequently asked, ~60% of marks*. - **Mamdani FIS** uses **fuzzy consequents** (linguistic), proposed in **1975**, and always requires an explicit defuzzification step. - **Sugeno FIS** uses **crisp/linear consequents**, proposed in **1985**, and uses the **weighted average** formula directly. - **Sugeno's weighted average:** $y^* = \dfrac{\sum w_i z_i}{\sum w_i}$ — simplest and most exam-friendly. - **Firing strength** of a rule = T-norm (Min or Product) of antecedent memberships. - **Mamdani vs Sugeno** — at least 4 comparison points are required for full marks. - **Sugeno advantages:** lower computation, ANFIS compatibility, smooth output. - **Mamdani advantages:** interpretability, expert knowledge embedding, transparency. - **Discretization trick:** When continuous integration is not feasible, use $\sum y_k \mu_k \,/\, \sum \mu_k$. - **Always show all intermediate steps** — KTU examiners deduct marks for skipped algebra. - **In a multimodal output**, the CoG is preferred over MOM for smooth control surfaces. - **Center of Sums** is computationally cheaper than CoG but can shift the centroid. - **ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System)** is a hybrid that uses Sugeno-type FIS trained via neural-network gradient descent — frequently referenced in KTU Module 5.

topics.title: Evolutionary Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evolutionary Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Evolutionary Computing (EC)** is a sub-field of computational intelligence and soft computing that employs iterative metaheuristic optimization algorithms inspired by the mechanics of biological evolution — specifically, the principles of **Darwinian natural selection**, genetic inheritance, random variation, and survival of the fittest.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> Evolutionary Computing refers to a family of population-based, stochastic search algorithms that maintain a population of candidate solutions and evolve them over successive generations using selection, variation (crossover and mutation), and replacement operators to converge toward optimal or near-optimal solutions to complex, often non-differentiable, real-world problems.

The major paradigms under the Evolutionary Computing umbrella include:

| EC Paradigm | Origin / Inventor | Key Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** | John Holland (1975) | Discrete/Combinatorial optimization |
| **Evolution Strategies (ES)** | Rechenberg, Schwefel (1960s) | Continuous numerical optimization |
| **Evolutionary Programming (EP)** | Lawrence Fogel (1960s) | Finite-state machine evolution |
| **Genetic Programming (GP)** | John Koza (1992) | Evolving computer programs / trees |
| **Differential Evolution (DE)** | Storn & Price (1995) | Real-parameter optimization |
| **Genetic Programming (GP)** | John Koza (1992) | Symbolic regression, automation |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Island of the Fittest"

Imagine an **archipelago of islands**, each hosting a population of creatures competing for a fixed supply of food. The climate changes every year (a generation):

1. **Initial Population** — Many creatures of different shapes/sizes land on the island (random initial solutions).
2. **Evaluation** — Each creature's ability to gather food is measured (fitness evaluation).
3. **Selection** — The well-fed creatures survive and reproduce; the starving ones die out (selection pressure).
4. **Reproduction with Variation** — Offspring inherit traits from parents but with small random tweaks (crossover + mutation).
5. **Replacement** — A new generation replaces the old (generational update).
6. **Convergence** — After many generations, the population converges to creatures optimally shaped for the environment (optimal/near-optimal solution).

> [!NOTE] > **The two fundamental forces of EC:**
> - **Exploitation** — refining the best known solutions (intensification via selection).
> - **Exploration** — searching new regions of the solution space (diversification via mutation).

This is precisely how EC balances the search of complex landscapes that classical calculus-based or exhaustive methods cannot handle efficiently.

### 1.3 Why Use Evolutionary Computing? — The Problem with Classical Methods

| Classical Method | Why It Fails | How EC Overcomes It |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient Descent | Requires differentiability, gets stuck in local optima | Population-based search escapes local optima |
| Exhaustive Search | Combinatorial explosion (NP-hard problems) | Heuristic search prunes the space efficiently |
| Linear/Dynamic Programming | Needs well-structured subproblems | No structural assumptions about the problem |
| Hill Climbing | No memory of past solutions | Population retains diverse candidate solutions |

> [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** EC does *not* guarantee the global optimum, but it consistently finds **high-quality, robust solutions** in reasonable time — a property called *anytime behavior*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic Evolutionary Algorithm — Operational Steps

Every EC algorithm follows a common skeleton. Let **P(t)** denote the population at generation **t**:

1. **Initialize** P(0) with random candidate solutions (chromosomes/individuals).
2. **Evaluate** the fitness f(x) of every individual in P(t).
3. **Select** parents from P(t) based on fitness-proportional or rank-based criteria.
4. **Vary** offspring via crossover (recombination) and mutation operators.
5. **Replace** some/all of P(t) with the new offspring to form P(t+1).
6. **Test convergence** — if a stopping criterion is satisfied, halt; else, set t ← t+1 and go to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Stopping Criteria (KTU Board Favorites):**
> - Fixed number of generations (computational budget).
> - No improvement in best fitness for **k** consecutive generations.
> - Population convergence (all individuals have ≥ 99% similarity).
> - Achieved target fitness threshold.

### 2.2 The Genetic Algorithm (GA) — Detailed Working

The GA, proposed by **John Henry Holland in 1975** and popularized by **David Goldberg (1989)**, is the canonical EC algorithm and the most tested in KTU examinations.

#### 2.2.1 Chromosome Encoding Schemes

| Encoding | Chromosome Representation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Encoding** | Bit string of length L (e.g., 1011001) | Classic discrete optimization |
| **Real-Valued (Float)** | Vector of reals (e.g., [3.14, 2.71]) | Continuous function optimization |
| **Permutation** | Ordered list of integers (e.g., [3,1,4,2,5]) | TSP, scheduling problems |
| **Tree-based** | LISP-style S-expressions | Genetic Programming |

#### 2.2.2 Fitness Function

The fitness function **f(x)** is the objective function that quantifies how "good" a candidate solution **x** is. It maps the decoded chromosome into a non-negative real number, often requiring transformation:

$$F(x) = \begin{cases} f(x) & \text{if } f(x) \geq 0 \\ \frac{1}{1 + c - f(x)} & \text{otherwise (minimization)} \end{cases}$$

where **c** is the largest absolute value of f(x) in the population, and **F(x)** is the scaled fitness.

#### 2.2.3 Selection Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield)

Let **N** be the population size, **fᵢ** the fitness of individual *i*, and **f̄** the mean fitness.

**(a) Roulette Wheel Selection (Fitness-Proportional Selection):**
Probability of selecting individual *i*:

$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{f_i}{N \cdot \bar{f}}$$

Expected number of copies of individual *i*:

$$E_i = N \cdot p_i = \frac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$$

**(b) Rank-Based Selection:**
Individuals are sorted by fitness; the *i*-th ranked individual gets selection probability:

$$p_i = \frac{2 \cdot s}{N(N+1)} \cdot (N - i + 1)$$

where **s** is the selection pressure (typically 1.0 ≤ s ≤ 2.0).

**(c) Tournament Selection:**
Randomly pick **k** individuals; select the fittest. Probability of size-k tournament:

$$P(\text{win}) = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{k-1} \quad \text{(for k=2: } P = 0.5\text{)}$$

**(d) Elitism:**
The top **e** individuals are copied directly to the next generation unmodified, guaranteeing that the best-so-far fitness never degrades.

#### 2.2.4 Crossover Operators (Recombination)

| Operator | Mechanism | Crossover Rate (p_c) |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Point Crossover** | One cut point splits parents, tails are swapped | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| **Two-Point Crossover** | Two cut points, middle segment is exchanged | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| **Uniform Crossover** | Each gene independently chosen from either parent | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| **Arithmetic Crossover** | Offspring = α·parent1 + (1−α)·parent2 (real-coded) | 0.6 – 0.9 |

#### 2.2.5 Mutation Operators

| Operator | Mechanism | Mutation Rate (p_m) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-Flip Mutation** | Each bit inverted with probability p_m | 1/L (L = chromosome length) |
| **Swap Mutation** | Two random gene positions are exchanged | 0.01 – 0.1 |
| **Scramble Mutation** | A subset of genes is randomly shuffled | 0.01 – 0.1 |
| **Gaussian Mutation** | gene ← gene + N(0, σ²) (real-coded) | 0.01 – 0.1 |

#### 2.2.6 Replacement Strategies

- **Generational Replacement** — Entire population replaced each generation.
- **Steady-State Replacement** — Only a few offspring inserted per generation.
- **(μ + λ) — ES Strategy** — Best μ chosen from combined μ parents + λ offspring.
- **(μ, λ) — ES Strategy** — Best μ chosen from λ offspring only (no parent survival).

### 2.3 Schema Theorem (Holland's Fundamental Theorem of GA)

A **schema** is a similarity template describing a subset of strings with similarities at certain string positions. For example, the schema **H = 1*0*1** matches {10001, 10011, 11001, 11011}.

Notation:
- **o(H)** = order of schema (number of fixed positions)
- **δ(H)** = defining length (distance between outermost fixed bits)
- **m(H, t)** = number of strings matching H at generation t
- **f(H)** = average fitness of strings matching H

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Holland's Schema Theorem (1975) — The Heart of GA Theory:**
>
> $$m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$$
>
> Above-average, low-order, short-defining-length schemata (called **building blocks**) grow exponentially across generations — this is the theoretical foundation of GA's power.

### 2.4 Convergence Proof (Rudolph's Theorem)

A standard GA with elitism (preserving the best individual) **converges in probability to the global optimum**:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P\left[ f^* \in P(t) \right] = 1$$

where **f*** is the optimal fitness value.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$ | Roulette wheel probability |
| 2 | $E_i = f_i / \bar{f}$ | Expected count of individual *i* |
| 3 | $\bar{f} = (1/N) \sum f_i$ | Mean population fitness |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = \sqrt{(1/N)\sum (f_i - \bar{f})^2}$ | Fitness standard deviation |
| 5 | $F = (1/N)\sum f_i^2 - \bar{f}^2$ | Variance of fitness |
| 6 | $m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot [1 - p_c\delta/(L-1) - p_m o]$ | Schema theorem |
| 7 | $L = \lceil \log_2((u-l)/\Delta) \rceil + 1$ | Binary encoding length for range [l, u] with precision Δ |
| 8 | $x = l + \text{decoded} \cdot (u-l)/(2^L - 1)$ | Decoding binary to real |
| 9 | $P_{tournament} = 0.5$ | Tournament size k = 2 |
| 10 | $\bar{v}_i = F_i / \sum F_i$ | Rank-based expected value |

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** EC is used in antenna design (NASA evolved antenna shapes), financial trading, route optimization (FedEx), drug discovery, robotics gait evolution, neural network weight optimization (neuroevolution), and scheduling (airlines, factories).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: Maximize f(x) = x² on x ∈ [0, 15]

This is the **canonical Goldberg (1989) example** — a KTU board favorite.

**Step 1 — Encoding:**
We use **4-bit binary** chromosomes since 2⁴ = 16 ≥ 16 values (0 to 15).

| Chromosome | Binary | Decoded x | f(x) = x² |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| C2 | 0101 | 5 | 25 |
| C3 | 0011 | 3 | 9 |
| C4 | 1001 | 9 | 81 |

**Step 2 — Compute Total and Mean Fitness:**

$$\sum f_i = 144 + 25 + 9 + 81 = 259$$

$$\bar{f} = \frac{259}{4} = 64.75$$

**Step 3 — Selection Probabilities (Roulette Wheel):**

$$p_1 = \frac{144}{259} = 0.5560$$

$$p_2 = \frac{25}{259} = 0.0965$$

$$p_3 = \frac{9}{259} = 0.0347$$

$$p_4 = \frac{81}{259} = 0.3127$$

Verification: $0.5560 + 0.0965 + 0.0347 + 0.3127 = 0.9999 \approx 1$ ✓

**Step 4 — Expected Count (E_i = N·p_i, N=4):**

$$E_1 = 4 \times 0.5560 = 2.224 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Actual count: 2}$$

$$E_2 = 4 \times 0.0965 = 0.386 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Actual count: 0}$$

$$E_3 = 4 \times 0.0347 = 0.139 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Actual count: 0}$$

$$E_4 = 4 \times 0.3127 = 1.251 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Actual count: 2}$$

(These are derived via stochastic sampling; in deterministic assignment, the rounded counts 2, 0, 0, 2 sum to 4 = N.)

**Step 5 — Mating Pool (after selection):**

| Slot | Chromosome | Binary | Fitness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C1 | 1100 | 144 |
| 2 | C1 | 1100 | 144 |
| 3 | C4 | 1001 | 81 |
| 4 | C4 | 1001 | 81 |

**Step 6 — Single-Point Crossover at position 2 (p_c = 0.7):**
Parents: C1 (1100) × C4 (1001) → Offspring: **O1 = 1101** (x=13, f=169), **O2 = 1000** (x=8, f=64)
Parents: C1 (1100) × C4 (1001) → Offspring: **O3 = 1101** (x=13, f=169), **O4 = 1000** (x=8, f=64)

**Step 7 — Bit-Flip Mutation (p_m = 0.01):**
Assume O1 and O3 have one bit flipped in the last position:
O1: 1101 → 1100 (x=12, f=144)
O3: 1101 → 1100 (x=12, f=144)

**Step 8 — New Generation:**

| Chromosome | Binary | x | f(x) |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1' | 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| O2 | 1000 | 8 | 64 |
| O3' | 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| O4 | 1000 | 8 | 64 |

$$\bar{f}_{\text{new}} = \frac{144 + 64 + 144 + 64}{4} = \frac{416}{4} = 104$$

$$\text{Improvement: } \bar{f}_{\text{new}} - \bar{f}_{\text{old}} = 104 - 64.75 = +39.25 \;\; (60.6\% \text{ increase})$$

> [!NOTE] > **This example demonstrates that even a single generation of GA produces a markedly fitter population on average, validating the schema theorem's claim of exponential growth of high-fitness schemata.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Holland's Schema Theorem (Sketch)

Starting from the growth equation of matching strings under selection alone:

$$m(H, t+1) = m(H, t) \cdot N \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$$

The probability that crossover **destroys** schema H (single-point, with cut falling inside the defining length) is:

$$P_{\text{destroy, crossover}} = \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1}$$

The probability that mutation **destroys** H (any fixed bit flips) is:

$$P_{\text{destroy, mutation}} = 1 - (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \approx p_m \cdot o(H) \quad \text{(for small } p_m\text{)}$$

Combining survival probabilities (assuming independence):

$$P_{\text{survive}} = \left(1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m \cdot o(H))$$

Therefore, the lower bound on growth:

$$m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Schemata that are short (small δ), low-order (small o), and above-average fitness (f(H) > f̄) receive exponentially increasing trials in subsequent generations.

### 3.3 Decoding Formula Derivation (Binary to Real)

For a binary string of length **L** representing a variable in range [l, u] with precision **Δ**:

$$L = \left\lceil \log_2\left(\frac{u - l}{\Delta} + 1\right) \right\rceil$$

A binary string **b_{L-1} b_{L-2} ... b_0** has decimal value:

$$d = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$

Mapped to the real interval:

$$x = l + d \cdot \frac{u - l}{2^L - 1}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Evolutionary Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START]) --> nodeB[Initialize Population P of size N with random individuals]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Decode each chromosome and evaluate fitness f of every individual]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Convergence criterion met}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE([OUTPUT: Best Individual and its Fitness])
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF[Apply Selection Operator to choose parents]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Crossover Operator with probability p_c to produce offspring]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Apply Mutation Operator with probability p_m to offspring]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Evaluate fitness of new offspring]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Apply Replacement Strategy: elitism, generational or steady state]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Update generation counter t = t + 1]
    nodeK --> nodeD
```

### 4.2 Genetic Algorithm — Detailed Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK[INPUT STAGE]
        dirA1[Problem Definition] --> dirA2[Encoding Scheme Selection]
        dirA2 --> dirA3[Parameter Setup: N, p_c, p_m, max_gen]
    end

    subgraph INIT_BLOCK[INITIALIZATION]
        dirB1[Generate Random Population P0]
    end

    subgraph EVAL_BLOCK[FITNESS EVALUATION]
        dirC1[Decode Chromosomes]
        dirC2[Compute Objective Function f x]
        dirC3[Apply Fitness Scaling if needed]
    end

    subgraph GENOPS_BLOCK[GENETIC OPERATORS]
        dirD1[Roulette Wheel Selection]
        dirD2[Tournament Selection]
        dirD3[Single Point Crossover]
        dirD4[Uniform Crossover]
        dirD5[Bit Flip Mutation]
        dirD6[Gaussian Mutation]
    end

    subgraph REPLACE_BLOCK[REPLACEMENT]
        dirE1[Elitism: copy best e individuals]
        dirE2[Generational Replacement]
        dirE3[Steady State Replacement]
    end

    subgraph TERM_BLOCK[TERMINATION]
        dirF1{Max Generations Reached}
        dirF2{Target Fitness Achieved}
        dirF3{No Improvement for k Generations}
    end

    dirA3 --> dirB1 --> dirC1 --> dirC2 --> dirC3
    dirC3 --> dirD1
    dirC3 --> dirD2
    dirD1 --> dirD3
    dirD1 --> dirD4
    dirD3 --> dirD5
    dirD4 --> dirD6
    dirD5 --> dirE1
    dirD6 --> dirE1
    dirE1 --> dirE2
    dirE2 --> dirE3
    dirE3 --> dirF1
    dirF1 -- NO --> dirC1
    dirF1 -- YES --> dirF2
    dirF2 -- NO --> dirF3
    dirF3 -- NO --> dirC1
    dirF2 -- YES --> dirG1([OUTPUT: Optimal Solution])
    dirF3 -- YES --> dirG1
```

### 4.3 Selection, Crossover and Mutation — Mechanism Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1[Parent 1: 1 1 0 0 1 0] --> nodeX1[Crossover at Point 3]
    nodeP2[Parent 2: 0 0 1 1 0 1] --> nodeX1
    nodeX1 --> nodeO1[Offspring 1: 1 1 0 1 0 1]
    nodeX1 --> nodeO2[Offspring 2: 0 0 1 0 1 0]
    nodeO1 --> nodeM1[Bit Flip Mutation at Position 5]
    nodeM1 --> nodeF1[Final Offspring: 1 1 0 1 1 1]
    nodeO2 --> nodeM2[No Mutation]
    nodeM2 --> nodeF2[Final Offspring: 0 0 1 0 1 0]
```

### 4.4 EC Paradigm Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeEC[Evolutionary Computing EC] --> nodeGA[Genetic Algorithms GA]
    nodeEC --> nodeES[Evolution Strategies ES]
    nodeEC --> nodeEP[Evolutionary Programming EP]
    nodeEC --> nodeGP[Genetic Programming GP]
    nodeEC --> nodeDE[Differential Evolution DE]
    nodeGA --> nodeGA1[Binary Encoding]
    nodeGA --> nodeGA2[Real Coded GA]
    nodeES --> nodeES1[mu plus lambda Strategy]
    nodeES --> nodeES2[mu comma lambda Strategy]
    nodeEP --> nodeEP1[Finite State Machines]
    nodeEP --> nodeEP2[Continuous EP]
    nodeGP --> nodeGP1[Tree Based Programs]
    nodeDE --> nodeDE1[Real Parameter Optimization]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Evolutionary Computing. List any four paradigms under it.**

**Model Answer:**

Evolutionary Computing is a family of population-based, stochastic optimization techniques inspired by biological evolution, employing selection, recombination, and mutation to evolve candidate solutions across generations.

Four paradigms:
1. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
2. Evolution Strategies (ES)
3. Genetic Programming (GP)
4. Differential Evolution (DE) / Evolutionary Programming (EP)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing paradigms with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the fitness function in a Genetic Algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

The fitness function **f(x)** evaluates and quantifies the quality of a candidate solution (chromosome) within the population. It serves three critical roles:

1. **Selection Driver** — Higher-fitness individuals receive higher probabilities of being chosen as parents, propagating good genetic material.
2. **Convergence Guide** — It determines the direction of search, biasing the population toward optimal regions.
3. **Performance Metric** — It is the optimization objective being maximized (or minimized) by the algorithm.

In Goldberg's classic example maximizing f(x) = x², the fitness function directly guides the population toward x = 15.

*(3 marks: 2 for role description + 1 for example.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the various selection mechanisms used in Genetic Algorithms. Discuss the merits and demerits of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Selection mechanisms choose parents from the current population for mating. The four principal mechanisms are:

**1. Roulette Wheel Selection (Fitness-Proportional):**
Each individual is assigned a slice of a "wheel" proportional to its fitness. The wheel is spun N times to select N parents.

$$p_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum f_j}$$

- *Merit:* Simple, intuitive, biologically motivated.
- *Demerit:* Premature convergence if one individual dominates; slow convergence when fitness values are close; negative fitness requires scaling.

**2. Rank-Based Selection:**
Individuals are sorted; selection probability depends on rank, not raw fitness.

$$p_i \propto (N - i + 1)$$

- *Merit:* Maintains selection pressure even when fitness values cluster; prevents early dominance.
- *Demerit:* Requires sorting (O(N log N)); loses absolute fitness information.

**3. Tournament Selection:**
Randomly pick k individuals; the fittest wins. Repeat N times.

- *Merit:* No global fitness comparison needed; works with negative fitness; tunable selection pressure via k.
- *Demerit:* Higher tournament size k reduces diversity; stochastic variance can be high.

**4. Elitism:**
The best *e* individuals are copied unchanged to the next generation.

- *Merit:* Guarantees monotonic improvement; preserves best solution.
- *Demerit:* Can reduce exploration if e is too large.

> [!NOTE] > **In KTU boards, draw a comparison table to score full 7 marks.**

---

**(b) [7 marks] Solve ONE generation of a Genetic Algorithm to maximize f(x) = x² for x ∈ [0, 15] starting with the following population: {1100, 0101, 0011, 1001}. Use roulette wheel selection, single-point crossover (cut at position 2) and bit-flip mutation (p_m = 0.01).**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Initial Population Evaluation**

| Chromosome | x | f(x) = x² |
|---|---|---|
| 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| 0101 | 5 | 25 |
| 0011 | 3 | 9 |
| 1001 | 9 | 81 |

$$\sum f = 144 + 25 + 9 + 81 = 259, \quad \bar{f} = 64.75$$

**Step 2: Selection Probabilities and Expected Counts**

| i | fᵢ | pᵢ = fᵢ/259 | Eᵢ = N·pᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 144 | 0.5560 | 2.224 |
| 2 | 25 | 0.0965 | 0.386 |
| 3 | 9 | 0.0347 | 0.139 |
| 4 | 81 | 0.3127 | 1.251 |

**Step 3: Mating Pool (using actual selection — assume 2 copies each of C1 and C4):**

| Slot | Chromosome | Fitness |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1100 | 144 |
| 2 | 1100 | 144 |
| 3 | 1001 | 81 |
| 4 | 1001 | 81 |

**Step 4: Single-Point Crossover at position 2:**

| Cross | Parents | Offspring |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1100 × 1001 | 11 01 = 1101, 10 00 = 1000 |
| 2 | 1100 × 1001 | 11 01 = 1101, 10 00 = 1000 |

**Step 5: Bit-Flip Mutation (assume O1 last bit flipped, others unchanged):**

| Offspring | Binary | x | f(x) |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1' | 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| O2 | 1000 | 8 | 64 |
| O3' | 1100 | 12 | 144 |
| O4 | 1000 | 8 | 64 |

**Step 6: New Generation Statistics**

$$\bar{f}_{\text{new}} = \frac{144 + 64 + 144 + 64}{4} = 104$$

$$\text{Improvement} = 104 - 64.75 = 39.25 \quad (\approx 60.6\% \text{ increase})$$

The best fitness improved from 144 to 144 (preserved), and the mean fitness rose significantly — illustrating GA's population-wide progress.

---

**Question B (Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks] State and explain Holland's Schema Theorem. Discuss its significance in GA theory.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of the Schema Theorem:**

$$m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$$

where:
- **m(H, t)** = number of strings in population matching schema H at generation t
- **f(H)** = average fitness of those matching strings
- **f̄** = average fitness of the entire population
- **δ(H)** = defining length of H
- **o(H)** = order of H
- **p_c, p_m** = crossover and mutation probabilities
- **L** = chromosome length

**Explanation:**

1. **Selection term f(H)/f̄:** If schema H has above-average fitness (f(H) > f̄), it grows multiplicatively each generation.

2. **Crossover survival term 1 − p_c·δ(H)/(L−1):** Short, contiguous schemata (small δ) survive crossover better. Long, scattered schemata are frequently disrupted.

3. **Mutation survival term 1 − p_m·o(H):** Low-order schemata (small o) tolerate mutation better; high-order schemata are vulnerable to bit-flips.

**Significance:**

- **Building Block Hypothesis** — GAs work by combining short, low-order, high-fitness schemata (building blocks) to form longer, higher-fitness strings.
- **Exponential Growth** — Above-average schemata grow as (f(H)/f̄)^t, dominating the population within a few generations.
- **Implicit Parallelism** — Although the population contains N strings, the GA processes ~ O(N³) schemata simultaneously, making it computationally efficient.

> [!NOTE] > The Schema Theorem is essentially the **theoretical justification for why GAs work** — a KTU board favorite for 7-mark derivations.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Compare and contrast the following EC paradigms: Genetic Algorithms (GA), Evolution Strategies (ES), and Genetic Programming (GP). Provide a detailed comparison table.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Genetic Algorithm (GA) | Evolution Strategy (ES) | Genetic Programming (GP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventor / Year** | Holland (1975) | Rechenberg & Schwefel (1965) | Koza (1992) |
| **Encoding** | Binary (classical) or real-valued | Real-valued vectors | Tree structures (LISP S-expressions) |
| **Individual Type** | Fixed-length chromosome | Real vector of strategy parameters | Variable-sized program tree |
| **Main Operator** | Crossover (dominant) | Mutation (dominant) | Subtree crossover |
| **Selection Type** | Roulette / Tournament / Rank | (μ, λ) or (μ + λ) deterministic | Tournament / Fitness-proportional |
| **Self-Adaptation** | No (typically) | Yes (strategy parameters evolve) | No (typically) |
| **Primary Application** | Discrete / combinatorial optimization | Continuous numerical optimization | Symbolic regression, automatic program synthesis |
| **Parameters** | p_c, p_m, population size | μ, λ, σ (mutation step), τ (learning rate) | Tree depth limit, population size |
| **Convergence** | Probabilistic; elitism needed for guarantee | Theoretically founded on (1+1)-ES convergence | Probabilistic with elitism |
| **Example Problem** | TSP, knapsack | Rosenbrock function, sphere function | Symbolic regression, robotic control |

**Key Differences:**

1. **GA emphasizes recombination (crossover)** as the primary search operator, while **ES relies primarily on mutation** with self-adaptive step sizes — this makes ES superior for continuous optimization.
2. **GP operates on executable structures** (parse trees), making it suitable for evolving algorithms, controllers, and mathematical expressions.
3. **GA uses binary representation by tradition**, while ES and modern GA variants use real-valued encoding for numerical problems.

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For continuous function optimization, ES and DE are preferred; for combinatorial problems, GA dominates; for program synthesis, GP is the unique choice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing selection probability p_i with expected count E_i** — they differ by a factor of N. Show both explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify Σp_i = 1** — Examiners award 1 mark for this verification step.
> 3. **Mixing up order o(H) and defining length δ(H)** — *Order* is the count of fixed positions; *defining length* is the distance between the first and last fixed positions.
> 4. **Not decoding chromosomes** before crossover/mutation — Examiners often deduct 1 mark if binary-to-integer conversion is skipped.
> 5. **Citing the schema theorem without explaining the building block hypothesis** — The hypothesis is the *intuition* behind the math; lose 2 marks if omitted.
> 6. **Drawing the GA flowchart without feedback arrows** — The loop from "replacement" back to "fitness evaluation" is mandatory.
> 7. **Using |x| (pipes) in markdown tables** — Use \vert x \vert in LaTeX to avoid breaking table formatting in board-paper PDFs.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Evolutionary Computing):**

**Core Concepts:**
- EC = population-based, stochastic, biologically-inspired optimization.
- Five major paradigms: GA, ES, EP, GP, DE.
- GA cycle: Initialize → Evaluate → Select → Crossover → Mutate → Replace → Loop.

**Mathematical Essentials:**
- Selection probability: $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$
- Expected count: $E_i = N \cdot p_i = f_i / \bar{f}$
- Mean fitness: $\bar{f} = (1/N) \sum f_i$
- Schema theorem: $m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot (f(H)/\bar{f}) \cdot [1 - p_c \delta/(L-1) - p_m o]$
- Decoding: $x = l + d \cdot (u-l)/(2^L - 1)$
- Binary length: $L = \lceil \log_2((u-l)/\Delta + 1) \rceil$

**Operator Defaults (memorize):**
- Population size N = 50 – 200
- Crossover rate $p_c$ = 0.6 – 0.9
- Mutation rate $p_m$ = 0.001 – 0.1 (inversely related to L)
- Tournament size k = 2 – 5
- Elitism e = 1 – 5% of N

**Selection Mechanisms:**
- Roulette Wheel — fitness-proportional, prone to premature convergence.
- Rank-Based — robust against fitness scaling, prevents dominance.
- Tournament — simple, tunable, no global fitness needed.
- Elitism — preserves best, guarantees monotonic improvement.

**Crossover Variants:**
- Single-Point, Two-Point, Uniform (binary).
- Arithmetic / BLX-α (real-valued).
- Subtree Crossover (GP).

**Mutation Variants:**
- Bit-Flip (binary).
- Gaussian / Polynomial (real-valued).
- Swap, Scramble, Inversion (permutation).
- Subtree Mutation (GP).

**Theoretical Pillars:**
- **Schema Theorem (Holland 1975)** — explains exponential growth of building blocks.
- **Building Block Hypothesis (Goldberg 1989)** — short, low-order, high-fitness schemata combine to form optima.
- **Implicit Parallelism** — GA processes O(N³) schemata in parallel.
- **No Free Lunch Theorem (Wolpert 1997)** — no single algorithm is best for all problems; average performance is equal across all problem classes.

**Real-World Applications:**
- Antenna design (NASA ST5 mission — evolved antenna shape).
- Financial portfolio optimization.
- Vehicle routing and scheduling.
- Drug discovery and protein folding.
- Neuroevolution (evolving neural network weights and architectures).
- Robotics gait and controller synthesis.
- Supply chain and logistics optimization.

**Key Differences to Remember:**
- GA = crossover-dominant, discrete focus.
- ES = mutation-dominant with self-adaptation, continuous focus.
- GP = tree-based, program-synthesis focus.
- DE = differential-vector mutation, fast convergence for real-valued problems.

> [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"RULE Mn"** for GA pipeline: **R**oulette/Tournament Select → **U**niform/Single-Point Crossover → **L**ittle mutation (bit-flip) → **E**litism replacement.
> - **"Schema = SIM card"**: **S**hort δ, **I**ow o, **M**ean fitness above average.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminologies of Evolutionary Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Terminologies of Evolutionary Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evolutionary Computing (EC)** is a sub-field of computational intelligence and soft computing that encompasses a family of population-based, stochastic optimization algorithms inspired by the mechanisms of **biological evolution** — namely *natural selection*, *survival of the fittest*, *reproduction*, and *random variation* (mutation and recombination).

According to the standard KTU 2024 syllabus formulation, evolutionary computing refers to a class of **meta-heuristic search algorithms** that maintain a population of candidate solutions and iteratively refine them using operators derived from evolutionary theory, with the goal of finding near-optimal (or optimal) solutions to complex, often NP-hard, optimization problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *Evolutionary Computing is a generic, population-based, stochastic optimization methodology that uses biologically inspired operators such as reproduction, mutation, recombination, and selection to evolve candidate solutions toward optimality over successive generations.*

### 1.2 Core Terminology Snapshot (Quick Glossary)

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Individual** | A single candidate solution in the population. |
| **Chromosome** | The encoded representation of an individual. |
| **Gene** | A single element (position) within a chromosome. |
| **Allele** | The value taken by a particular gene. |
| **Population** | A collection of individuals maintained at one iteration. |
| **Generation** | One full iteration of the evolutionary cycle. |
| **Fitness** | A numerical score measuring how good a solution is. |
| **Selection** | Operator that biases reproduction toward fitter individuals. |
| **Crossover** | Recombination operator mixing two parent chromosomes. |
| **Mutation** | Small random perturbation applied to a chromosome. |
| **Phenotype** | The decoded, real-world expression of a chromosome. |
| **Genotype** | The raw encoded form of the individual. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Darwin's Garden

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box: "The Butterfly Garden"**
>
> Imagine a large garden with thousands of butterflies. Each butterfly's wing pattern determines how well it can hide from birds (its **fitness**). Every season, birds eat the most visible butterflies (low-fitness ones are **selected out**). The surviving butterflies reproduce, and their offspring inherit a mix of their parents' wing patterns (**crossover**), with occasional small random color changes (**mutation**). Over many seasons, the garden is dominated by hard-to-spot butterflies. This is precisely what an evolutionary algorithm does: it breeds better and better solutions to your problem.

This analogy maps directly to the table below:

| Biological Term | EC Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Organism (butterfly) | Individual / Chromosome |
| Trait (wing color) | Gene / Allele |
| Environment fitness | Objective / Fitness function |
| Survival of the fittest | Selection operator |
| Mating of parents | Crossover / Recombination |
| Random genetic drift | Mutation operator |
| Generation of offspring | New generation |
| Species over time | Converged population |

### 1.4 Why Evolutionary Computing? (Syllabus Justification)

EC is preferred over classical gradient-based optimization when:

- The objective function is **non-differentiable**, **discontinuous**, or **noisy**.
- The search space is **large**, **combinatorial**, or **multi-modal**.
- The problem is **dynamic** (the landscape changes over time).
- A **good-enough** solution is acceptable when the exact optimum is computationally infeasible.

Standard reference metrics frequently mentioned in KTU 2024 question papers:

- **Population size (N)**: typically **50–200** individuals.
- **Chromosome length (L)**: depends on encoding precision.
- **Number of generations (G)**: typically **100–1000**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic Tip:** Whenever asked *"Why EC over gradient methods?"*, the one-line KTU-standard answer is: *EC does not require gradient information and is therefore suited to non-differentiable, non-convex, black-box optimization problems.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Evolutionary Computing

The execution of any Evolutionary Algorithm (EA) follows a strict, well-defined cycle. Below is the **universal EA template** (canonical pseudocode-level reasoning) that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

**Step-by-Step Operational Logic:**

1. **Initialization (t = 0)**
   Generate an initial population $P(0)$ of $N$ individuals, each encoding a candidate solution. Encoding may be binary, real-valued, integer, or permutation-based.

2. **Evaluation (Fitness Assignment)**
   For every individual $x_i \in P(t)$, compute the **fitness value** $f(x_i)$ using the problem-specific fitness function.

3. **Selection**
   Choose parent individuals from $P(t)$ for reproduction, with selection probability proportional (or biased) to fitness. The mathematical form of the selection operator (roulette-wheel, tournament, rank-based) determines selection pressure.

4. **Variation — Crossover (Recombination)**
   With probability $p_c$ (the **crossover probability**, typically **0.6–0.9**), combine two parents $p_1, p_2$ to produce one or more offspring $c_1, c_2$.

5. **Variation — Mutation**
   With probability $p_m$ (the **mutation probability**, typically small, e.g., **0.001–0.05**), perturb each gene of each offspring stochastically.

6. **Replacement (Environmental Selection)**
   Form the next population $P(t+1)$ from a combination of parents and offspring, often using **elitism** (carrying the best $k$ individuals forward unchanged).

7. **Termination Check**
   Stop when a stopping criterion is met (e.g., maximum generations, target fitness, convergence, or computational budget exhausted). Otherwise, set $t = t + 1$ and go to Step 2.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Chromosome — The Genotype / Phenotype Distinction

The **genotype** is the raw string of symbols stored in the chromosome. The **phenotype** is what the genotype *means* in the problem space.

For example, given a binary genotype:

$$\text{Genotype: } 10110$$

If the encoding is Gray code with 5 bits representing an integer in $[0, 31]$, the phenotype could be:

$$\text{Phenotype: } x = 22$$

This **decoding step** is essential — the algorithm operates on genotypes, but fitness is evaluated on phenotypes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pearl:** A frequent exam question asks, *"Where is the fitness function applied — on the genotype or the phenotype?"* The correct KTU answer is: **the fitness function is applied on the phenotype**, after decoding the genotype.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the most frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 3.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \lceil \log_2((u - l) \cdot 10^p) \rceil$ | Binary encoding length for range $[l, u]$ at $p$ decimal digits of precision | Determining chromosome length |
| 2 | $p_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Fitness-proportionate (roulette-wheel) selection probability for individual $i$ | Selection mechanism |
| 3 | $E[T] = N$ | Expected number of copies of individual $i$ under fitness-proportionate selection | Selection pressure |
| 4 | $m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \left[1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | **Schema Theorem** (Holland, 1975) | Lower bound on schema survival |
| 5 | $p_m = \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Recommended mutation probability ($L$ = chromosome length) | Parameter setting |
| 6 | $d_H(x, y) = \sum_{k=1}^{L} \vert x_k - y_k \vert$ | Hamming distance between two binary chromosomes | Diversity measure |
| 7 | $\bar{f}(t) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(x_i)$ | Mean population fitness at generation $t$ | Convergence tracking |
| 8 | $f_{\max}(t) = \max_{x_i \in P(t)} f(x_i)$ | Maximum (best) fitness at generation $t$ | Convergence tracking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX Rule Reminder:** In the table above, all absolute-value and set-membership expressions use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x_k - y_k \vert$) instead of a bare `|` pipe, to prevent the table rows from breaking in the rendered markdown. **Students are advised to adopt the same convention when writing ESE answers.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evolutionary computing terminologies are not abstract vocabulary — they map directly onto real engineering systems:

- **Chromosome encoding** → choosing how to represent a wing's aerodynamic parameters in aircraft design optimization.
- **Fitness function** → defining the cost-to-go in a robot's path-planning problem.
- **Generation** → one iteration of redesign in an automated antenna-tuning loop.
- **Convergence** → the point at which a control system has stabilized on a near-optimal controller gain set.

In KTU 2024 scheme, applications expected in answers include: **neural network weight optimization (training)**, **job-shop scheduling**, **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **feature selection in machine learning**, and **PID controller tuning**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Context:** When asked in ESE to *"Name two real-world applications of EC"*, KTU board examiners expect answers like: *training the weights of a neural network using GA (genetic algorithm)* and *solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)*. Always mention the application **and** the EC variant used.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the rigorous derivations of the **most-asked EC formulae** in KTU 2024 ESE.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Binary Encoding Length for a Given Precision

**Problem Statement:**
Given a continuous decision variable $x \in [a, b]$ and a required precision of $p$ decimal places, determine the minimum number of binary bits $L$ required for a chromosome to represent every distinguishable value of $x$.

**Step 1 — Determine the number of distinct representable values.**
The interval $[a, b]$ has length $(b - a)$. With $p$ decimal places of precision, the smallest distinguishable increment is $10^{-p}$. Therefore, the number of distinct values $N_{\text{val}}$ we must encode is:

$$N_{\text{val}} = \frac{b - a}{10^{-p}} = (b - a) \cdot 10^{p}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the minimum bit length.**
A binary string of length $L$ can represent $2^{L}$ distinct patterns. We require $2^{L} \geq N_{\text{val}}$. Taking binary logarithms:

$$L \cdot \log_2(2) \geq \log_2\left((b - a) \cdot 10^{p}\right)$$

$$L \geq \log_2\left((b - a) \cdot 10^{p}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the ceiling to obtain the smallest integer $L$.**

$$L = \left\lceil \log_2\!\left((b - a) \cdot 10^{p}\right) \right\rceil$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula appears in **nearly every KTU ESE paper** for Module 3. Memorize it; also remember the alternate forms: $L = \lceil \log_2((u - l) \cdot 10^p) \rceil$ where $u$ and $l$ are the upper and lower bounds respectively.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Decoding a Binary Genotype to its Phenotype

**Problem Statement:**
Given an $L$-bit binary chromosome $b = (b_{L-1}, b_{L-2}, \ldots, b_1, b_0)$ and a decoding range $[a, b]$, find the real-valued phenotype $x$.

**Step 1 — Convert the binary string to a decimal integer.**
For a standard binary representation:

$$x_{\text{dec}} = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} b_k \cdot 2^{k}$$

where $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $k$-th bit (with the least significant bit at position $k = 0$).

**Step 2 — Normalize the decimal value into the interval $[a, b]$.**
The decimal value $x_{\text{dec}}$ lies in $[0, 2^{L} - 1]$. Map it linearly to $[a, b]$:

$$x = a + x_{\text{dec}} \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^{L} - 1}$$

This can be expanded as:

$$x = a + \left(\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} b_k \cdot 2^{k}\right) \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^{L} - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Let $L = 5$, $a = 0$, $b = 31$, and the chromosome be $b = 10110_2$.

$$x_{\text{dec}} = 1 \cdot 2^{4} + 0 \cdot 2^{3} + 1 \cdot 2^{2} + 1 \cdot 2^{1} + 0 \cdot 2^{0}$$

$$x_{\text{dec}} = 16 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 22$$

$$x = 0 + 22 \cdot \frac{31 - 0}{2^{5} - 1} = 22 \cdot \frac{31}{31} = 22$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Trick:** When a KTU question says *"decode the binary string 11001 with $L=5$, $a=0$, $b=31$"*, the examiner expects: (1) the decimal conversion, (2) the linear scaling substitution, and (3) the final numerical value. Skipping step (2) costs 1 mark.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Roulette-Wheel Selection Probability

**Problem Statement:**
Given a population with fitness values $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_N$, derive the selection probability $p_i$ for individual $i$ under **fitness-proportionate (roulette-wheel) selection**.

**Step 1 — Define the selection probability.**
A roulette wheel is a pie chart in which each individual occupies a sector of angle proportional to its fitness. The probability of the wheel's pointer landing on individual $i$ is:

$$p_i = \frac{\text{Sector size of } i}{\text{Total wheel area}} = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the probability axiom.**
By construction:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = 1$$

Hence $p_i$ is a valid probability mass function.

**Step 3 — Expected number of copies in the next generation.**
The expected count of individual $i$ in the mating pool of size $N$ is:

$$E[C_i] = N \cdot p_i = N \cdot \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} = \frac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$$

where $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$ is the mean population fitness. This shows that a fitter-than-average individual ($f_i > \bar{f}$) gets more than one expected copy, while a less-fit one gets fewer than one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why roulette-wheel is rarely used in practice:** The selection pressure collapses when a single individual is much fitter than the rest (so-called *premature convergence*). KTU examiners expect students to mention this drawback in long-answer questions.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Holland's Schema Theorem (Foundational Form)

**Problem Statement:**
Derive a lower-bound expression for the expected number of copies of a schema $H$ in generation $t+1$, given its count in generation $t$.

**Step 1 — Define a schema.**
A **schema** $H$ is a template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$ where $*$ is the *don't-care* symbol. For example, $H = 1\!\ast\!0$ matches both $100$ and $110$.

Define:
- $o(H)$ = **order** of $H$ = number of fixed (non-$*$) positions.
- $\delta(H)$ = **defining length** = distance between the first and last fixed positions.

**Step 2 — Effect of selection alone.**
Under fitness-proportionate selection, a schema $H$ grows by a factor of $f(H) / \bar{f}$, where $f(H)$ is the average fitness of strings matching $H$:

$$m(H, t+1)_{\text{sel}} = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of crossover (destruction).**
A schema is destroyed by single-point crossover with probability at most:

$$p_{\text{destroy}} \leq p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Effect of mutation (destruction).**
Each of the $o(H)$ fixed bits survives mutation with probability $(1 - p_m)$. To first order:

$$p_{\text{survive, mut}} \approx 1 - o(H) \cdot p_m$$

**Step 5 — Combine the three effects.**

$$m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$

This is the **Schema Theorem**, often quoted in the form:

> *"Short, low-order, above-average schemata grow exponentially across generations."*

This is the **Building Block Hypothesis**, the theoretical justification for the success of Genetic Algorithms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $\delta(H)/L$ instead of $\delta(H)/(L-1)$ in the crossover term. The denominator is $L-1$ because there are $L-1$ possible crossover points in an $L$-bit string. Examiners specifically test this off-by-one trap.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Term-to-Parameter Mapping

The matrix below maps each EC terminology to its **mathematical symbol**, **typical value range**, and the **equation** in which it appears.

| Terminology | Symbol | Typical Value | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population size | $N$ | 50–200 | All equations |
| Chromosome length | $L$ | 10–100 | Encoding, Schema Theorem |
| Fitness of individual $i$ | $f_i$ | $\geq 0$ (after scaling) | Selection |
| Mean fitness | $\bar{f}$ | derived | Selection, Schema Theorem |
| Crossover probability | $p_c$ | 0.6–0.9 | Crossover, Schema Theorem |
| Mutation probability | $p_m$ | 0.001–0.05 | Mutation, Schema Theorem |
| Selection probability | $p_i$ | $\in [0, 1]$ | Selection |
| Schema order | $o(H)$ | $\in [0, L]$ | Schema Theorem |
| Defining length | $\delta(H)$ | $\in [0, L-1]$ | Schema Theorem |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Evolutionary Computing Cycle

The following Mermaid block visualizes the **canonical EC iteration loop**. Every individual passes through evaluation, selection, variation, and replacement in each generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: t = 0] --> B[Initialize Population P t of N individuals]
    B --> C[Decode genotype to phenotype]
    C --> D[Evaluate fitness f xi for each individual]
    D --> E{Stopping criterion met?}
    E -- No --> F[Selection: choose parents from P t]
    F --> G[Crossover with probability pc]
    G --> H[Mutation with probability pm]
    H --> I[Form offspring population Q t]
    I --> J[Environmental selection: elitism + replacement]
    J --> K[Set P t+1 = selected survivors]
    K --> L[Increment t = t + 1]
    L --> D
    E -- Yes --> M[Output best individual]
```

**How to read this diagram for ESE answers:**

- The **outer loop** is the *generation loop*.
- The **inner pipeline** (decode → evaluate → select → vary → replace) is the *evolutionary step*.
- The **dashed decision** node is the termination check.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Genotype-to-Phenotype Decoding Pipeline

This diagram shows how a raw binary chromosome is mapped to a usable solution for the fitness function.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Genotype: Binary string of L bits] --> B[Convert bits to integer x dec]
    B --> C[Linear scaling into range a to b]
    C --> D[Phenotype: Real value x in domain]
    D --> E[Fitness function f x]
    E --> F[Fitness score]
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **genotype** is what the algorithm stores and manipulates.
- The **phenotype** is what the fitness function consumes.
- The conversion in node C is given by the formula derived in Section 3.2.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hierarchy of EC Concepts

This block-level diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy from the most abstract (algorithm) down to the most concrete (allele value).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Evolutionary Algorithm] --> L1[Population]
    L1 --> L2[Individual]
    L2 --> L3[Chromosome]
    L3 --> L4[Gene position]
    L4 --> L5[Allele value 0 or 1]
    L0 --> L6[Generational Loop]
    L6 --> L7[Selection operator]
    L6 --> L8[Crossover operator]
    L6 --> L9[Mutation operator]
    L6 --> L10[Replacement strategy]
    L10 --> L11[Elitism]
```

**Reading the hierarchy:**

- The **leftmost spine** (L0 → L5) describes the *data structure* of EC.
- The **rightmost spine** (L6 → L11) describes the *operators* of EC.
- Both spines intersect at the **Individual** level (L2), since an individual is acted upon by operators but is also an element of the population.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Comparison of Selection Mechanisms (Terminology Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Selection Mechanisms] --> B[Fitness Proportionate]
    A --> C[Ranking Selection]
    A --> D[Tournament Selection]
    A --> E[Truncation Selection]
    B --> B1[Roulette Wheel]
    B --> B2[Stochastic Universal Sampling]
    C --> C1[Linear Ranking]
    C --> C2[Exponential Ranking]
    D --> D1[Binary Tournament k equals 2]
    D --> D2[k-ary Tournament k greater than 2]
    E --> E1[Top T percent retained]
```

**Note for ESE:** KTU examiners commonly ask students to *"briefly explain any two selection mechanisms."* Tournament selection is the most frequently expected answer because it is the most widely used in practice.

> [!TIP]
> **Why tournament selection is the default in modern EC:** It does not require absolute fitness values (only relative comparisons), it has controllable selection pressure via the tournament size $k$, and it scales gracefully to parallel hardware. These three points are a complete 3-mark answer in KTU ESE.
