## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the following terms used in Evolutionary Computing: (i) Chromosome, (ii) Fitness Function, (iii) Generation.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Chromosome:** A chromosome is the encoded string representation of a candidate solution in an evolutionary algorithm. It is composed of discrete symbols (bits, real numbers, integers, or permutations) drawn from a problem-specific alphabet. The chromosome is the data structure on which genetic operators (crossover, mutation) operate.

(ii) **Fitness Function:** A fitness function $f : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ maps every candidate solution (phenotype) to a non-negative real number quantifying its quality with respect to the optimization objective. The fitness function is the *only* problem-specific component of an EC algorithm and is evaluated on the **phenotype**, not the genotype.

(iii) **Generation:** A generation corresponds to one complete iteration of the evolutionary cycle. At the end of generation $t$, a new population $P(t+1)$ is produced by applying selection, crossover, and mutation to the current population $P(t)$. The transition from $P(t)$ to $P(t+1)$ is, by definition, one generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Part A:** Many students incorrectly say *"fitness function is applied on the genotype."* This is **wrong** in KTU 2024 scheme. The correct answer is **phenotype**. Lose 1 mark for this.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Genotype and Phenotype in Evolutionary Computing with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Genotype | Phenotype |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The encoded form of an individual stored in the chromosome. | The decoded, problem-space expression of the individual. |
| **Data type** | Binary / integer / real / permutation string. | Real number, vector, schedule, or any problem-domain object. |
| **Operator action** | Crossover and mutation operate here. | The fitness function is evaluated here. |
| **Example** | `10110` (binary string). | `x = 22` (real number in $[0, 31]$). |

The mapping from genotype to phenotype is performed by a **decoder** (or *interpretation function*). The same phenotype can arise from multiple genotypes (a phenomenon known as **genetic redundancy**), and the same genotype can have different phenotypic effects in different environments (a phenomenon known as **pleiotropy**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Part A:** Giving only a verbal distinction without the *example* is a half-answer. KTU 2024 Part A model answers require at least one worked example for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Module 3 internal-choice format: **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various terminologies used in Evolutionary Computing with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A continuous variable $x$ is to be encoded as a binary chromosome in the range $[-1, 2]$ with a precision of 4 decimal places. Determine the required chromosome length $L$ and decode the binary string `0101101` to its real value. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The following terminologies form the *core vocabulary* of EC and are essential to understanding any EA (Evolutionary Algorithm):

1. **Individual / Chromosome** — A single candidate solution, encoded as a string of symbols.
2. **Gene** — A single position within the chromosome.
3. **Allele** — The value taken by a gene (e.g., 0 or 1 for binary encoding).
4. **Population** — A set of $N$ individuals maintained simultaneously.
5. **Generation** — One iteration of the EA loop producing a new population.
6. **Fitness** — A scalar quality score assigned to an individual via the fitness function.
7. **Selection** — A probabilistic operator that favors fitter individuals as parents.
8. **Crossover (Recombination)** — An operator that combines two parents to produce offspring.
9. **Mutation** — A small random perturbation applied to a chromosome.
10. **Elitism** — The deterministic copying of the best $k$ individuals into the next generation.
11. **Convergence** — The state where the population stabilizes and the best fitness stops improving.
12. **Search Space vs. Solution Space** — Search space is the space of all possible genotypes; solution space is the space of all phenotypes.

A neat block diagram representing the EA cycle has been provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes; the student should reproduce an equivalent flowchart in the answer booklet, clearly labeling:

- the **input** (initial population),
- the **evolutionary operators** (selection → crossover → mutation),
- the **replacement step** (forming $P(t+1)$),
- the **termination check**, and
- the **output** (best individual found).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the chromosome length $L$.**

Given range $[a, b] = [-1, 2]$ and precision $p = 4$ decimal places.

$$L = \left\lceil \log_2\!\left((b - a) \cdot 10^{p}\right) \right\rceil$$

$$L = \left\lceil \log_2\!\left((2 - (-1)) \cdot 10^{4}\right) \right\rceil$$

$$L = \left\lceil \log_2(3 \cdot 10^{4}) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_2(30000) \right\rceil$$

Computing the logarithm:

$$\log_2(30000) = \frac{\ln(30000)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{10.309}{0.6931} \approx 14.873$$

$$L = \lceil 14.873 \rceil = 15 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Decode the binary string `0101101`.**

Note: The given string has 7 bits, which is fewer than the required 15. For pedagogical clarity we decode it as if the actual chromosome length $L = 7$ (a common textbook simplification). For the full-precision case the same procedure is applied with $L = 15$.

Convert the 7-bit string to decimal. Reading left-to-right as the most-to-least significant bit:

$$x_{\text{dec}} = 0 \cdot 2^{6} + 1 \cdot 2^{5} + 0 \cdot 2^{4} + 1 \cdot 2^{3} + 1 \cdot 2^{2} + 0 \cdot 2^{1} + 1 \cdot 2^{0}$$

$$x_{\text{dec}} = 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 45$$

Apply the linear-scaling decoding formula:

$$x = a + x_{\text{dec}} \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^{L} - 1}$$

$$x = -1 + 45 \cdot \frac{2 - (-1)}{2^{7} - 1} = -1 + 45 \cdot \frac{3}{127}$$

$$x = -1 + \frac{135}{127} = -1 + 1.0630 = 0.0630$$

So the real-valued phenotype is:

$$x \approx 0.0630$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Part B (b):** Students frequently make two errors here:
> 1. Forgetting the **ceiling** in $L = \lceil \log_2(\cdot) \rceil$, leading to under-allocation of bits and precision loss.
> 2. Using $2^{L}$ instead of $2^{L} - 1$ in the decoder denominator. Always use $2^{L} - 1$ to map the *inclusive* range $[0, 2^{L}-1]$ into the *inclusive* range $[a, b]$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the following EC operators: (i) Single-point Crossover, (ii) Bit-flip Mutation, (iii) Roulette-wheel Selection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Genetic Algorithm uses a population of $N = 6$ individuals with fitness values $f = \{10, 25, 15, 30, 5, 20\}$. Compute the fitness-proportionate selection probability for each individual and the expected number of copies in the next generation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**(i) Single-Point Crossover:**
Two parent chromosomes of length $L$ are aligned, a single crossover point $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L - 1\}$ is chosen uniformly at random, and the genetic material beyond $k$ is exchanged.

**Example:**
Let $L = 6$, parents be $P_1 = 1\,0\,1\,0\,1\,0$ and $P_2 = 0\,1\,0\,1\,0\,1$, and let $k = 3$.

$$P_1: \underbrace{1\,0\,1}_{\text{kept}} \vert \underbrace{0\,1\,0}_{\text{swapped}}$$

$$P_2: \underbrace{0\,1\,0}_{\text{kept}} \vert \underbrace{1\,0\,1}_{\text{swapped}}$$

Offspring: $C_1 = 1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,1$ and $C_2 = 0\,1\,0\,0\,1\,0$.

**(ii) Bit-Flip Mutation:**
Each bit of an offspring chromosome is independently flipped (i.e., $0 \rightarrow 1$ or $1 \rightarrow 0$) with a small probability $p_m$.

**Example:**
Let $p_m = 0.2$ and the offspring be $C_1 = 1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,1$. Flipping the third and sixth bits (chosen randomly) gives $C_1' = 1\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0$.

**(iii) Roulette-Wheel Selection:**
Each individual is assigned a sector on a "wheel" whose angular span is proportional to its fitness. Spinning the wheel $N$ times yields a mating pool in which fitter individuals are over-represented. The selection probability of individual $i$ is $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.

**Example:**
For fitness values $\{10, 20, 30\}$, the wheel sectors are $120^{\circ}$, $240^{\circ}$, and $360^{\circ}$ — total $720^{\circ}$, normalized to $1/6$, $1/3$, $1/2$ respectively.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the total fitness.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i = 10 + 25 + 15 + 30 + 5 + 20 = 105$$

**Step 2 — Compute each selection probability $p_i = f_i / 105$.**

| Individual $i$ | Fitness $f_i$ | Probability $p_i$ | Expected copies $E[C_i] = N \cdot p_i = 6 p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $10/105 \approx 0.0952$ | $0.571$ |
| 2 | 25 | $25/105 \approx 0.2381$ | $1.429$ |
| 3 | 15 | $15/105 \approx 0.1429$ | $0.857$ |
| 4 | 30 | $30/105 \approx 0.2857$ | $1.714$ |
| 5 | 5 | $5/105 \approx 0.0476$ | $0.286$ |
| 6 | 20 | $20/105 \approx 0.1905$ | $1.143$ |
| **Sum** | **105** | **1.0000** | **6.000** |

**Step 3 — Verify the expected number of copies.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} E[C_i] = 0.571 + 1.429 + 0.857 + 1.714 + 0.286 + 1.143 = 6.000 \checkmark$$

This confirms the calculation. Note that individual 4 (the fittest, $f_4 = 30$) has the highest expected copy count ($E[C_4] \approx 1.714$), while individual 5 (the least fit, $f_5 = 5$) has the lowest ($E[C_5] \approx 0.286$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Part B (b):** Many students compute the selection probabilities correctly but forget to *also* compute the expected number of copies. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests both — losing 2 marks for omitting the second part.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 3

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes That Cost KTU Students Marks in EC Terminology Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing schema order $o(H)$ with defining length $\delta(H)$.** Order counts fixed positions; defining length measures the *span* between the first and last fixed positions.
> 2. **Forgetting the minus 1 in the decoder denominator.** Use $2^{L} - 1$, not $2^{L}$.
> 3. **Stating the schema theorem without the schema theorem's pre-conditions.** The theorem is valid only for *fitness-proportionate selection* and *single-point crossover*. Examiners deduct marks if these qualifiers are absent.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** A chromosome length is dimensionless; the precision is in decimal places; the fitness is in objective-function units. Always state them.
> 5. **Mislabelling genotype and phenotype.** The fitness function is applied to the **phenotype** (decoded real value), not the **genotype** (raw bit string).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Terminologies of Evolutionary Computing**
>
> - **EC** is a population-based, stochastic, gradient-free optimization technique.
> - **Individual / Chromosome** is the encoded candidate solution; **gene** is one position; **allele** is the value at a position.
> - **Population** has size $N$ (typical 50–200); **generation** is one full iteration.
> - **Genotype** = raw encoded string; **phenotype** = decoded real-world solution. Fitness is computed on the **phenotype**.
> - **Encoding length** for range $[a, b]$ at $p$ decimal places is $L = \lceil \log_2((b - a) \cdot 10^p) \rceil$.
> - **Decoding** uses the linear map $x = a + x_{\text{dec}} \cdot (b - a) / (2^{L} - 1)$.
> - **Roulette-wheel selection probability** is $p_i = f_i / \sum f_j$. Expected copies $= N \cdot p_i = f_i / \bar{f}$.
> - **Crossover** mixes parents with probability $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$; **mutation** perturbs with probability $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$.
> - **Schema theorem**: $m(H, t+1) \geq m(H, t) \cdot (f(H)/\bar{f}) \cdot [1 - p_c \delta(H)/(L-1) - p_m o(H)]$.
> - **Schema order** $o(H)$ = number of fixed bits; **defining length** $\delta(H)$ = distance between first and last fixed bits.
> - **Elitism** preserves the best $k$ individuals; it guarantees monotone non-decreasing best-fitness across generations.
> - **Convergence** is reached when the population diversity $\rightarrow 0$ or when the best fitness plateau is detected.
> - **Search space** = space of genotypes; **solution space** = space of phenotypes.
> - **Real-world KTU 2024 applications**: NN weight training, TSP, scheduling, VLSI partitioning, PID tuning.
> - **Standard KTU examiner answer** to *"Why EC?"*: *EC is gradient-free and hence suited to non-differentiable, non-convex, black-box optimization.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Genetic Algorithm, Operators in Genetic Algorithm - Coding, Selection, Crossover, Mutation, Stopping Condition for Genetic Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Genetic Algorithm, Operators in Genetic Algorithm - Coding, Selection, Crossover, Mutation, Stopping Condition for Genetic Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a class of **adaptive stochastic search and optimization techniques** based on the mechanics of **natural genetics and Darwinian evolution**. Formally introduced by **John Holland (1975)** and rigorously formalized by **David Goldberg (1989)**, a GA maintains a population of candidate solutions encoded as chromosome-like structures, and evolves them toward better solutions through iterative application of biologically inspired operators: **selection, crossover, and mutation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST417 – Module 3):** *Genetic Algorithms (GAs) are search algorithms based on the mechanics of natural selection and natural genetics. They combine survival of the fittest among string structures with a structured yet randomized information exchange to form a search algorithm with some innovative flair of human search.*

**Mathematical Formulation (General GA Search Problem):**

$$\text{Given } f : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad \text{find } \mathbf{x}^* \in \mathcal{S} \text{ such that } f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} f(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the solution space, $f(\cdot)$ is the **objective (fitness) function**, and $\mathbf{x}^*$ is the **globally optimal solution**.

### 1.2 Biological-to-Computational Terminology Mapping

| Biological Term | Computational Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Chromosome | Solution (encoded string) |
| Gene | Decision variable / parameter |
| Allele | Bit value or symbol |
| Population | Set of candidate solutions |
| Fitness | Objective function value |
| Reproduction | Selection operator |
| Recombination | Crossover operator |
| Mutation | Mutation operator |
| Generation | Iteration cycle |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Evolving Robot Army"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are an engineer trying to design the **best-performing robot** for a race. You start with **100 random designs** (population). You **race all robots** (evaluate fitness) and only allow the **fastest 20** to "reproduce" (selection). You then **mix the design blueprints** of two parents to create a child (crossover), and occasionally **randomly flip a screw position** (mutation). Over many generations, your robot army becomes progressively faster — this is exactly how a Genetic Algorithm works, except "designs" are encoded numbers and "racing" is mathematical evaluation.

### 1.4 Why Genetic Algorithms? (Key Advantages)

- **Global Search Capability:** Maintains a *population* of solutions → escapes local optima (unlike gradient descent or hill climbing).
- **Derivative-Free:** Requires **only the objective function value**, not gradients or differentiability.
- **Parallel by Nature:** Population can be evaluated independently and concurrently.
- **Robust & Noise-Tolerant:** Performs well on noisy, discontinuous, multimodal, and non-convex landscapes.
- **Domain Independence:** Once encoded, the same GA framework solves diverse problems (TSP, scheduling, neural net training, feature selection, antenna design).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** GAs are **not** guaranteed to find the *optimal* solution; they are **heuristic** algorithms that find *good-enough* solutions within acceptable time budgets. This is a 2-mark exam favorite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Canonical Genetic Algorithm Flow

A standard GA executes the following **five-stage iterative cycle** until a stopping criterion is met:

1. **Initialization** — Generate an initial population $P(0)$ of $N$ chromosomes (randomly or heuristically).
2. **Fitness Evaluation** — Compute $f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ for every individual $\mathbf{x}_i \in P(t)$.
3. **Selection** — Choose parents for mating pool, biased toward higher fitness.
4. **Genetic Operators** — Apply **Crossover** (recombination) and **Mutation** to produce offspring.
5. **Replacement** — Form new generation $P(t+1)$ from parents + offspring (generational) or by replacing weak parents (steady-state).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown of Each Operator

#### (A) Coding / Encoding (Representation)

Encoding defines **how solutions are represented** in chromosome form. The choice critically impacts GA performance.

**Common Encoding Schemes:**

| Encoding Type | Chromosome Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Encoding** | `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0` | Classic GA, discrete optimization |
| **Real-Valued (Floating-Point)** | `[3.14, 2.71, 1.618]` | Continuous optimization |
| **Integer / Permutation** | `[5, 2, 8, 1, 4]` | TSP, scheduling, ordering |
| **Tree-Based (GP)** | `(+ (* x y) (- a b))` | Genetic Programming, symbolic regression |
| **Gray Code** | `1 1 0 1 0 1 0 0` | Mitigates Hamming cliff in binary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gray Code Advantage:** Consecutive integers differ in **only 1 bit**, eliminating the "Hamming cliff" problem of standard binary (e.g., 0111 → 1000 changes 4 bits). This is a frequent KTU exam point.

**Binary ↔ Decimal Decoding Formula:**

For a binary string $b_{L-1}\,b_{L-2}\ldots b_1\,b_0$ of length $L$ representing a variable in range $[a, b]$:

$$x = a + \frac{(b - a)}{2^L - 1} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_i \cdot 2^{i}$$

**Resolution (precision) per variable:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1}$$

#### (B) Selection Operator

Selection implements the **"survival of the fittest"** principle — fitter individuals receive higher probability of being chosen as parents.

**Major Selection Methods — Theory Snapshot:**

| Method | Selection Probability $P_i$ | Bias | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roulette Wheel (FPS)** | $P_i = f_i / \sum_j f_j$ | High | Simple but variance issue for low $f$ |
| **Rank-Based** | Based on rank position | Medium | Scale-invariant |
| **Tournament (size $k$)** | Win $k$ random pairings | Controllable | Most widely used; $k=2$ standard |
| **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** | Equispaced pointers | Low | Zero bias, single spin |
| **Truncation** | Top $T\%$ only | Very High | Aggressive, used in Evolution Strategies |
| **Boltzmann** | $P_i \propto e^{f_i/T}$ | Adaptive | Simulated annealing schedule |

**Roulette Wheel Derivation:**

For population $\{ \mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N \}$ with fitness $\{ f_1, \ldots, f_N \}$, the probability of selecting individual $i$ is:

$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

**Expected Count** of individual $i$ in next generation:

$$E_i = N \cdot P_i = \frac{N \cdot f_i}{\bar{f}}, \quad \text{where } \bar{f} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$$

#### (C) Crossover Operator (Recombination)

Crossover combines **two parent chromosomes** to produce offspring, exploiting promising **building blocks** (schemas).

**Crossover Types — Comprehensive Matrix:**

| Crossover Type | Mechanism | Diagram Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Point** | Cut at one point, swap tails | `A: 1 1 1 \| 0 0 0`  →  `C1: 1 1 1 \| 1 1 1` |
| **Two-Point** | Cut at 2 points, swap middle | `A: 1 1 \| 0 0 \| 1`  →  swap center |
| **Multi-Point (Uniform)** | Each gene independently chosen from parent A or B | Per-gene random |
| **Arithmetic** | $C_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$ | Used for real-valued GA |
| **Order Crossover (OX)** | Preserves relative order | TSP applications |
| **Partially Mapped (PMX)** | Preserves absolute positions | TSP, permutations |
| **Cycle Crossover (CX)** | Preserves cycles between parents | Permutation problems |
| **SBX (Simulated Binary)** | Mimics single-point in real domain | Continuous optimization |

**Crossover Probability $P_c$:** Typically $0.6 \leq P_c \leq 0.95$. If no crossover, parents pass unchanged.

#### (D) Mutation Operator

Mutation introduces **small random changes** to genes, maintaining **genetic diversity** and preventing premature convergence to local optima.

**Mutation Mechanisms by Encoding:**

| Encoding | Mutation Operator | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Binary | **Bit Flip** | $0 \rightarrow 1$ or $1 \rightarrow 0$ |
| Real-valued | **Gaussian / Uniform Perturbation** | $x' = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma)$ |
| Integer | **Random Reset** | Replace with random value in domain |
| Permutation | **Swap / Insert / Scramble / Inversion** | TSP-style mutations |

**Mutation Probability $P_m$:** Typically $0.001 \leq P_m \leq 0.1$ (much smaller than $P_c$).

**Bit-Flip Mutation Expected Count** for a chromosome of length $L$:

$$E[\text{flips}] = L \cdot P_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossover vs. Mutation (3-Mark KTU Favorite):**
> - **Crossover** → *exploitation* (refines existing good solutions)
> - **Mutation** → *exploration* (introduces new genetic material, escapes local optima)
> - Both are **necessary**; remove either and performance degrades.

#### (E) Stopping Condition (Termination Criterion)

A GA is an **iterative algorithm** — it requires a defined termination condition. Common criteria:

| Stopping Criterion | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Generations** | Run for $T$ predefined iterations | Benchmarking, fixed budget |
| **Convergence Threshold** | Stop when $\geq X\%$ of population has identical fitness | Detects stagnation |
| **No Improvement** | Stop if best fitness unchanged for $G$ generations | Practical convergence |
| **Target Fitness** | Stop when $f(\mathbf{x}_{best}) \geq f_{target}$ | Known optima / goal |
| **Time / Resource Budget** | Stop after CPU seconds / function evaluations | Real-world deployment |
| **Diversity Collapse** | Stop if population diversity $<$ threshold | Avoid premature convergence |
| **Saturation of Fitness Variance** | $\sigma^2(f) < \epsilon$ | Statistical detection |

**Combined KTU-Recommended Criterion:**

$$\text{STOP if } \left[ t \geq T_{max} \right] \;\lor\; \left[ |f_{best}(t) - f_{best}(t-k)| \leq \epsilon \text{ for } k \text{ generations} \right] \;\lor\; \left[ f_{best}(t) \geq f^* \right]$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Fitness Probability (FPS) | $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$ | Selection probability |
| Expected count | $E_i = N \cdot f_i / \bar{f}$ | Times individual selected |
| Binary decoding | $x = a + \frac{b-a}{2^L-1} \cdot \sum b_i 2^i$ | Variable value |
| Resolution | $\Delta x = (b-a)/(2^L-1)$ | Precision per bit |
| Bit-flip expectation | $E[\text{flips}] = L P_m$ | Mutations per chromosome |
| Arithmetic crossover | $C_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1-\alpha) P_2$ | Real-valued blend |
| Gaussian mutation | $x' = x + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Real-valued perturbation |
| Schema order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions |
| Schema defining length | $\delta(H)$ | Distance between first and last fixed |
| Schema fitness | $f(H, t)$ | Mean fitness of instances |
| Schema Theorem | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H,t)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - P_c \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - P_m \cdot o(H) \right]$ | Growth of short, low-order, high-fitness schemas |

> [!WARNING]
> **Schema Theorem Caveat:** Strictly applies only to **proportional selection + single-point crossover + bit-flip mutation**. Other operators (tournament, uniform crossover) require modified analysis. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing shape optimization (Airbus A380).
- **Robotics:** Evolution of walking gaits for legged robots.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction, DNA sequence alignment.
- **VLSI Design:** Circuit partitioning, FPGA routing.
- **Finance:** Portfolio optimization, algorithmic trading rule discovery.
- **Telecommunications:** Antenna design (NASA ST5 mission), network routing.
- **Machine Learning:** Feature selection, hyperparameter tuning, neural architecture search (NAS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Complete GA Cycle on a Toy Problem

**Problem:** Maximize $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0, 31]$, using 5-bit binary chromosomes, population size $N = 4$, single-point crossover at $P_c = 1.0$, bit-flip mutation at $P_m = 0.01$, roulette wheel selection.

**Step 1 — Initial Population (random generation):**

| Chromosome | Binary | Decimal $x$ | Fitness $f(x) = x^2$ | Probability $P_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | `01101` | 13 | 169 | 0.146 |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | `11000` | 24 | 576 | 0.496 |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | `01000` | 8 | 64 | 0.055 |
| $\mathbf{x}_4$ | `10011` | 19 | 361 | 0.303 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **1170** | **1.000** |

**Step 2 — Compute Expected Counts (using $E_i = N \cdot f_i / \bar{f}$):**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{1170}{4} = 292.5$$

$$E_1 = \frac{4 \cdot 169}{292.5} = 2.31, \quad E_2 = \frac{4 \cdot 576}{292.5} = 7.88, \quad E_3 = \frac{4 \cdot 64}{292.5} = 0.88, \quad E_4 = \frac{4 \cdot 361}{292.5} = 4.94$$

**Step 3 — Roulette Wheel Selection (assume 2 spins select):**

| Spin Range $[0, \text{cum.})$ | Individual |
|---|---|
| $[0, 0.146)$ | $\mathbf{x}_1$ |
| $[0.146, 0.642)$ | $\mathbf{x}_2$ |
| $[0.642, 0.697)$ | $\mathbf{x}_3$ |
| $[0.697, 1.000)$ | $\mathbf{x}_4$ |

Assume random spins yield parents: $P_1 = \mathbf{x}_2 = \texttt{11000}$, $P_2 = \mathbf{x}_4 = \texttt{10011}$.

**Step 4 — Single-Point Crossover (cut at position 2):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &: \underbrace{11}_{\text{head}}\mid\underbrace{000}_{\text{tail}} \\
P_2 &: \underbrace{10}_{\text{head}}\mid\underbrace{011}_{\text{tail}}
\end{aligned}$$

Offspring after swap:

$$C_1 = \texttt{11} \mid \texttt{011} = \texttt{11011} \;(= 27, \; f = 729)$$
$$C_2 = \texttt{10} \mid \texttt{000} = \texttt{10000} \;(= 16, \; f = 256)$$

**Step 5 — Bit-Flip Mutation ($P_m = 0.01$, so 2-bit and 1-bit mutation probability):**

Assume no bits flip (probable for this small $P_m$). New population after generation 1:

| Chromosome | $x$ | $f(x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| `11011` | 27 | **729** ← new best |
| `10000` | 16 | 256 |
| `11000` | 24 | 576 |
| `10011` | 19 | 361 |

**Observation:** Best fitness jumped from **576 → 729** in one generation. Repeating over many generations drives population toward $x = 31$, $f = 961$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** In Part B questions, **always show the fitness table, probability table, and at least one full crossover-mutation cycle** to score full marks. A numerical worked example is the surest way to earn a 7.

### 3.2 Schema Theorem — Full Derivation

A **schema** $H$ is a template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ is the "don't care" symbol. The schema $H = 1*0*1$ matches strings like `10011`, `11011`, etc.

**Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975):** The expected number of schema $H$ in the next generation is bounded by:

$$\begin{aligned}
E[m(H, t+1)] &\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - P_c \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - P_m \cdot o(H) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step A — Selection effect (Roulette Wheel):**

Under proportional selection, the expected number of copies of $H$ is:

$$m(H, t+1)_{\text{select}} = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}}$$

because an instance of $H$ has probability $f(H,t)/\bar{f}$ of being selected at each spin.

**Step B — Crossover disruption:**

A schema of defining length $\delta(H)$ is destroyed by single-point crossover only if the cut point falls **within** the defining length. With cut uniform over $L-1$ possible positions:

$$P_{\text{disrupt, cross}} = \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1}$$

**Step C — Mutation disruption:**

A schema survives mutation only if **all $o(H)$ fixed positions** remain unchanged. With bit-flip probability $P_m$:

$$P_{\text{survive, mut}} = (1 - P_m)^{o(H)} \geq 1 - P_m \cdot o(H) \quad (\text{for small } P_m)$$

**Step D — Combine all effects (assuming independence):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E[m(H, t+1)] &\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}} \cdot (1 - P_c P_{\text{disrupt}}) \cdot (1 - P_m \cdot o(H)) \\
&\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - P_c \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - P_m \cdot o(H)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation (Building Block Hypothesis):**

Schemas with **short defining length, low order, and above-average fitness** grow exponentially — these are called **building blocks**. GAs implicitly combine and propagate building blocks to construct high-quality solutions.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Schema Counting and Fitness

For string `1 0 1 1 0` of length 5, identify all **order-2 schemas** and compute schema fitness if the population contains strings matching them with average fitness values.

**All order-2 schemas (one fixed, one fixed — e.g., positions 1 and 4):**

| Schema | Fixed Positions | Order $o(H)$ | Defining Length $\delta(H)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `1****` | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| `*0***` | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| `*1***` | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| `1*0**` | 1, 3 | 2 | 2 |
| `*011*` | 2, 3, 4 | 3 | 2 |
| `**1*0` | 3, 5 | 2 | 2 |

This hands-on calculation is what KTU examiners expect for the 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Genetic Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START]) --> initP[Initialize Population P of N chromosomes randomly]
    initP --> evalFit[Evaluate Fitness f xi for all individuals]
    evalFit --> checkStop{Stopping Condition Met?}
    checkStop -- NO --> selOp[Selection Operator]
    selOp --> crossOp[Crossover Operator with Probability Pc]
    crossOp --> mutOp[Mutation Operator with Probability Pm]
    mutOp --> replaceOp[Replacement to form P of t+1]
    replaceOp --> evalFit
    checkStop -- YES --> outBest[Output Best Solution x star and f x star]
    outBest --> stopA([STOP])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of GA Operators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POP[Population Pool]
        ind1[Chromosome 1]
        ind2[Chromosome 2]
        ind3[Chromosome N]
    end

    subgraph ENC[Encoding Module]
        binEnc[Binary Encoder]
        realEnc[Real Valued Encoder]
        permEnc[Permutation Encoder]
    end

    subgraph FIT[Fitness Evaluation]
        fitFunc[Objective Function f x]
        fitVal[Fitness Score]
    end

    subgraph SEL[Selection Module]
        rwSel[Roulette Wheel]
        tnSel[Tournament Selection]
        rkSel[Rank Based]
    end

    subgraph GEN[Genetic Operators]
        cross[Crossover]
        mut[Mutation]
    end

    subgraph OFF[Offspring Generation]
        off1[Child Chromosomes]
    end

    POP --> ENC
    ENC --> FIT
    FIT --> SEL
    SEL --> GEN
    GEN --> OFF
    OFF --> FIT
```

### 4.3 Crossover Operator Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start Crossover]) --> pcCheck{Probability Pc satisfied?}
    pcCheck -- NO --> noCross[Pass Parents Unchanged]
    pcCheck -- YES --> typeChoose{Crossover Type}
    typeChoose -- Single Point --> sp[Cut at 1 random point swap tails]
    typeChoose -- Two Point --> tp[Cut at 2 points swap middle segment]
    typeChoose -- Uniform --> uni[For each gene pick randomly from parent A or B]
    typeChoose -- Arithmetic --> arith[Compute C1 = alpha times P1 plus 1 minus alpha times P2]
    typeChoose -- Order OX --> ox[Copy substring from P1 fill rest in P2 order]
    sp --> produceKids[Produce 2 offspring]
    tp --> produceKids
    uni --> produceKids
    arith --> produceKids
    ox --> produceKids
    noCross --> outC([Return Parents])
    produceKids --> mutStage[Proceed to Mutation]
```

### 4.4 Mutation Operator Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startM([Start Mutation]) --> loopGene[For each gene in each offspring]
    loopGene --> randCheck{Random r less than Pm?}
    randCheck -- NO --> keepGene[Gene unchanged]
    randCheck -- YES --> encCheck{Encoding Type}
    encCheck -- Binary --> flip[Flip bit 0 to 1 or 1 to 0]
    encCheck -- Real Valued --> gauss[Perturb by Gaussian noise N 0 sigma]
    encCheck -- Permutation --> swap[Swap two random positions]
    flip --> nextGene[Next gene]
    gauss --> nextGene
    swap --> nextGene
    keepGene --> nextGene
    nextGene --> endCheck{All genes processed?}
    endCheck -- NO --> loopGene
    endCheck -- YES --> outM([Return Mutated Offspring])
```

### 4.5 Selection Pressure and Stopping Trade-Off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Selection Pressure] --> B[High Diversity]
    A --> C[Slow Convergence]
    D[High Selection Pressure] --> E[Fast Convergence]
    D --> F[Premature Convergence Risk]
    B --> G[Continue GA Iteration]
    C --> G
    E --> H[Check Stopping Condition]
    F --> H
    G --> I[More Generations Needed]
    H --> J{Stopping Condition Met?}
    J -- YES --> K[Output Best Solution]
    J -- NO --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a process; arrows represent control/data flow. These mirror how examiners expect students to draw flowcharts in the 14-mark Part B questions. Hand-drawing the *same* structure with labeled boxes is worth full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`**  
**Define fitness function. Why is fitness scaling often required in Genetic Algorithms? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **fitness function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a problem-specific objective function that quantitatively evaluates how "good" a candidate solution $\mathbf{x}$ is. It drives the **selection operator** — fitter chromosomes receive higher probability of reproduction.

**Why fitness scaling is needed:**

1. **Early generations:** Raw fitness values may have low variance → near-uniform selection → slow progress.
2. **Late generations:** Population clusters around optima → fitness values become very close → weak selection pressure → stagnation.

**Common scaling schemes:**

| Scheme | Formula |
|---|---|
| Linear Scaling | $f' = a \cdot f + b$ |
| Sigma Truncation | $f' = f - (\bar{f} - c \cdot \sigma_f)$ |
| Power Law | $f' = f^k$ |
| Boltzmann | $f' = e^{f/T}$ |

$(3/3 \text{ marks expected})$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`**  
**Differentiate between Crossover and Mutation operators in GA. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Crossover | Mutation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Exploitation — combines existing good genes | Exploration — introduces new genetic material |
| **Operates on** | 2 (or more) parents | 1 individual |
| **Probability** | High: $P_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$ | Low: $P_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ |
| **Effect on schema** | Disrupts long schemas | Disrupts low-order fixed positions |
| **Biology analogy** | Sexual reproduction | Random genetic drift |
| **Without it** | No new combinations | Premature convergence to local optima |

Both operators are **complementary and essential**.

$(3/3 \text{ marks expected})$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

**Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) Explain the different selection methods in Genetic Algorithms with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Roulette Wheel Selection (Fitness Proportional Selection) — 2 marks**

$$P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$$

The population is mapped onto a "roulette wheel" where each slice size ∝ fitness. A single spin selects one individual. Simple, but exhibits **high selection variance** and fails when fitness values are very small or negative.

**Example (4 individuals with fitness 2, 4, 1, 3; sum = 10):**

| Individual | Fitness | Probability |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 0.20 |
| B | 4 | 0.40 |
| C | 1 | 0.10 |
| D | 3 | 0.30 |

**2. Rank-Based Selection — 1.5 marks**

Individuals are sorted by fitness; selection probability is **proportional to rank** rather than absolute fitness. Eliminates scaling issues. Linear ranking:

$$P_i = \frac{1}{N}\left[ \eta^{+} - (\eta^{+} + \eta^{-}) \cdot \frac{i - 1}{N - 1} \right]$$

where $i$ is rank, $\eta^+ \in [1, 2]$, $\eta^- = 2 - \eta^+$.

**3. Tournament Selection (most widely used) — 2 marks**

Pick $k$ individuals uniformly at random; the one with the highest fitness wins the tournament. Probability of selection depends on tournament size $k$ and rank.

For $k = 2$, probability of individual of rank $i$ winning:

$$P_i = \frac{N - i + 1}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{N - 1} + \left(\frac{i - 1}{N}\right) \cdot P_i$$

**4. Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS) — 1.5 marks**

A single spin uses **$N$ equally-spaced pointers** on the wheel. Guarantees $\lfloor N \cdot P_i \rfloor$ or $\lceil N \cdot P_i \rceil$ copies of individual $i$ — **zero bias**, low stochastic noise.

> [!TIP]
> Mention **Truncation Selection** (top $T\%$, typically $T = 50$) and **Boltzmann Selection** (selection pressure annealed via temperature $T$) for a complete 7-mark answer.

---

**(b) With neat diagrams, discuss the various crossover techniques used in Genetic Algorithms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Single-Point Crossover — 1.5 marks**

Random cut point; tails exchanged. Disrupts schemas with $\delta(H)$ crossing the cut.

```
Parent 1:  1 1 0 1 | 0 0 1
Parent 2:  0 1 1 0 | 1 1 0
                  ↓
Child  1:  1 1 0 1 | 1 1 0
Child  2:  0 1 1 0 | 0 0 1
```

**2. Two-Point Crossover — 1.5 marks**

Two cut points; middle segment swapped. Lower schema disruption probability.

**3. Uniform Crossover — 1.5 marks**

Each gene independently chosen from parent A (with prob 0.5) or B. Maximum mixing.

**4. Arithmetic (Linear) Crossover — 1.5 marks**

For real-valued chromosomes:

$$C_1 = \alpha P_1 + (1 - \alpha) P_2, \quad C_2 = (1 - \alpha) P_1 + \alpha P_2$$

with $\alpha \in [0, 1]$, often $\alpha = 0.5$.

**5. Order Crossover (OX) and PMX for Permutation — 1 mark**

Used for TSP-class problems; preserves relative order or absolute positions.

> [!TIP]
> A 7-mark answer must include **at least 4 crossover types with ASCII diagrams**. Examiners reward visual clarity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`**

**(a) Explain the encoding/coding schemes used in Genetic Algorithms with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Binary Encoding — 2 marks**

Most classical. Each variable encoded in $L$ bits. Example: $x \in [0, 31]$ in 5 bits.

| Bit Length $L$ | Range Size | Resolution $\Delta x$ |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 32 | 1.0 |
| 10 | 1024 | 0.031 |
| 20 | ~1M | $3 \times 10^{-5}$ |

**2. Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Encoding — 2 marks**

Chromosome is a vector of real numbers: `[3.14, -2.71, 1.618]`. No decoding overhead. **Essential for continuous optimization.** Requires specialized crossover/mutation (SBX, Gaussian).

**3. Permutation / Integer Encoding — 1.5 marks**

Chromosome is a permutation: `[3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]`. Standard for TSP, scheduling. Requires special operators (OX, PMX, CX) to maintain feasibility.

**4. Gray Code Encoding — 1.5 marks**

Adjacent integers differ by **1 bit** only. Decoding rule: $g_i = b_i \oplus b_{i-1}$ where $\oplus$ is XOR.

| Decimal | Binary | Gray |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 011 | 010 |
| 6 | 100 | 101 |
| 7 | 101 | 111 |

Eliminates Hamming cliffs; improves GA performance on numerical optimization.

> [!TIP]
> Mention **Tree-based encoding (Genetic Programming)** for the final 0.5 mark to show breadth of understanding.

---

**(b) Explain the mutation operator and various stopping conditions used in Genetic Algorithms. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Mutation Operator (3.5 marks):**

Mutation introduces **small random perturbations** to maintain population diversity.

**Mechanisms by encoding:**

| Encoding | Mutation | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Binary | **Bit-Flip** | $0 \leftrightarrow 1$ |
| Real | **Gaussian / Uniform** | $x' = x + \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma)$ |
| Permutation | **Swap, Insert, Invert, Scramble** | Maintains feasibility |

**Properties:**

- Mutation probability $P_m$ is kept **low** ($0.001$–$0.1$).
- For binary, **expected mutations per chromosome** = $L \cdot P_m$.
- Mutation is the **only source of new genetic material not present in initial population** — without it, GA cannot explore outside the schema space defined by initial population.

**Adaptive mutation:** $P_m$ decreases over generations to shift from exploration (early) to exploitation (late).

$$P_m(t) = P_{m,0} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{T_{max}}\right)$$

**Part 2 — Stopping Conditions (3.5 marks):**

1. **Fixed Number of Generations** — Simplest; $T_{max}$ predefined.
2. **Fitness Convergence** — Stop when best fitness does not improve for $G$ consecutive generations:

$$f_{best}(t) = f_{best}(t-1) = \ldots = f_{best}(t-G) \Rightarrow \text{STOP}$$

3. **Population Convergence** — Stop when $\geq 90\%$ of population is identical.
4. **Target Fitness Reached** — $f_{best}(t) \geq f^*$ → STOP.
5. **Computational Budget** — Time / CPU / function evaluation limit reached.
6. **Diversity-Based** — Stop if Shannon diversity index $<$ threshold.
7. **Statistical Saturation** — Variance of fitness across population $\sigma^2(f) < \epsilon$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often state "stop after $N$ generations" as the *only* criterion. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for not listing **at least 3 alternative stopping conditions with brief justification**. A good 7-mark answer must include the *adaptive/conditional* logic, not just a single rule.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on GA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing selection with replacement** — Selection chooses parents; replacement decides who survives into the next generation. These are **separate stages**.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $P_i$ and $\bar{f}$** — Any numerical GA question requires the **fitness probability table** as the first table; omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing $f(x) = x$ for maximization** without stating whether the problem is max or min — KTU examiners demand explicit declaration.
> 4. **Crossover with wrong cut convention** — Indexing matters: "cut at position 2" can mean "after gene 2" or "between gene 2 and 3." Always label.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply mutation probability to each gene independently** — students often apply one global coin flip to the whole chromosome, which is **incorrect** — $P_m$ is **per-gene**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Concepts of Genetic Algorithm: Key Takeaways**

- **GA** = population-based stochastic search, inspired by Darwinian evolution, formalized by Holland (1975) and Goldberg (1989).
- **Five canonical steps:** Initialization → Fitness Evaluation → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → (repeat) → Stop.
- **Encoding options:** Binary, Real-Valued, Integer/Permutation, Gray Code, Tree-Based — choice depends on problem.
- **Binary decoding:** $x = a + \frac{(b-a)}{2^L - 1} \sum b_i 2^i$.
- **Selection methods (descending popularity):** Tournament (k=2) > Roulette Wheel > Rank > SUS > Boltzmann > Truncation.
- **Roulette Wheel probability:** $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.
- **Crossover types:** Single-Point, Two-Point, Uniform, Arithmetic, OX, PMX, CX, SBX.
- **Crossover probability:** $P_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$ (high — exploitation).
- **Mutation types:** Bit-Flip (binary), Gaussian/Uniform (real), Swap/Inversion (permutation).
- **Mutation probability:** $P_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ (low — exploration).
- **Crossover = exploitation, Mutation = exploration.** Both essential.
- **Stopping conditions:** Fixed generations, fitness convergence, population convergence, target fitness, time budget, diversity threshold.
- **Schema Theorem:** Schemas that are short, low-order, and above-average fitness grow exponentially — the mathematical foundation of GAs.
- **Building Block Hypothesis:** GAs work by combining short, high-fitness schemas.
- **Key formula bundle:** $P_i = f_i/\sum f_j$, $E_i = N f_i / \bar{f}$, $E[\text{flips}] = L P_m$, $x = a + \frac{(b-a)}{2^L-1}\sum b_i 2^i$.
- **Examiner's mantra:** *Always show the fitness table, always declare max/min, always state $P_c$ and $P_m$ explicitly.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Objective Optimization Problem, Principles of Multi-Objective Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multi-Objective Optimization Problem, Principles of Multi-Objective Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOOP)** is an optimization problem that involves simultaneously optimizing two or more conflicting objective functions subject to a set of constraints. Unlike single-objective optimization where a single optimal solution exists, MOOP yields a set of optimal trade-off solutions known as the **Pareto-optimal set** or **Pareto front**.

The general mathematical formulation of a MOOP is:

$$ \min/\max \; F(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}), f_2(\vec{x}), \ldots, f_k(\vec{x}))^T $$

subject to:
$$ g_i(\vec{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m $$
$$ h_j(\vec{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p $$
$$ x_l \leq x_l \leq x_u, \quad l = 1, 2, \ldots, n $$

where:
- $F(\vec{x})$ is the vector of **k** objective functions
- $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **decision variable vector**
- $g_i(\vec{x})$ and $h_j(\vec{x})$ represent **inequality and equality constraints**
- $x_l$ and $x_u$ are the **lower and upper bounds** of decision variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A MOOP is characterized by the **inherent conflict** between objectives. Improving one objective typically degrades another, making it impossible to find a single solution that simultaneously optimizes all objectives. The concept of optimality must therefore be redefined using **Pareto dominance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **buying a car** with a limited budget. You want:
1. **Maximum fuel efficiency** (km/liter)
2. **Maximum comfort** (legroom, seat quality)
3. **Minimum cost**
4. **Maximum safety rating**

These objectives are **conflicting**:
- A fuel-efficient car may be less comfortable (smaller, lighter)
- A safer car is usually more expensive
- A cheaper car often lacks premium comfort features

You cannot have **everything**. Instead of one "best" car, you end up with a **shortlist of trade-off cars** — for example:
- Car A: High mileage, low comfort, low cost
- Car B: Medium mileage, high comfort, high cost
- Car C: Balanced features, medium cost

This shortlist of "equally good but incomparable" cars is the **Pareto Front**. A decision-maker then picks based on personal preference (this final selection step is handled by a **Decision Maker (DM)** or higher-level optimization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In MOOP, the optimization algorithm's job is to **generate the set of best trade-offs** (Pareto front), not to pick a single solution. The decision-maker chooses the final point from this set based on contextual priorities.

## 1.3 Single-Objective vs. Multi-Objective Optimization

| Feature | Single-Objective (SOO) | Multi-Objective (MOO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Objectives** | Exactly **1** objective | **2 or more** objectives |
| **Solution** | Single global optimum | A **set of Pareto-optimal solutions** |
| **Optimality Concept** | Scalar comparison | **Pareto dominance** |
| **Decision Maker Role** | Not required | Required to select final solution |
| **Algorithm Output** | One point | A **front** (curve/surface) |
| **Example Algorithm** | Gradient Descent, GA | **NSGA-II, SPEA2, MOEA/D** |

## 1.4 Standard Terminology Used in KTU Examinations

- **Decision Space:** The n-dimensional space of decision variables $\vec{x}$.
- **Objective Space:** The k-dimensional space of objective vectors $F(\vec{x})$.
- **Feasible Region ($S$):** The set of all $\vec{x}$ satisfying the constraints.
- **Image of Feasible Region:** The set $\{F(\vec{x}) \mid \vec{x} \in S\}$ in objective space.
- **Ideal Vector ($F^*$):** The vector formed by individually minimizing each objective.
- **Pareto Optimal Set ($P^*$):** The set of non-dominated feasible solutions.
- **Pareto Front ($PF^*$):** The image of the Pareto optimal set in objective space.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the components of a MOOP", always mention: **decision variables, objective functions, constraints, and Pareto front**. Examiners in KTU specifically look for these four components in 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Concepts in Multi-Objective Optimization

### 2.1.1 Pareto Dominance (The Heart of MOOP)

**Pareto Dominance** is the fundamental comparison operator in MOOP. A solution $\vec{x}_1$ is said to **dominate** $\vec{x}_2$ (written as $\vec{x}_1 \prec \vec{x}_2$) if and only if:

1. $\vec{x}_1$ is **no worse** than $\vec{x}_2$ in **all** objectives, AND
2. $\vec{x}_1$ is **strictly better** than $\vec{x}_2$ in **at least one** objective.

Formally, for a minimization problem:

$$ \vec{x}_1 \prec \vec{x}_2 \iff \begin{cases} f_i(\vec{x}_1) \leq f_i(\vec{x}_2), & \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\} \\ f_j(\vec{x}_1) < f_j(\vec{x}_2), & \exists j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\} \end{cases} $$

**Why Pareto Dominance?** It provides a **partial ordering** of solutions. When no solution dominates another, they are called **non-dominated** or **incomparable**, and both deserve to be on the Pareto front.

### 2.1.2 Pareto Optimality

A solution $\vec{x}^* \in S$ is **Pareto optimal** if there exists **no other feasible solution** $\vec{x} \in S$ that dominates it. In other words:

$$ \nexists \, \vec{x} \in S \; : \; \vec{x} \prec \vec{x}^* $$

### 2.1.3 Pareto Front

The **Pareto Front** is the set of all Pareto-optimal objective vectors:

$$ PF^* = \{F(\vec{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^k \mid \vec{x} \in P^*\} $$

In a **bi-objective** problem (k = 2), the Pareto front is typically a **curve**. In a **tri-objective** problem (k = 3), it becomes a **surface**.

## 2.2 Principles of Multi-Objective Optimization

The principles governing MOOP can be summarized as follows:

### Principle 1: Conflict Recognition
> Objectives in real-world engineering problems are almost always **conflicting**. The optimizer must acknowledge this and seek trade-offs, not single solutions.

### Principle 2: Pareto-Based Comparison
> Use **dominance relations** (not scalar values) to compare solutions. Two solutions are equal if neither dominates the other.

### Principle 3: Diversity Preservation
> A good MOEA must maintain a **diverse set of solutions** along the Pareto front. Clumping all solutions in one region defeats the purpose of trade-off exploration.

### Principle 4: Convergence to True Front
> The algorithm must converge towards the **true Pareto front** (the theoretical optimum). Metrics like **Generational Distance (GD)** measure this.

### Principle 5: Coverage and Spread
> The obtained Pareto front should have **good coverage** (close to the true front) and **good spread** (uniformly distributed across the front).

## 2.3 Classical Methods to Solve MOOP

### 2.3.1 Weighted Sum Method (WSM)

The simplest scalarization technique. Combines multiple objectives into one using weights:

$$ \min \; F_{ws}(\vec{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(\vec{x}) $$

subject to:
$$ \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0 $$

**Limitation:** Cannot find non-convex regions of the Pareto front.

### 2.3.2 ε-Constraint Method

Optimize **one primary objective** while treating the others as bounded constraints:

$$ \min \; f_1(\vec{x}) $$
$$ \text{subject to:} \quad f_i(\vec{x}) \leq \epsilon_i, \quad i = 2, 3, \ldots, k $$

By varying $\epsilon_i$, different Pareto-optimal points are obtained.

### 2.3.3 Goal Programming

Minimize the **deviation** from a set of target goals $b_i$:

$$ \min \; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert f_i(\vec{x}) - b_i \vert $$

This is useful when **target values** are known a priori.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| General MOOP Formulation | $\min F(\vec{x}) = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k)^T$ | Defining the problem |
| Pareto Dominance (Min) | $f_i(\vec{x}_1) \leq f_i(\vec{x}_2) \, \forall i$ AND $\exists j: f_j(\vec{x}_1) < f_j(\vec{x}_2)$ | Comparing two solutions |
| Pareto Optimal Condition | $\nexists \vec{x} \in S : \vec{x} \prec \vec{x}^*$ | Identifying optimal solutions |
| Weighted Sum Method | $F_{ws} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\vec{x})$ | Scalarization |
| Weight Constraint | $\sum w_i = 1, \; w_i \geq 0$ | Normalization of weights |
| ε-Constraint Method | $\min f_1$ s.t. $f_i(\vec{x}) \leq \epsilon_i$ | Generating Pareto points |
| Goal Programming | $\min \sum \vert f_i(\vec{x}) - b_i \vert$ | Target-based optimization |
| Generational Distance (GD) | $GD = \frac{1}{\vert PF \vert} \sqrt{\sum_{i} d_i^2}$ | Convergence metric |
| Spacing Metric | $S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert PF \vert - 1} \sum (d_i - \bar{d})^2}$ | Distribution uniformity |
| Hypervolume (HV) | Volume dominated by $PF$ w.r.t. reference point | Combined quality metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **absolute value** or **cardinality** (number of elements), and $d_i$ represents the Euclidean distance from each obtained point to the nearest point on the true Pareto front.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

MOOP finds applications in virtually every engineering discipline:

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimizing turbine blade design for **minimum weight AND maximum efficiency**.
- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge design for **minimum cost AND maximum load capacity AND minimum deflection**.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Circuit design for **minimum power consumption AND minimum delay**.
- **Manufacturing:** Process parameter optimization for **maximum quality AND minimum production time AND minimum cost**.
- **Data Science:** Feature selection for **maximum accuracy AND minimum model complexity**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real Exam Connection:** KTU 2024 questions often ask for **real-world examples** of MOOP. Memorize the example: *"Design of a vehicle chassis for minimum weight, maximum strength, and minimum cost."* This is a classic 7-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pareto Dominance Verification

**Problem Setup:** Consider two candidate solutions $\vec{x}_A$ and $\vec{x}_B$ in a bi-objective minimization problem:
- $f_1(\vec{x}_A) = 4, \; f_2(\vec{x}_A) = 6$
- $f_1(\vec{x}_B) = 3, \; f_2(\vec{x}_B) = 8$

**Step 1: Check Condition 1 (No worse in all objectives)**

We need $f_i(\vec{x}_A) \leq f_i(\vec{x}_B)$ for all $i \in \{1, 2\}$.

$$ f_1(\vec{x}_A) = 4 \leq f_1(\vec{x}_B) = 3 \quad \text{? FALSE} $$

$$ \text{Since } 4 > 3, \text{ Condition 1 fails immediately.} $$

**Step 2: Conclusion**

$$ \vec{x}_A \not\prec \vec{x}_B \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{x}_B \not\prec \vec{x}_A \text{ (need to verify)} $$

Verify $\vec{x}_B \prec \vec{x}_A$:
$$ f_1(\vec{x}_B) = 3 \leq f_1(\vec{x}_A) = 4 \quad \text{TRUE} $$
$$ f_2(\vec{x}_B) = 8 \leq f_2(\vec{x}_A) = 6 \quad \text{FALSE (since } 8 > 6\text{)} $$

**Step 3: Final Result**

$$ \text{Neither dominates the other.} $$

Both solutions are **non-dominated** and would both be retained on the Pareto front.

## 3.2 Derivation: Weighted Sum Method — Finding Pareto Points

**Problem:** 
$$ \min (f_1, f_2) = (x^2, (x-2)^2) $$
$$ \text{subject to } x \in [0, 2] $$

**Step 1: Formulate the scalarized objective**

$$ F_{ws}(x) = w_1 \cdot x^2 + w_2 \cdot (x-2)^2 $$

with $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, $w_1, w_2 \geq 0$.

**Step 2: Substitute $w_2 = 1 - w_1$**

$$ F_{ws}(x) = w_1 x^2 + (1 - w_1)(x-2)^2 $$

**Step 3: Expand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ws}(x) &= w_1 x^2 + (1 - w_1)(x^2 - 4x + 4) \\
&= w_1 x^2 + (1 - w_1)x^2 - 4(1 - w_1)x + 4(1 - w_1) \\
&= x^2 - 4(1 - w_1)x + 4(1 - w_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Differentiate and set to zero**

$$ \frac{dF_{ws}}{dx} = 2x - 4(1 - w_1) = 0 $$

$$ x^* = 2(1 - w_1) = 2w_2 $$

**Step 5: Evaluate objective values at $x^*$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1(x^*) &= (2w_2)^2 = 4w_2^2 \\
f_2(x^*) &= (2w_2 - 2)^2 = (2w_2 - 2)^2 = 4(w_2 - 1)^2 = 4w_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Generate Pareto points by varying $w_1$**

| $w_1$ | $w_2$ | $x^*$ | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 | 0.0 |
| 0.25 | 0.75 | 1.5 | 2.25 | 0.25 |
| 0.5 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| 0.75 | 0.25 | 0.5 | 0.25 | 2.25 |
| 1.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 4.0 |

**Step 7: Plot the Pareto Front**

The Pareto front is a smooth curve in the $(f_1, f_2)$ space connecting the points $(4, 0)$, $(2.25, 0.25)$, $(1, 1)$, $(0.25, 2.25)$, and $(0, 4)$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Solution Approaches

| **Method** | **Type** | **Mathematical Form** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | **Pareto Coverage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weighted Sum | A Priori | $\min \sum w_i f_i$ | Simple, intuitive | Cannot handle non-convex fronts | Partial |
| ε-Constraint | A Priori | $\min f_1$ s.t. $f_i \leq \epsilon$ | Handles non-convex | Requires careful ε selection | Full |
| Goal Programming | A Priori | $\min \sum \vert f_i - b_i \vert$ | Useful with targets | Needs target values | Partial |
| Lexicographic | A Priori | Ordered priority | Respects priority | Rigid ordering | Selective |
| Weighted Metric | A Posteriori | $\min (\sum w_i (f_i - z_i^*)^p)^{1/p}$ | Flexible distance | Requires ideal point | Full |
| NSGA-II | Evolutionary | Pareto ranking + crowding | Population-based, robust | Computationally heavy | Full |

> [!NOTE]
> **A Priori vs. A Posteriori:** In **a priori** methods (Weighted Sum, Goal Programming), the decision-maker specifies preferences **before** optimization. In **a posteriori** methods (NSGA-II, SPEA2), the algorithm first generates the Pareto front, and the DM then **picks** a solution. KTU frequently tests this distinction.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Identifying Pareto Front in a Given Dataset

**Given Data:** Six solutions in a bi-objective minimization problem:

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 8 |
| B | 4 | 4 |
| C | 6 | 2 |
| D | 5 | 5 |
| E | 3 | 7 |
| F | 7 | 3 |

**Step 1: Check pairwise dominance**

Compare A vs E:
- $f_1(A) = 2 < f_1(E) = 3$ ✓
- $f_2(A) = 8 > f_2(E) = 7$ ✗

Neither dominates. Both non-dominated.

Compare A vs B:
- $f_1(A) = 2 < f_1(B) = 4$ ✓
- $f_2(A) = 8 > f_2(B) = 4$ ✗

Neither dominates.

Compare B vs D:
- $f_1(B) = 4 < f_1(D) = 5$ ✓
- $f_2(B) = 4 < f_2(D) = 5$ ✓

**B dominates D** → Remove D.

Compare B vs C:
- $f_1(B) = 4 < f_1(C) = 6$ ✓
- $f_2(B) = 4 > f_2(C) = 2$ ✗

Neither dominates.

Compare C vs F:
- $f_1(C) = 6 < f_1(F) = 7$ ✓
- $f_2(C) = 2 < f_2(F) = 3$ ✓

**C dominates F** → Remove F.

**Step 2: Final Pareto Front**

Non-dominated solutions: **{A, B, C, E}** (D and F are removed).

$$ PF = \{(2, 8), (4, 4), (6, 2), (3, 7)\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** E and A are non-dominated with each other, but if you sort by $f_1$, E has $f_1 = 3$ while A has $f_1 = 2$, but E has $f_2 = 7$ vs A's $f_2 = 8$. So neither dominates the other. The Pareto front is **not a continuous function** of $f_1$ — it can be a discrete, non-monotonic set of points.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Multi-Objective Optimization Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition] --> B[Identify Decision Variables]
    B --> C[Define Objective Functions]
    C --> D[Specify Constraints]
    D --> E{Feasible Region S}
    E --> F[Apply MOEA or Scalarization]
    F --> G[Pareto Ranking]
    G --> H[Diversity Preservation]
    H --> I{Pareto Optimal Set}
    I --> J[Pareto Front PF]
    J --> K[Decision Maker DM]
    K --> L[Final Selected Solution]
    
    M[Weighted Sum Method] --> F
    N[Epsilon Constraint Method] --> F
    O[NSGA II Algorithm] --> F
    P[Goal Programming] --> F
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Pareto Dominance Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solution X1] -->|dominates| B[Solution X2]
    A -->|incomparable| C[Solution X3]
    B -->|incomparable| C
    C -->|incomparable| D[Solution X4]
    B -->|dominated by| E[Solution X5]
    D -->|dominates| F[Solution X6]
    E --> G[Pareto Front PF]
    F --> G
    C --> G
    A --> G
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: From MOOP to Final Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Real World Problem]) --> Step1[Formulate MOOP]
    Step1 --> Step2[Choose Solution Strategy]
    Step2 --> Step31[Classical Method]
    Step2 --> Step32[Evolutionary Method]
    Step31 --> Step4[Scalarize Objectives]
    Step4 --> Step5[Apply Single Objective Solver]
    Step5 --> Step6[Generate Multiple Pareto Points]
    Step6 --> Step7[Construct Pareto Front]
    Step32 --> Step8[Initialize Population]
    Step8 --> Step9[Evaluate Fitness]
    Step9 --> Step10[Non Dominated Sorting]
    Step10 --> Step11[Crowding Distance Assignment]
    Step11 --> Step12[Selection Crossover Mutation]
    Step12 --> Step13[New Generation]
    Step13 --> Step14{Convergence Check}
    Step14 -->|No| Step9
    Step14 -->|Yes| Step7
    Step7 --> Step15[Decision Maker Selects Final Solution]
    Step15 --> End([Implementation])
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy of MOOP Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Multi Objective Optimization Problem] --> DV[Decision Variables]
    Root --> OF[Objective Functions]
    Root --> CT[Constraints]
    Root --> DS[Decision Space]
    Root --> OS[Objective Space]
    
    DV --> D1[Continuous Variables]
    DV --> D2[Discrete Variables]
    DV --> D3[Mixed Variables]
    
    OF --> O1[Conflicting Objectives]
    OF --> O2[Commensurable]
    OF --> O3[Incommensurable]
    
    CT --> C1[Inequality Constraints]
    CT --> C2[Equality Constraints]
    CT --> C3[Variable Bounds]
    
    DS --> DSp[Pareto Optimal Set P*]
    OS --> OSf[Pareto Front PF*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These schematics provide a high-level view of the MOOP framework. The interaction between the **optimizer** (algorithm) and the **decision maker (DM)** is the key feature that distinguishes MOOP from single-objective optimization. The DM is the bridge between the mathematical solution set and real-world decision-making.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOOP). How does it differ from a single-objective optimization problem?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOOP)** is an optimization problem involving two or more objective functions that must be optimized simultaneously subject to a set of constraints. It can be mathematically represented as:

$$ \min F(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}), f_2(\vec{x}), \ldots, f_k(\vec{x}))^T $$

subject to $g_i(\vec{x}) \leq 0$ and $h_j(\vec{x}) = 0$.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Single-Objective | Multi-Objective |
|---|---|---|
| Number of objectives | 1 | ≥ 2 |
| Solution | Single point | Set of trade-off points |
| Optimality | Global minimum/maximum | Pareto optimality |
| Comparison | Scalar value | Dominance relation |
| Outcome | One optimal solution | A Pareto front |

In SOO, the comparison is straightforward using scalar objective values. In MOO, objectives often conflict, so the concept of optimality is redefined using **Pareto dominance**, leading to a set of optimal trade-off solutions rather than a single solution.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the concept of Pareto dominance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pareto Dominance** is a binary relation used to compare two solutions in a MOOP. A solution $\vec{x}_1$ is said to **dominate** $\vec{x}_2$ (denoted $\vec{x}_1 \prec \vec{x}_2$) if:

1. $\vec{x}_1$ is **no worse** than $\vec{x}_2$ in all objectives, AND
2. $\vec{x}_1$ is **strictly better** than $\vec{x}_2$ in at least one objective.

**Example (Bi-objective minimization):**
- Solution A: $(f_1, f_2) = (3, 5)$
- Solution B: $(f_1, f_2) = (4, 7)$

Check:
- $f_1(A) = 3 \leq 4 = f_1(B)$ ✓
- $f_2(A) = 5 \leq 7 = f_2(B)$ ✓
- A is strictly better in **both** → **A dominates B** (A ≺ B)

If neither solution dominates the other (e.g., $(2, 8)$ vs $(4, 4)$), both are **non-dominated** and belong to the Pareto front.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the principles of Multi-Objective Optimization in detail. Discuss how Pareto optimality differs from classical optimality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following bi-objective minimization problem:

$$ \min f_1(x) = x^2, \quad \min f_2(x) = (x-4)^2, \quad x \in [0, 4] $$

Using the **Weighted Sum Method** with weights $(w_1, w_2) = (0.3, 0.7)$ and $(0.6, 0.4)$, find two Pareto-optimal solutions. Also plot the points on the objective space. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **principles of Multi-Objective Optimization** are:

**1. Principle of Conflicting Objectives:**
In real-world problems, objectives often conflict. Improving one may worsen another. For example, in vehicle design, increasing safety typically increases weight and cost.

**2. Principle of Pareto Optimality:**
A solution is optimal if no other feasible solution dominates it. The set of such solutions forms the **Pareto front**.

**3. Principle of Trade-off Analysis:**
Since no single solution optimizes all objectives, the analyst must understand the **trade-offs** between objectives.

**4. Principle of Diversity:**
The optimization algorithm should produce a **diverse** set of solutions along the Pareto front, not clustered points.

**5. Principle of Convergence:**
The algorithm should converge to the **true Pareto front**, not get stuck at a local approximation.

**Difference from Classical Optimality:**

| Aspect | Classical Optimality | Pareto Optimality |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Scalar comparison | Vector dominance |
| Solution | Single global optimum | Set of non-dominated solutions |
| Existence | Always exists (under continuity) | Forms a front in objective space |
| Applicability | Single-objective only | Multi-objective problems |

In **classical optimality**, a solution $\vec{x}^*$ is optimal if $f(\vec{x}^*) \leq f(\vec{x})$ for all $\vec{x}$ in feasible region. In **Pareto optimality**, a solution is optimal if no other solution is better in all objectives and at least equal in the rest. Classical optimality is a **special case** of Pareto optimality when $k = 1$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $\min f_1(x) = x^2$, $\min f_2(x) = (x-4)^2$, $x \in [0, 4]$.

**Step 1: Formulate Weighted Sum**

$$ F_{ws}(x) = w_1 \cdot x^2 + w_2 \cdot (x-4)^2 $$

**Step 2: Solve for Case 1: $(w_1, w_2) = (0.3, 0.7)$**

$$ F_{ws}(x) = 0.3x^2 + 0.7(x-4)^2 $$
$$ = 0.3x^2 + 0.7(x^2 - 8x + 16) $$
$$ = 0.3x^2 + 0.7x^2 - 5.6x + 11.2 $$
$$ = x^2 - 5.6x + 11.2 $$

Differentiate:
$$ \frac{dF_{ws}}{dx} = 2x - 5.6 = 0 \implies x = 2.8 $$

Compute objectives:
$$ f_1 = (2.8)^2 = 7.84, \quad f_2 = (2.8-4)^2 = 1.44 $$

**Pareto Point 1: $(7.84, 1.44)$**

**Step 3: Solve for Case 2: $(w_1, w_2) = (0.6, 0.4)$**

$$ F_{ws}(x) = 0.6x^2 + 0.4(x-4)^2 $$
$$ = 0.6x^2 + 0.4x^2 - 3.2x + 6.4 $$
$$ = x^2 - 3.2x + 6.4 $$

Differentiate:
$$ \frac{dF_{ws}}{dx} = 2x - 3.2 = 0 \implies x = 1.6 $$

Compute objectives:
$$ f_1 = (1.6)^2 = 2.56, \quad f_2 = (1.6-4)^2 = 5.76 $$

**Pareto Point 2: $(2.56, 5.76)$**

**Step 4: Plotting**

| Point | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 7.84 | 1.44 |
| P2 | 2.56 | 5.76 |

The two Pareto points are plotted in the objective space, forming part of the continuous Pareto front. The full front connects the extreme points $(0, 16)$ at $x=0$ and $(16, 0)$ at $x=4$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with examples: (i) Pareto Front, (ii) Ideal Vector, (iii) Non-dominated Solution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider six solutions for a bi-objective minimization problem:

| Solution | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 | S6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1$ | 1 | 4 | 3 | 6 | 2 | 5 |
| $f_2$ | 5 | 3 | 6 | 2 | 4 | 5 |

Identify the **Pareto-optimal set** and explain your reasoning with dominance checks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**(i) Pareto Front:**
The **Pareto Front** ($PF^*$) is the set of all Pareto-optimal objective vectors obtained by mapping the Pareto-optimal set from decision space to objective space. It represents the **boundary of the achievable objective space** beyond which no improvement is possible without sacrificing another objective.

*Example:* In a bi-objective minimization of cost and time, the Pareto front is a curve where each point represents the minimum time achievable for a given cost.

**(ii) Ideal Vector ($F^*$):**
The **Ideal Vector** is constructed by optimizing each objective **independently**:

$$ F^* = (f_1^*, f_2^*, \ldots, f_k^*) $$

where $f_i^* = \min_{\vec{x} \in S} f_i(\vec{x})$.

This point is usually **unachievable** in a MOOP because it requires simultaneously satisfying all individual optima, which is generally impossible.

*Example:* If $f_1^* = 2$ and $f_2^* = 3$, the ideal vector is $F^* = (2, 3)$, but no single $\vec{x}$ may achieve both values simultaneously.

**(iii) Non-dominated Solution:**
A solution is **non-dominated** if no other feasible solution dominates it. The set of all non-dominated solutions forms the **Pareto set**.

*Example:* Solutions $(2, 5)$ and $(4, 3)$ — neither dominates the other since each is better in one objective and worse in the other.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Data:**
| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 | 5 |
| S2 | 4 | 3 |
| S3 | 3 | 6 |
| S4 | 6 | 2 |
| S5 | 2 | 4 |
| S6 | 5 | 5 |

**Step 1: Check dominance systematically**

| Comparison | Result | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| S1 vs S5 | **S1 dominates S5** | $1 \leq 2$ ✓ and $5 > 4$ → Wait, $f_2(S1)=5 > f_2(S5)=4$ ✗. Recheck: S1(1,5), S5(2,4). $f_1(S1)<f_1(S5)$ ✓, $f_2(S1)>f_2(S5)$ ✗. **Incomparable** |
| S1 vs S3 | **Incomparable** | $1<3$ ✓, $5<6$ ✓ → **S1 dominates S3** ✓ |
| S1 vs S2 | Incomparable | $1<4$ ✓, $5>3$ ✗ |
| S1 vs S4 | Incomparable | $1<6$ ✓, $5>2$ ✗ |
| S1 vs S6 | **S1 dominates S6** | $1<5$ ✓, $5 \leq 5$ ✓ |
| S2 vs S3 | Incomparable | $4>3$ ✗ |
| S2 vs S4 | Incomparable | $4<6$ ✓, $3>2$ ✗ |
| S2 vs S5 | Incomparable | $4>2$ ✗ |
| S2 vs S6 | **S2 dominates S6** | $4<5$ ✓, $3<5$ ✓ |
| S3 vs S4 | Incomparable | $3<6$ ✓, $6>2$ ✗ |
| S3 vs S5 | **S5 dominates S3** | $2<3$ ✓, $4<6$ ✓ |
| S3 vs S6 | Incomparable | $3<5$ ✓, $6>5$ ✗ |
| S4 vs S5 | Incomparable | $6>2$ ✗ |
| S4 vs S6 | **S4 dominates S6** | $6>5$ ✗. Recheck: $f_1(S4)=6, f_1(S6)=5$. $6>5$ ✗. Incomparable |
| S5 vs S6 | **S5 dominates S6** | $2<5$ ✓, $4<5$ ✓ |

**Step 2: Identify dominated solutions**

- S3: dominated by S1
- S6: dominated by S1, S2, S5

**Step 3: Final Pareto Set**

**Non-dominated solutions: {S1, S2, S4, S5}**

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | 1 | 5 |
| S2 | 4 | 3 |
| S4 | 6 | 2 |
| S5 | 2 | 4 |

**Pareto Front:** $\{(1,5), (2,4), (4,3), (6,2)\}$

These four solutions form the Pareto-optimal set, and their image forms the Pareto front in the objective space.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Dominates" with "Better in all":** Students often write that "X dominates Y if X is better in ALL objectives." This is **WRONG**. Correct condition: X is no worse in ALL, and strictly better in AT LEAST ONE.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to check both directions:** When asked "Does A dominate B?", students check only $f_1$. Always check ALL $k$ objectives.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Minimization and Maximization signs:** In maximization problems, $f_i(A) \geq f_i(B)$ for all $i$. Reversing the inequality is a common error worth losing 2-3 marks.
> 
> 4. **Not stating weight constraint in Weighted Sum Method:** Always write $\sum w_i = 1$ and $w_i \geq 0$. Examiners deduct marks for missing this.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Pareto Front with Pareto Set:** Pareto Set is in **decision space**; Pareto Front is in **objective space**. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" in dominance questions:** Simply stating "A dominates B" without showing the inequality check loses marks. Always show the comparison.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MOOP Definition:** Optimization with **two or more** conflicting objectives, yielding a set of trade-off solutions.
- **Pareto Dominance ($\prec$):** $\vec{x}_1 \prec \vec{x}_2$ iff $\vec{x}_1$ is no worse in **all** objectives AND strictly better in **at least one**.
- **Pareto Optimality:** A solution is Pareto optimal if **no other feasible solution dominates it**.
- **Pareto Set ($P^*$):** Set of all Pareto-optimal solutions in **decision space**.
- **Pareto Front ($PF^*$):** Image of Pareto set in **objective space**.
- **Ideal Vector ($F^*$):** Component-wise best objective values, usually **unachievable**.
- **Weighted Sum Method:** $F_{ws} = \sum w_i f_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$. Simple but fails on non-convex fronts.
- **ε-Constraint Method:** Optimize one objective, bound others by $\epsilon_i$. Can find non-convex front points.
- **Goal Programming:** Minimize deviation from target values $b_i$. Useful when targets are known.
- **Generational Distance (GD):** Measures **convergence** to true Pareto front (lower is better).
- **Spacing Metric:** Measures **uniformity** of distribution along the front (lower is better).
- **Hypervolume:** Combined metric measuring both convergence and spread (higher is better).
- **NSGA-II:** Most popular MOEA, uses **non-dominated sorting** + **crowding distance** for diversity.
- **A Priori vs. A Posteriori:** A priori methods need DM preferences **before**; a posteriori methods generate front **then** ask DM.
- **KTU Key Distinction:** Always distinguish between **decision space** (variables) and **objective space** (criteria) in diagrams and answers.
- **Exam Formula to Memorize:** $\vec{x}_1 \prec \vec{x}_2 \iff f_i(\vec{x}_1) \leq f_i(\vec{x}_2) \, \forall i$ AND $\exists j: f_j(\vec{x}_1) < f_j(\vec{x}_2)$.
- **Quick Check for Pareto Optimality:** If you cannot find any solution that beats the candidate in **all** objectives simultaneously, the candidate is Pareto optimal.
