<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) — The Foundation

In real-world engineering problems, engineers rarely optimize just **one** objective. A car designer wants to simultaneously **minimize fuel consumption**, **minimize emission**, and **maximize passenger comfort**. These objectives often **conflict** with each other. This gives rise to the field of **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOOP) is defined as the task of finding a vector of decision variables $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ that satisfies a set of constraints and **optimizes** a vector of objective functions $\vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}), f_2(\vec{x}), \ldots, f_k(\vec{x}))$, where $k \geq 2$.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize/Maximize} \quad & \vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}),\, f_2(\vec{x}),\, \ldots,\, f_k(\vec{x})) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_i(\vec{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_j(\vec{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& \vec{x} \in \mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\mathcal{S}$ is the **feasible search space** (decision space) and the image of $\mathcal{S}$ under $\vec{F}$ is the **objective space** (criterion space).

## 1.2 Pareto Dominance — The Heart of Multi-Objective Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pareto Dominance (Core Definition):**
> A decision vector $\vec{x}^{(1)}$ is said to **Pareto-dominate** $\vec{x}^{(2)}$ (denoted $\vec{x}^{(1)} \prec \vec{x}^{(2)}$) if and only if:
> 1. $\vec{x}^{(1)}$ is **no worse** than $\vec{x}^{(2)}$ in **all** objectives, i.e., $f_i(\vec{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\vec{x}^{(2)})$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ (assuming minimization).
> 2. $\vec{x}^{(1)}$ is **strictly better** than $\vec{x}^{(2)}$ in **at least one** objective, i.e., $f_j(\vec{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\vec{x}^{(2)})$ for at least one $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.

In simpler terms: A solution **dominates** another if it is **at least as good in everything** and **better in something**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Job Selection" Analogy 🎓

Imagine two job offers as **solutions** to a multi-objective problem (more salary, less workload, closer to home):

| Offer | Salary (Higher Better) | Workload (Lower Better) | Distance (Lower Better) |
|:-----:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| A | ₹12 LPA | 50 hrs/week | 5 km |
| B | ₹10 LPA | 40 hrs/week | 8 km |
| C | ₹11 LPA | 45 hrs/week | 7 km |

- **A does NOT dominate B** because A has higher workload (worse) and more distance (worse), even though salary is better.
- **A does NOT dominate C** either — mixed trade-offs.
- **None dominates any other** → these are all **non-dominated** or **Pareto-optimal** solutions!

## 1.3 Pareto Optimality — The "Best Compromise Set"

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Optimal / Non-Dominated Solution:**
> A solution $\vec{x}^* \in \mathcal{S}$ is called **Pareto Optimal** (or **non-dominated**) if there exists **no other feasible** solution $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ that dominates it, i.e., there is no $\vec{x}$ such that $\vec{x} \prec \vec{x}^*$.

The set of **all** Pareto-optimal solutions is the **Pareto Set** ($\mathcal{P}^*$) in decision space, and its image $\vec{F}(\mathcal{P}^*)$ is the **Pareto Front** ($\mathcal{P}\mathcal{F}^*$) in objective space.

## 1.4 Collective Systems — Wisdom of the Crowd

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collective Intelligence Systems (Swarm Intelligence):**
> A **collective system** is a population-based metaheuristic in which simple, decentralized agents interact locally with each other and the environment, producing emergent **global intelligent behavior** without centralized control. Examples include **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** and **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**.

The core philosophy: **Simple rules + Local interactions = Global optimum**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dominance — The Mathematical Construction

To compare two solutions $\vec{x}^{(a)}$ and $\vec{x}^{(b)}$, we define a **dominance operator** $\prec$ on the objective space. For a minimization problem with $k$ objectives:

$$
\vec{x}^{(a)} \prec \vec{x}^{(b)} \iff
\begin{cases}
\forall i \in \{1, \ldots, k\}: f_i(\vec{x}^{(a)}) \leq f_i(\vec{x}^{(b)}) \\
\exists j \in \{1, \ldots, k\}: f_j(\vec{x}^{(a)}) < f_j(\vec{x}^{(b)})
\end{cases}
$$

If neither $\vec{x}^{(a)} \prec \vec{x}^{(b)}$ nor $\vec{x}^{(b)} \prec \vec{x}^{(a)}$ holds, the two solutions are said to be **incomparable** or **mutually non-dominating**.

### Why Dominance Matters in Soft Computing

- **NSGA-II, SPEA2, MOEA/D** — all famous Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) — use dominance as the **primary selection operator**.
- It provides a **partial order** (not total order) on the solution set, which is exactly what we need for trade-off problems.

## 2.2 Pareto Front — The Geometric Picture

The **Pareto Front** is a (k−1)-dimensional manifold in the k-dimensional objective space. In the **bi-objective** case ($k=2$), it reduces to a **curve**; in **tri-objective** case ($k=3$), it is a **surface**.

| Property | Description |
|:---------|:------------|
| **Non-Dominated** | No point on the front is dominated by another point on the front |
| **Trade-off Curve** | Moving along it improves one objective while worsening another |
| **Ideal Point** | $f_i^{ideal} = \min f_i(\vec{x})$ — usually **unattainable** simultaneously |
| **Nadir Point** | $f_i^{nadir} = \max_{\vec{x} \in \mathcal{P}^*} f_i(\vec{x})$ — the "worst" among bests |

## 2.3 Optimality Conditions — Kuhn-Tucker Generalization

For unconstrained multi-objective problems, a necessary condition is that the **gradient of each objective vanishes** at a Pareto-optimal point (stationarity). For constrained problems, we invoke the **Generalized Kuhn-Tucker (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker) Conditions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KKT Conditions for Multi-Objective Optimization:**
> At a Pareto-optimal point $\vec{x}^*$, there exist Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_i \geq 0$ (one per objective) and $\mu_j, \nu_l$ (for constraints) **not all zero**, such that:
> 1. **Stationarity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \nabla f_i(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{l=1}^{p} \nu_l \nabla h_l(\vec{x}^*) = \vec{0}$
> 2. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_j(\vec{x}^*) \leq 0$ and $h_l(\vec{x}^*) = 0$
> 3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\lambda_i \geq 0,\, \mu_j \geq 0$
> 4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\mu_j g_j(\vec{x}^*) = 0$ for all $j$
> 5. **Normalization:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i = 1$ (or another convention to avoid trivial solution)

The KKT conditions are **first-order necessary** conditions. If the problem is **convex**, they are also **sufficient**.

## 2.4 Scalarization — Converting MOO to Single-Objective

The most classical method to solve an MOOP is to convert it into a **single-objective** problem using a weighted sum:

$$
\min_{\vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \quad F(\vec{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\vec{x}), \quad w_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1
$$

By varying $\vec{w} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k)$, different Pareto-optimal points are traced out (for convex fronts).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — KTU Examination High-Yield Formula Reference**

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|:--------|:-------------------|:------|
| Pareto Dominance | $f_i(\vec{x}^{(a)}) \leq f_i(\vec{x}^{(b)}) \forall i$ AND strict for at least one $i$ | Minimization form |
| Pareto Optimality | $\nexists \vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ such that $\vec{x} \prec \vec{x}^*$ | Definition of non-dominated |
| Weighted Sum Scalarization | $F(\vec{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i f_i(\vec{x})$ | $w_i \geq 0$, $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Tchebycheff Scalarization | $\min_{\vec{x}} \max_{i} \left[ w_i \mid f_i(\vec{x}) - z_i^* \mid \right]$ | Generates **any** Pareto point (convex + non-convex) |
| KKT Stationarity | $\sum_{i} \lambda_i \nabla f_i + \sum_{j} \mu_j \nabla g_j + \sum_{l} \nu_l \nabla h_l = \vec{0}$ | Multiplier weights |
| PSO Velocity Update | $v_{id}^{t+1} = w v_{id}^t + c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}^t) + c_2 r_2 (g_d - x_{id}^t)$ | Inertia + cognitive + social |
| ACO Pheromone Update | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ | $\rho$ = evaporation rate |
| Ideal Point | $z_i^{ideal} = \min_{\vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}} f_i(\vec{x})$ | Component-wise min |
| Nadir Point | $z_i^{nadir} = \max_{\vec{x} \in \mathcal{P}^*} f_i(\vec{x})$ | Worst value on Pareto front |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Design:** Trade-off between **weight** vs. **strength** vs. **cost** in wing design.
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Trade-off between **power** vs. **delay** vs. **area** in circuit layout.
- **Supply Chain:** Trade-off between **delivery time** vs. **transport cost** vs. **carbon emission**.
- **Telecommunications:** Trade-off between **throughput** vs. **latency** vs. **jitter** in network routing (directly linked to **ACO** for routing).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Why KKT Conditions Generalize Pareto Optimality

We start with the constrained multi-objective problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}), f_2(\vec{x}), \ldots, f_k(\vec{x})) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\vec{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1: Define the Lagrangian Function

For the multi-objective case, each objective $f_i$ gets its own multiplier $\lambda_i \geq 0$, and each inequality constraint gets $\mu_j \geq 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\vec{x}, \vec{\lambda}, \vec{\mu}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i f_i(\vec{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j g_j(\vec{x})
$$

**Logic:** We combine all objectives and constraints into a single scalar expression to apply calculus-based optimization.

### Step 2: Apply Stationarity (First-Order Necessary Condition)

For an **unconstrained local minimum** of $\mathcal{L}$, the gradient with respect to $\vec{x}$ must vanish:

$$
\nabla_{\vec{x}} \mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \nabla f_i(\vec{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\vec{x}) = \vec{0}
$$

**Logic:** This is the first-order KKT condition. It says the weighted sum of objective gradients balances the constraint gradients.

### Step 3: Add the Remaining KKT Constraints

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(a) Primal Feasibility:} \quad g_j(\vec{x}^*) \leq 0 \quad \forall j \\
&\text{(b) Dual Feasibility:} \quad \lambda_i \geq 0,\ \mu_j \geq 0 \\
&\text{(c) Complementary Slackness:} \quad \mu_j \cdot g_j(\vec{x}^*) = 0 \quad \forall j \\
&\text{(d) Normalization:} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Complementary slackness says: if a constraint is **inactive** ($g_j < 0$), then $\mu_j = 0$ (it doesn't influence the optimum); if it is **active** ($g_j = 0$), then $\mu_j \geq 0$ may be nonzero.

### Step 4: Interpret the Conditions Geometrically

The stationarity condition can be rewritten as:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \nabla f_i(\vec{x}^*) = - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\vec{x}^*)
$$

The left side is a **conic combination of objective gradients** in the negative direction. The right side is the **negative conic combination of active constraint gradients**. At a Pareto optimum, **improving one objective necessarily worsens another** — exactly what the $\lambda_i \geq 0$ formulation captures.

### Step 5: Sufficiency Under Convexity

If $f_i(\vec{x})$ are **convex** and $g_j(\vec{x})$ are convex, then a feasible $\vec{x}^*$ satisfying KKT conditions is **globally Pareto optimal**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Pareto Front Identification

**Problem:** Consider the bi-objective minimization problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1(\vec{x}) &= x_1^2 + x_2^2 \\
f_2(\vec{x}) &= (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. Determine the Pareto front using the weighted sum method.

### Step 1: Construct the Weighted Sum

$$
F_w(\vec{x}) = w f_1(\vec{x}) + (1-w) f_2(\vec{x}), \quad w \in [0, 1]
$$

### Step 2: Expand the Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_w(\vec{x}) &= w(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + (1-w)\left[(x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2\right] \\
&= w x_1^2 + (1-w)(x_1^2 - 4x_1 + 4) + w x_2^2 + (1-w)(x_2^2 - 4x_2 + 4) \\
&= x_1^2 - 4(1-w)x_1 + 4(1-w) + x_2^2 - 4(1-w)x_2 + 4(1-w) \\
&= x_1^2 - 4(1-w)x_1 + x_2^2 - 4(1-w)x_2 + 8(1-w)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We expanded the squared terms, grouped by variable, and prepared for differentiation.

### Step 3: Compute the Gradient and Set to Zero

$$
\frac{\partial F_w}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4(1-w) = 0 \implies x_1^* = 2(1-w)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial F_w}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 4(1-w) = 0 \implies x_2^* = 2(1-w)
$$

**Logic:** Standard single-variable minimization in $x_1$ and $x_2$ separately, since $F_w$ is separable.

### Step 4: Substitute Back into the Objectives

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1(\vec{x}^*(w)) &= \left[2(1-w)\right]^2 + \left[2(1-w)\right]^2 = 8(1-w)^2 \\
f_2(\vec{x}^*(w)) &= (2(1-w) - 2)^2 + (2(1-w) - 2)^2 = (2w)^2 + (2w)^2 = 8w^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Form the Parametric Pareto Front

As $w$ varies from $0$ to $1$, the point $(f_1, f_2)$ traces out the **Pareto Front**:

$$
f_2 = 8w^2 = 8(1 - \sqrt{f_1/8})^2
$$

Simplifying, the **Pareto Front equation** is:

$$
\sqrt{f_1/8} + \sqrt{f_2/8} = 1 \implies \sqrt{f_1} + \sqrt{f_2} = 2\sqrt{2}
$$

**Logic:** This is a quarter-ellipse in the first quadrant — the classical shape of a bi-objective Pareto front for two quadratic objectives with displaced minima.

## 3.3 PSO Velocity-Position Update Derivation

The Particle Swarm Optimization update rule arises from a **trajectory analysis** where a particle's velocity is driven by three forces:

### Step 1: Decompose the Velocity Update

$$
v_{id}^{t+1} = \underbrace{w v_{id}^t}_{\text{Inertia}} + \underbrace{c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}^t)}_{\text{Cognitive}} + \underbrace{c_2 r_2 (g_d - x_{id}^t)}_{\text{Social}}
$$

- **Inertia term ($w v_{id}^t$):** Particle's tendency to continue in its current direction.
- **Cognitive term ($c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}^t)$):** Pull toward the particle's own best-known position (personal memory).
- **Social term ($c_2 r_2 (g_d - x_{id}^t)$):** Pull toward the swarm's global best position (collective intelligence).

### Step 2: Update the Position

$$
x_{id}^{t+1} = x_{id}^t + v_{id}^{t+1}
$$

### Step 3: Convergence Analysis (Simplified 1-D Case)

Setting $r_1 = r_2 = 0$ and analyzing the homogeneous solution $x^{t+1} = (1+w)x^t - w x^{t-1}$:

The characteristic equation $\lambda^2 - (1+w)\lambda + w = 0$ has roots:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(1+w) \pm \sqrt{(1+w)^2 - 4w}}{2} = \frac{(1+w) \pm \sqrt{(1-w)^2}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = w
$$

For **convergence** (oscillation decay), we need $\vert \lambda_2 \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert w \vert < 1$. The KTU-standard inertia range is $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$.
