# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Pareto Optimality. How does it differ from a global optimum in single-objective optimization?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pareto Optimality:** A solution $\vec{x}^* \in \mathcal{S}$ is Pareto Optimal if there is **no other feasible solution** $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ that Pareto-dominates it, i.e., $\nexists \vec{x} \in \mathcal{S}$ such that $\vec{x} \prec \vec{x}^*$.

| Aspect | Global Optimum (Single-Objective) | Pareto Optimality (Multi-Objective) |
|:-------|:----------------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| **Number of Objectives** | Exactly 1 | 2 or more |
| **Comparison** | Total order (can rank all solutions) | Partial order (many solutions incomparable) |
| **Number of Optima** | Exactly one unique global min (under strict convexity) | A **set** of trade-off solutions |
| **Goal** | Find the single best | Find the best **compromise set** (Pareto Front) |
| **Selection Method** | Scalar comparison $f(a) < f(b)$ | Vector comparison via dominance |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a collective system in soft computing? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **collective system** (or **swarm intelligence system**) is a population-based metaheuristic that solves complex optimization problems by simulating the **decentralized, self-organized behavior** of natural agents (ants, bees, birds, fish). Each agent follows **simple local rules**, and through **agent-agent and agent-environment interactions**, an **emergent global intelligence** arises — finding high-quality solutions without any central controller.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** — Simulates ants depositing pheromones to find shortest paths (used in **TSP**, **network routing**).
2. **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** — Simulates birds/fish flocking where particles adjust trajectories based on personal and social bests (used in **function optimization**, **neural network training**).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Pareto Dominance** with mathematical formulation. Given the following 5 solutions with two objectives (both to be minimized), construct the **Pareto Front**.

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|:--------:|:-----:|:-----:|
| S1 | 1 | 5 |
| S2 | 3 | 3 |
| S3 | 5 | 1 |
| S4 | 2 | 4 |
| S5 | 4 | 2 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and explain the **Generalized Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** for a constrained multi-objective optimization problem. How do these conditions reduce to the standard KKT conditions for a single-objective problem?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Apply dominance pairwise:**

- **S1(1,5) vs S2(3,3):** $f_1(S1) = 1 < 3 = f_1(S2)$ (S1 better), $f_2(S1) = 5 > 3 = f_2(S2)$ (S2 better). **→ Incomparable**
- **S1(1,5) vs S3(5,1):** S1 better in $f_1$, worse in $f_2$. **→ Incomparable**
- **S1(1,5) vs S4(2,4):** $f_1(S1) = 1 < 2 = f_1(S4)$ AND $f_2(S1) = 5 > 4 = f_2(S4)$. **→ Incomparable**
- **S2(3,3) vs S4(2,4):** $f_1(S2) = 3 > 2 = f_1(S4)$ (S4 better), $f_2(S2) = 3 < 4 = f_2(S4)$ (S2 better). **→ Incomparable**
- **S4(2,4) vs S5(4,2):** $f_1(S4) = 2 < 4 = f_1(S5)$, $f_2(S4) = 4 > 2 = f_2(S5)$. **→ Incomparable**
- **S2(3,3) vs S5(4,2):** $f_1(S2) = 3 < 4 = f_1(S5)$, $f_2(S2) = 3 > 2 = f_2(S5)$. **→ Incomparable**
- **S3(5,1) vs S5(4,2):** $f_1(S3) = 5 > 4 = f_1(S5)$, $f_2(S3) = 1 < 2 = f_2(S5)$. **→ Incomparable**

**Step 2 — Pareto Front Identification:**

Since no solution dominates any other, **all 5 solutions are non-dominated**.

$$
\mathcal{P}\mathcal{F}^* = \{(1,5),\, (2,4),\, (3,3),\, (4,2),\, (5,1)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Observation:** These 5 points lie on the line $f_1 + f_2 = 6$, which is a **linear Pareto Front** (continuous convex trade-off).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Multi-Objective KKT Formulation:**

Given:

$$
\min \vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k), \quad \text{s.t. } g_j(\vec{x}) \leq 0,\ h_l(\vec{x}) = 0
$$

**KKT conditions** state that at a Pareto-optimal $\vec{x}^*$, there exist multipliers $\lambda_i \geq 0$ (for objectives), $\mu_j \geq 0$ (for inequalities), $\nu_l$ (free for equalities) such that:

1. **Stationarity:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \nabla f_i(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{l=1}^{p} \nu_l \nabla h_l(\vec{x}^*) = \vec{0}
$$

2. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_j(\vec{x}^*) \leq 0,\ h_l(\vec{x}^*) = 0$

3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\lambda_i \geq 0,\ \mu_j \geq 0$

4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\mu_j g_j(\vec{x}^*) = 0$

5. **Normalization:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i > 0$

**Step 2 — Reduction to Single-Objective KKT:**

For a single-objective problem, $k = 1$, so there is only one multiplier $\lambda_1$ which can be **set to 1** (by scaling freedom). The stationarity condition collapses to:

$$
\nabla f_1(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\vec{x}^*) + \sum_{l=1}^{p} \nu_l \nabla h_l(\vec{x}^*) = \vec{0}
$$

This is precisely the **classical KKT condition** for a single-objective problem. The other conditions remain identical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Many students forget the **normalization condition** $\sum \lambda_i > 0$ — without it, the trivial solution $\lambda_i = \mu_j = \nu_l = 0$ satisfies stationarity trivially.
> 2. Don't confuse **KKT sufficient** (needs convexity) with **KKT necessary** (general first-order conditions). Examiners love asking this distinction.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** algorithm as a collective system. Derive the **velocity and position update equations** and explain the role of the inertia weight, cognitive coefficient, and social coefficient.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** algorithm. How does pheromone update and evaporation drive the collective intelligence of the ant colony?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — PSO Overview:**

PSO is a population-based stochastic optimization technique inspired by the **social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling**, proposed by **Kennedy and Eberhart (1995)**. Each particle in the swarm represents a candidate solution and moves through the search space guided by its own experience and the swarm's collective experience.

**Step 2 — Velocity Update Equation:**

For particle $i$ in dimension $d$ at iteration $t$:

$$
v_{id}^{t+1} = w \cdot v_{id}^{t} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}^{t}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{d} - x_{id}^{t})
$$

Where:
- $w$ = **inertia weight** (controls exploration vs. exploitation)
- $c_1$ = **cognitive coefficient** (attraction to personal best $p_{id}$)
- $c_2$ = **social coefficient** (attraction to global best $g_d$)
- $r_1, r_2$ = uniform random numbers in $[0, 1]$

**Step 3 — Position Update Equation:**

$$
x_{id}^{t+1} = x_{id}^{t} + v_{id}^{t+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Role of Each Parameter:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Role | Effect on Search |
|:----------|:------:|:----|:-----------------|
| Inertia Weight | $w$ | Balances exploration vs. exploitation | High $w$ → global search; Low $w$ → local refinement |
| Cognitive Coefficient | $c_1$ | Pull toward particle's own best | Encourages individual learning |
| Social Coefficient | $c_2$ | Pull toward swarm's best | Encourages collective convergence |

**Step 5 — Convergence Condition:**

For convergence (avoiding divergence), the analysis yields: $0 < w < 1$, with $c_1 + c_2 \leq 4$ and $w > (c_1 + c_2)/2 - 1$. Standard KTU-recommended values: $w = 0.729$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.494$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — ACO Overview:**

Ant Colony Optimization, proposed by **Dorigo (1992)**, is a probabilistic metaheuristic inspired by the **pheromone-laying behavior of real ants** searching for food. Ants deposit pheromone on paths they traverse; shorter paths accumulate pheromone faster, attracting more ants — leading to emergent shortest-path discovery.

**Step 2 — Construction Phase:**

At each iteration, ant $k$ at node $i$ chooses next node $j$ with probability:

$$
P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

Where:
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ = pheromone level on edge $(i,j)$
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ = **heuristic visibility** (inverse of distance)
- $\alpha$ = pheromone influence weight
- $\beta$ = heuristic influence weight
- $\mathcal{N}_i^{k}$ = set of unvisited neighbors

**Step 3 — Pheromone Update:**

After all ants complete their tours, pheromone is updated on each edge $(i,j)$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{M} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

Where:
- $\rho \in (0, 1]$ = **evaporation rate** (prevents premature convergence)
- $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_k$ if ant $k$ traversed edge $(i,j)$, else $0$
- $Q$ = pheromone constant, $L_k$ = tour length of ant $k$

**Step 4 — Collective Intelligence Emergence:**

- **Positive feedback:** Shorter tours deposit more pheromone → attract more ants → reinforce the path.
- **Negative feedback (evaporation):** Forgotten old trails → enables exploration of new routes.
- **Stigmergy:** Indirect communication through the environment (pheromone trails), **not direct ant-to-ant communication**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Students often forget the **evaporation term** $(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ — without it, the algorithm can get stuck in local optima forever.
> 2. Confusing $\alpha$ (pheromone exponent) with $\rho$ (evaporation rate) is a very common mistake. Remember: $\alpha$ controls **how strongly ants follow existing trails**, while $\rho$ controls **how fast old trails fade**.
> 3. In ACO, the **heuristic $\eta$** depends on the problem (e.g., $1/d$ for TSP), unlike the **learned $\tau$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **Pareto Dominance** requires BOTH conditions: (i) no worse in all objectives, AND (ii) strictly better in at least one.
- ⭐ **Pareto Optimal Solution** = a non-dominated feasible solution; the **Pareto Front** is the image of all such solutions in objective space.
- ⭐ **Pareto Front** is typically a (k−1)-dimensional manifold in k-dimensional objective space.
- ⭐ **Ideal Point** = component-wise min (usually unattainable); **Nadir Point** = component-wise max of Pareto set.
- ⭐ **KKT Conditions** for MOO involve a **weighted sum of objective gradients** balancing constraint gradients.
- ⭐ **Normalization condition** $\sum \lambda_i > 0$ is **mandatory** to avoid trivial zero-multiplier solution.
- ⭐ KKT conditions are **necessary** in general but **sufficient only under convexity**.
- ⭐ **Weighted Sum Scalarization** can only find **convex portions** of the Pareto front; for non-convex fronts, use **Tchebycheff** or **$\varepsilon$-constraint** methods.
- ⭐ **PSO** uses **three driving forces**: inertia ($w$), cognitive ($c_1$), social ($c_2$).
- ⭐ **Standard PSO parameters:** $w \approx 0.729$, $c_1 = c_2 \approx 1.494$ (constriction coefficient approach).
- ⭐ **ACO** uses **two mechanisms**: **pheromone deposit** (positive feedback) and **evaporation** (negative feedback / exploration).
- ⭐ **Stigmergy** = indirect communication through environment modification (the key to swarm intelligence).
- ⭐ **Collective systems** exhibit **emergence** — global optimal behavior from local simple rules without central control.
- ⭐ **NSGA-II** uses **Non-Dominated Sorting** + **Crowding Distance** — both are extensions of dominance concepts.
- ⭐ **Applications** of MOO/Collective Systems: VLSI design, aerospace, supply chain, network routing, neural architecture search, financial portfolio optimization.
- ⭐ **Trade-off Curve** is the geometric essence of MOO — improving one objective necessarily worsens another on the Pareto Front.

**End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Dominance, Pareto-Optimality, Optimality Conditions, Collective Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biological Self-Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biological Self-Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> **Biological Self-Organization (BSO)** is the spontaneous emergence of macroscopic order, pattern, or coherent structure from the local interactions of decentralized, autonomous biological (or bio-inspired) components, operating without any external controller, blueprint, or global instruction set. The macroscopic order arises purely from the **non-linear collective dynamics** of the interacting units, governed by four universal mechanisms: **positive feedback, negative feedback, randomness/fluctuations, and multiple direct/indirect interactions (stigmergy)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why BSO matters in Soft Computing (PECST417 perspective):**
> BSO is the *conceptual bedrock* of all nature-inspired meta-heuristics — **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)**, **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**, **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)**, and **Cellular Automata** derive their entire design philosophy from observing how simple biological agents (ants, bees, birds, cells) achieve globally optimal solutions using only local rules. Every swarm intelligence algorithm in your KTU syllabus is an *engineered abstraction* of a biological self-organizing process.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ant Colony Kitchen"

Imagine a colony of **leaf-cutter ants** that must organize itself to find the shortest path from the colony to the *N* nearest food sources scattered in a forest. There is **no foreman ant** issuing orders. There is **no blueprint** of the forest. Each ant follows 4 trivial local rules:

1. Wander randomly.
2. When you find food, return to the nest **laying a chemical (pheromone) trail**.
3. When you reach a fork, **prefer the path with the strongest pheromone concentration**.
4. **Pheromone evaporates** over time (negative feedback).

Despite these 4 simple, local, decentralized rules, after some hours, the colony has built a near-optimal pheromone highway network connecting the nest to all food sources. The highway network is a **self-organized global structure** that **no individual ant knows** exists. This is BSO in its purest form.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If the question asks "Define Biological Self-Organization," the examiner expects you to mention **three keywords explicitly**: (1) *decentralized agents*, (2) *local interaction rules*, and (3) *emergence of global order without external control*. Hitting all three scores full marks.

### 1.2 Defining Properties of BSO

| Property | Operational Meaning | Biological Example |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Decentralization** | No single agent has global knowledge or authority | No "leader cell" in an embryo |
| **Local Information** | Each agent senses only its immediate neighborhood | Ants detect pheromone within a few mm |
| **Emergence** | Global pattern cannot be predicted from inspecting any single agent | Termite cathedral mounds |
| **Autopoiesis** | System maintains and repairs its own organization | Wound healing in tissue |
| **Robustness** | Loss of individual agents does not collapse the system | Bee colony survives loss of foragers |
| **Adaptivity** | Reorganization in response to environmental changes | Flock re-routes around an obstacle |

### 1.3 The Four Universal Ingredients (Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz, 1997)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 2024 framework** for analyzing any self-organizing system.

1. **Positive Feedback (Amplification):** Reinforces good structures. *Example:* Pheromone laying in ants.
2. **Negative Feedback (Stabilization):** Counter-balances amplification, prevents runaway growth. *Example:* Pheromone evaporation, resource depletion.
3. **Randomness / Fluctuations:** Allows exploration of new solutions and breaks deadlocks. *Example:* Random walk in ant foraging.
4. **Multiple Interactions (Stigmergy):** Indirect communication via environment modification. *Example:* Pheromone on the trail, temperature in bee swarms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stigmergy** is *the* most-tested concept in KTU module 4. Definition to memorize:
> **Stigmergy** = a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents in which the agent's actions modify the local environment, and that modification subsequently influences the behavior of other agents.
> First proposed by **Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959)** to describe termite nest construction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematics of Self-Organization

A self-organizing system is fundamentally a **complex adaptive system (CAS)**. Mathematically, it is modeled as a set of *N* agents, each with state $s_i(t)$, evolving under a stochastic local rule $f$:

$$s_i(t + \Delta t) = f\bigl(s_i(t),\; \mathcal{N}_i(t),\; \xi(t)\bigr)$$

where:
- $s_i(t)$ = state of agent *i* at time $t$.
- $\mathcal{N}_i(t)$ = neighborhood state (local information).
- $\xi(t)$ = stochastic perturbation (randomness/fluctuations).

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind each parameter:**
> - *Local rule $f$* is the **micro-level cause** — it represents the agent's behavior (lay pheromone, change direction, differentiate, etc.).
> - *Neighborhood $\mathcal{N}_i$* enforces **locality** — the system has no global channel.
> - *Stochastic term $\xi$* is the **exploration engine** — without it, the system would converge prematurely to a sub-optimal attractor.
> - The *macroscopic order* is the **emergent consequence** — a *stable attractor* of the collective dynamics in state space.

### 2.2 Reaction-Diffusion Framework (Turing, 1952)

The **Turing model** of morphogenesis is the prototype for biological pattern formation via BSO. Two morphogens $u$ and $v$ react and diffuse across space:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D_u \nabla^2 u + f(u, v)$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = D_v \nabla^2 v + g(u, v)$$

- $D_u,\; D_v$ = diffusion coefficients, with $D_v \gg D_u$ (inhibitor diffuses faster than activator).
- $f,\;g$ = local non-linear reaction kinetics.
- $\nabla^2$ = Laplacian operator (spatial coupling).

This produces **Turing patterns** — stripes, spots, and spirals seen on animal coats (zebra, leopard, giraffe).

### 2.3 Stigmergy & Pheromone Dynamics (ACO Foundation)

The canonical stigmergic update rule used in Ant Colony Optimization:

$$\tau_{ij}(t + \Delta t) = (1 - \rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij}$$

where:
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ = pheromone intensity on edge $(i,j)$ at time $t$.
- $\rho \in (0, 1]$ = **evaporation rate** (negative feedback).
- $(1-\rho)\cdot\tau_{ij}(t)$ = remaining pheromone after evaporation.
- $\Delta\tau_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ = total pheromone deposited by all *m* ants that traversed $(i,j)$.

The ant's transition probability from node $i$ to node $j$:

$$P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\bigl[\tau_{ij}(t)\bigr]^{\alpha} \cdot \bigl[\eta_{ij}\bigr]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} \bigl[\tau_{il}(t)\bigr]^{\alpha} \cdot \bigl[\eta_{il}\bigr]^{\beta}}$$

where:
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ = **heuristic visibility** (inverse of edge length).
- $\alpha$ = pheromone influence weight, $\beta$ = heuristic influence weight.
- $\mathcal{N}_i^k$ = set of unvisited neighbors of ant $k$ at node $i$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print or memorize this table — it covers 80% of the numerical/theory problems asked in KTU ESE on Module 4.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Legend | Where Used in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State update of an agent | $s_i(t+\Delta t) = f(s_i, \mathcal{N}_i, \xi)$ | General BSO modeling |
| 2 | Reaction-Diffusion (Turing) | $\partial_t u = D_u\nabla^2 u + f(u,v)$ | Pattern formation |
| 3 | Pheromone update (evaporation) | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \Delta\tau_{ij}$ | ACO derivation |
| 4 | ACO transition probability | $P_{ij}^{k} = \frac{\tau_{ij}^{\alpha}\eta_{ij}^{\beta}}{\sum_l \tau_{il}^{\alpha}\eta_{il}^{\beta}}$ | ACO design |
| 5 | Particle velocity update (PSO) | $v_i \leftarrow \omega v_i + c_1 r_1(p_{best}-x_i) + c_2 r_2(g_{best}-x_i)$ | PSO derivation |
| 6 | Position update (PSO) | $x_i \leftarrow x_i + v_i$ | PSO iteration |
| 7 | Stigmergy | Indirect comm. via environment modification | Termite, ant models |
| 8 | Emergence | Global pattern $\neq$ sum of parts | Conceptual questions |
| 9 | Autopoiesis | Self-maintaining, self-repairing | Cell biology analogy |
| 10 | Pheromone decay constant | $\tau(t) = \tau_0 \, e^{-\rho t}$ | Decay problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - **Equation 3 & 4:** Foundation of every **Ant Colony Optimization** algorithm used in vehicle routing, network routing, and job-shop scheduling.
> - **Equation 5 & 6:** Foundation of **Particle Swarm Optimization** used for neural network training, antenna design, and power system optimization.
> - **Equation 2:** Foundation of computational morphogenesis, used in **tissue engineering** and **bio-printed scaffold design**.

### 2.5 Classification of BSO Systems (Critical for KTU Theory)

KTU examiners frequently test the **levels of biological self-organization**:

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cellular** | Single-celled organisms coordinating | *Dictyostelium* slime mold aggregation via cAMP |
| **Multi-cellular / Developmental** | Embryonic cells differentiating into tissues | Vertebrate limb formation |
| **Population-level** | Groups of organisms coordinating | Ant colonies, bee swarms, bird flocks |
| **Ecosystem-level** | Multiple species co-evolving | Coral reef symbiosis |
| **Computational abstraction** | Bio-inspired algorithms | ACO, PSO, ABC, Firefly Algorithm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **worked-out in board-exam style**. Every algebraic transition is shown; no step is skipped. KTU examiners award full marks only when transitions are explicit.

### 3.1 Derivation: Pheromone Concentration as a Function of Time (First-Order Decay)

**Problem Setup:** Suppose an ant deposits an initial pheromone concentration $\tau_0$ on a trail segment at time $t=0$. The pheromone evaporates continuously with rate constant $\rho$. Derive the concentration $\tau(t)$ at any time $t > 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation.** Pheromone decay obeys a first-order rate law (rate is proportional to current concentration):

$$\frac{d\tau(t)}{dt} = -\rho \, \tau(t)$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.** Divide both sides by $\tau(t)$ and multiply by $dt$:

$$\frac{d\tau(t)}{\tau(t)} = -\rho \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides** from $0$ to $t$ (concentration goes from $\tau_0$ to $\tau(t)$):

$$\int_{\tau_0}^{\tau(t)} \frac{d\tau'}{\tau'} = -\rho \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\ln \tau(t) - \ln \tau_0 = -\rho t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\tau(t)}{\tau_0}\right) = -\rho t$$

$$\frac{\tau(t)}{\tau_0} = e^{-\rho t}$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau(t) = \tau_0 \, e^{-\rho t}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering meaning:** After a time $t = 1/\rho$ (the *time constant*), the pheromone has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. KTU often asks: *What is the half-life?* The half-life $\tau_{1/2}$ is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\rho} = \frac{0.693}{\rho}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ant Colony Optimization — Pheromone Update for the Elitist Ant System

**Problem Setup:** In a graph with *n* nodes, an ant $k$ traverses a path of length $L_k$ from source to destination and deposits $\Delta\tau$ pheromone per unit length. There are *m* ants in total, and the global best-so-far path of length $L_{bsf}$ receives an additional bonus $e \cdot \Delta\tau$ per unit length. Derive the update rule.

**Step 1 — Local deposit from ant *k*:** The amount of pheromone deposited on an edge $(i,j)$ by ant $k$ is:

$$\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\Delta \tau}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses edge } (i,j) \\[2mm] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all ants** (contribution to edge $(i,j)$ from all *m* ants):

$$\Delta \tau_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$$

**Step 3 — Add the elitist reinforcement** (extra pheromone on the global best path):

$$\Delta \tau_{ij}^{elite} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{e \cdot \Delta \tau}{L_{bsf}} & \text{if edge } (i,j) \in \text{best-so-far path} \\[2mm] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the evaporation + deposition update rule:**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{ij}(t + \Delta t) = (1 - \rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij} + \Delta \tau_{ij}^{elite}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** Three competing forces act on every edge:
- **Evaporation** $(1-\rho)\cdot\tau_{ij}(t)$ — forgets old, unused paths (negative feedback).
- **Standard deposition** $\Delta \tau_{ij}$ — reinforces paths used by current-generation ants (positive feedback).
- **Elitist bonus** $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{elite}$ — aggressively reinforces the best-known path (intensification of search).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Particle Swarm Optimization — Velocity & Position Update

**Problem Setup:** A particle *i* in a swarm has current position $\mathbf{x}_i(t)$ and velocity $\mathbf{v}_i(t)$. It remembers its personal best position $\mathbf{p}_i$ and knows the global best $\mathbf{g}$. Inertia weight is $\omega$, cognitive coefficient is $c_1$, social coefficient is $c_2$, and $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ are uniform random numbers. Derive the iterative update equations.

**Step 1 — Identify the three "forces" acting on the particle:**
- **Inertia term** $\omega \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ — tendency to continue in the same direction (momentum).
- **Cognitive term** $c_1 r_1(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i)$ — pull toward the particle's own best memory (self-experience).
- **Social term** $c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ — pull toward the swarm's best-known position (peer knowledge).

**Step 2 — Combine the three forces into the velocity update:**

$$\mathbf{v}_i(t + 1) = \omega \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \bigl(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\bigr) + c_2 r_2 \bigl(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Update the position** (simple kinematic step):

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}_i(t + 1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t + 1)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Clamp the velocity** (numerical stability; prevents explosion):

$$v_{i,d} = \begin{cases} v_{max} & \text{if } v_{i,d} > v_{max} \\ -v_{max} & \text{if } v_{i,d} < -v_{max} \\ v_{i,d} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Self-organization interpretation.** Notice that **no particle has a global map** of the search space. Each particle interacts only with:
- its own memory $\mathbf{p}_i$ (internal feedback),
- the global best $\mathbf{g}$ (broadcast to all — a *weak* form of global signal),
- the random scalars $r_1, r_2$ (fluctuations).
The convergence of the swarm to the global optimum is an **emergent phenomenon** — pure BSO.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: Pheromone Concentration Along Two Paths

**Problem (KTU 2024 ESE-style, 7 marks):** Two ants, $A_1$ and $A_2$, simultaneously leave a nest to find food. The straight path is 10 m long, the curved path is 14 m long. Both ants walk at 1 m/s. Ants deposit $\Delta\tau = 1$ unit of pheromone per meter traversed. Evaporation rate is $\rho = 0.1$ per second. Compute the pheromone intensity on each path at $t = 10$ s and determine which path an incoming ant $A_3$ will prefer if both paths are equally short in its heuristic view ($\eta_{straight} = \eta_{curved}$).

**Step 1 — Compute traversal time for each ant:**

$$t_{straight} = \frac{10 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ m/s}} = 10 \text{ s}$$

$$t_{curved} = \frac{14 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ m/s}} = 14 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial pheromone deposit on each path** (deposition occurs *during* the trip, continuously). For simplicity, take deposit at the moment the ant completes the trip:

$$\tau_{straight}^{(0)} = \Delta\tau \times 10 = 1 \times 10 = 10 \text{ units}$$

$$\tau_{curved}^{(0)} = \Delta\tau \times 14 = 1 \times 14 = 14 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Decay the curved-path pheromone to $t = 10$ s.** The ant on the curved path finishes at $t = 14$ s. But we are told to evaluate intensities *at* $t = 10$ s, so the curved-path ant has not even completed the trip yet — we approximate by assuming average deposit and decay:

$$\tau_{curved}(10) = \frac{10}{14} \times 14 \times e^{-0.1 \times 10} = 10 \times e^{-1} = 10 \times 0.3679 \approx 3.68 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Straight path:** the ant finishes at exactly $t = 10$ s, so no decay applies to the deposit made *during* the trip (the deposit is instantaneous at the moment of return). Effective intensity:

$$\tau_{straight}(10) = 10 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Compute preference ratio for ant $A_3$** (assume $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 0$ for simplicity):

$$\frac{P_{straight}}{P_{curved}} = \frac{\tau_{straight}^{\alpha}}{\tau_{curved}^{\alpha}} = \frac{10}{3.68} \approx 2.72$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Ant $A_3$ is **2.72 times more likely** to take the straight path. This is the *Bridge Experiment* of Goss et al. (1989) — a classic empirical confirmation of BSO in ant colonies.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuator's note:** Notice that even though the curved path received *more total* pheromone (14 vs 10 units), the **decay of time** (the curved-path ant took longer) caused the **straight path to dominate**. This is the *self-organizing shortest-path discovery* — a key KTU viva question.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping BSO Concepts to Soft Computing Algorithms

| BSO Concept | Biological Source | Algorithmic Instantiation | KTU Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pheromone stigmergy | Ant trails | Trail-based probabilistic construction | **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** |
| Flocking & alignment | Bird flocks | Velocity-matching + cognitive + social | **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** |
| Waggle dance | Honey bees | Employed–Onlooker–Scout phases | **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)** |
| Slime mold foraging | *Physarum polycephalum* | Probability field on graph edges | **Physarum-inspired routing** |
| Cellular differentiation | Embryogenesis | Iterative rule application on grid | **Cellular Automata (CA)** |
| Evolution by selection | Darwinian evolution | Selection, crossover, mutation | **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** |
| Bacterial chemotaxis | *E. coli* flagella | Tumble + run + attractant/repellent | **Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **raw uppercase text labels** inside double quotes, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 mermaid safeguards.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Biological Self-Organizing System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIOLOGICAL SELF ORGANIZING SYSTEM] --> B[DECENTRALIZED AGENT POOL]
    A --> C[ENVIRONMENTAL SUBSTRATE]
    A --> D[LOCAL INTERACTION RULES]
    B --> E[AGENT 1]
    B --> F[AGENT 2]
    B --> G[AGENT N]
    E --> H[STATE S1]
    F --> I[STATE S2]
    G --> J[STATE SN]
    D --> K[POSITIVE FEEDBACK]
    D --> L[NEGATIVE FEEDBACK]
    D --> M[STOCHASTICITY]
    D --> N[STIGMERGY]
    C --> O[PHEROMONE FIELD]
    C --> P[TEMPERATURE FIELD]
    C --> Q[GRADIENT FIELD]
    H --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    O --> R[EMERGENT GLOBAL PATTERN]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    K --> R
    L --> R
    M --> R
    N --> R
    R --> S[ADAPTIVE ROBUST COHERENT STRUCTURE]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *agent pool* interacts with the *environmental substrate* through *local rules*; the substrate accumulates a *field* (e.g., pheromone); the field, modulated by the *four BSO ingredients*, gives rise to a *global pattern*; this pattern feeds back (dashed feedback loop) to influence future agent behavior. This is a **closed-loop self-organizing system**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Mapping BSO to Ant Colony Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ANT COLONY OPTIMIZATION] --> B[INITIALIZE PHEROMONE TAU]
    B --> C[CONSTRUCT SOLUTIONS PROBABILISTICALLY]
    C --> D[APPLY HEURISTIC ETA AND PHEROMONE TAU]
    D --> E[EVALUATE OBJECTIVE FUNCTION]
    E --> F[UPDATE PHEROMONE EVAPORATION]
    F --> G[DEPOSIT PHEROMONE ON EDGES USED]
    G --> H[ELITIST REINFORCEMENT OPTIONAL]
    H --> I{TERMINATION CRITERION MET}
    I -->|NO| C
    I -->|YES| J[OUTPUT GLOBAL BEST SOLUTION]
    K[POSITIVE FEEDBACK PHEROMONE DEPOSIT] --> G
    L[NEGATIVE FEEDBACK EVAPORATION RHO] --> F
    M[STOCHASTICITY RANDOM NUMBER R] --> D
    N[STIGMERGY INDIRECT EDGE MARKING] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **canonical ACO control loop**. Note the four BSO ingredients are explicitly wired into the loop. When the KTU examiner asks "show how BSO is implemented in ACO," this is the answer they expect.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Reaction-Diffusion Turing Pattern Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INITIAL UNIFORM MORPHOGEN FIELD U V] --> B[COMPUTE LAPLACIAN]
    B --> C[APPLY REACTION KINETICS F U V]
    C --> D[UPDATE FIELD U X Y T PLUS DT]
    D --> E[CHECK STABILITY AND PATTERN]
    E -->|INSTABLE| F[SPONTANEOUS SYMMETRY BREAKING]
    E -->|STABLE| G[HOMOGENEOUS STEADY STATE]
    F --> H[STRIPES OR SPOTS OR SPIRALS]
    H --> I[BIOLOGICAL PATTERN ZEBRA LEOPARD GIRAFFE]
    G --> J[NO PATTERN]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A nearly uniform field of two morphogens (e.g., $u$ = activator, $v$ = inhibitor) becomes *unstable* under small perturbations because the inhibitor diffuses much faster than the activator. This **Turing instability** triggers spontaneous symmetry breaking, and the field self-organizes into stripes, spots, or spirals — the *mathematical origin* of biological patterns.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Four Universal BSO Ingredients with Biological Examples

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FOUR UNIVERSAL BSO INGREDIENTS] --> B[POSITIVE FEEDBACK]
    A --> C[NEGATIVE FEEDBACK]
    A --> D[RANDOMNESS]
    A --> E[MULTIPLE INTERACTIONS]

    B --> B1[ANT PHEROMONE DEPOSITION]
    B --> B2[BEE WAGGLE DANCE]
    B --> B3[CELL AUTOCATALYSIS]

    C --> C1[PHEROMONE EVAPORATION]
    C --> C2[CELL APOPTOSIS]
    C --> C3[POPULATION SATURATION]

    D --> D1[ANT RANDOM WALK]
    D --> D2[GENETIC MUTATION]
    D --> D3[THERMAL FLUCTUATION]

    E --> E1[STIGMERGY INDIRECT]
    E --> E2[DIRECT VISUAL TACTILE]
    E --> E3[CHEMICAL QUORUM SENSING]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **canonical KTU board answer** to the question "List and explain the four ingredients of biological self-organization." Memorize the mapping.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Define Biological Self-Organization. List any four of its characteristic features.**
> **Model Answer:**
> Biological Self-Organization (BSO) is the spontaneous emergence of a globally coherent, ordered pattern from the local interactions of decentralized, autonomous components, **without any external controller or blueprint**.
> **Four characteristic features:**
> 1. **Decentralization** — no central agent issues commands.
> 2. **Local information** — each agent senses only its immediate neighborhood.
> 3. **Emergence** — global pattern is not a sum of parts; it is qualitatively new.
> 4. **Robustness** — loss of individual agents does not collapse the system.
> *(3 marks — definition 1 + 4 features 0.5 each)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. What is Stigmergy? Give one biological and one computational example.**
> **Model Answer:**
> **Stigmergy** is a mechanism of *indirect coordination* between agents in which an agent's actions modify the local environment, and that modification subsequently influences the behavior of other agents. The term was coined by **Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959)** while studying termite nest construction.
> - **Biological example:** Ants deposit pheromone on trails; subsequent ants follow stronger trails.
> - **Computational example:** Pheromone updates on edges of a graph in **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)**.
> *(3 marks — definition 1.5 + 2 examples 0.75 each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING**
> 1. **Do NOT** write generic definitions for both parts of a 14-mark question. KTU valuators deduct 1–2 marks if the 7-mark sub-parts are *not* clearly distinguished.
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the **diagrammatic representation**. Even a hand-drawn block diagram earns 1 mark. In typed answers, always include a `mermaid` or labeled block diagram.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse *stigmergy* with *emergence*. Stigmergy is a *mechanism*; emergence is a *consequence*.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget to write the **units** of pheromone concentration, evaporation rate, etc. — KTU 2024 explicitly tests for units in the mark scheme.
> 5. **Do NOT** ignore the **boundary conditions** (e.g., $\rho \in (0, 1]$). Writing the limits of parameters scores an extra half-mark in most solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **Q.A. (a) Explain the concept of Biological Self-Organization. Discuss the four universal ingredients proposed by Bonabeau, Dorigo and Theraulaz, with one biological example for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Biological Self-Organization (BSO) is the process by which a system's components interact locally through simple rules to produce a globally ordered pattern, **without any external controller, central plan, or blueprint**. The global pattern is an *emergent* property of the collective dynamics.

**The Four Universal Ingredients:**

**1. Positive Feedback (Amplification):** Reinforces successful structures and amplifies small fluctuations into macroscopic order.
- *Biological example:* In ant foraging, ants that find food return to the nest laying a pheromone trail. The pheromone recruits more ants, which lay *more* pheromone, creating a runaway positive feedback loop. The result is the spontaneous formation of a *pheromone highway* — a global path structure that no individual ant designed.

**2. Negative Feedback (Stabilization & Boundedness):** Counter-balances positive feedback; prevents the system from collapsing or exploding.
- *Biological example:* Pheromone *evaporation*. If evaporation did not exist, old trails would persist forever and the colony could never adapt to a changed environment. Evaporation bounds the pheromone intensity and forces the colony to continuously re-explore.

**3. Randomness / Fluctuations:** Introduces exploration; breaks symmetries; prevents premature convergence to sub-optimal patterns.
- *Biological example:* Ants that have *not* found a strong pheromone trail perform a *random walk*. This exploratory behavior ensures the colony discovers new food sources that are farther or off the beaten path.

**4. Multiple Interactions (Direct + Indirect / Stigmergy):** Agents interact with each other *and* with the environment. The indirect (stigmergic) channel is the hallmark of BSO.
- *Biological example:* Termite nest construction. A termite deposits a mud pellet on the existing structure; the *shape* of the resulting pillar influences where the *next* termite will deposit its pellet. The pillar grows via a stigmergic cascade, eventually producing a cathedral mound with internal ventilation — without any architect termite.

**Conclusion:** The interplay of these four ingredients is the *engine* of every biological self-organizing system, and is the inspiration for every swarm-intelligence algorithm in Soft Computing.

*(7 marks — definition 1, 4 ingredients × 1.5 marks each = 6, total 7)*

---

> **Q.A. (b) Derive the Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) pheromone update rule for the Ant System (AS) variant. Show explicitly how the four BSO ingredients appear in the update equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the update rule.** In the basic Ant System (AS), pheromone on every edge $(i,j)$ is updated after each ant has constructed a complete tour:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$$

**Step 2 — Define each term:**

- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ is the pheromone concentration on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.
- $\rho \in (0, 1]$ is the **evaporation coefficient**.
- $(1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$ is the *residual pheromone* after evaporation.
- $\sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ is the *total fresh pheromone* deposited by all $m$ ants.

**Step 3 — Define the per-ant deposit.** An ant $k$ traversing a tour of length $L_k$ deposits:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ used edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $Q$ is a constant.

**Step 4 — Identify the four BSO ingredients in the equation:**

| BSO Ingredient | Where it appears in the equation |
|---|---|
| **Positive feedback** | $\sum \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ — edges used by short tours receive more pheromone, attracting more ants, reinforcing the edge. |
| **Negative feedback** | $(1-\rho)$ multiplier — the evaporation term reduces unused edges, balancing the amplification. |
| **Randomness** | Not in the update *itself*, but in the **transition probability** $P_{ij}^{k}$ that uses the random number $q \sim U(0,1)$. |
| **Stigmergy (multiple interactions)** | Pheromone on the edge is the medium of *indirect* communication; ants never talk to each other. |

**Step 5 — Iterate.** This update is applied at every iteration $t = 1, 2, \dots, T_{max}$, and the algorithm outputs the **best-so-far tour** $L_{bsf}$ as the approximate optimum.

**Conclusion:** The Ant System equation is a *direct mathematical transcription* of the four BSO ingredients. ACO is, in this sense, an *engineered biological self-organizing system* operating on a graph.

*(7 marks — update rule 2, definitions 2, BSO mapping table 2, conclusion 1)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
> **Q.B. (a) With the help of the Reaction-Diffusion (Turing) framework, explain how biological patterns such as zebra stripes and leopard spots self-organize. Derive the conditions under which a uniform field becomes unstable. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Reaction-Diffusion model.** Turing (1952) proposed that biological pattern formation arises from the interaction of two morphogens $u$ and $v$ that react and diffuse:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = D_u\,\nabla^{2} u + f(u,v)$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = D_v\,\nabla^{2} v + g(u,v)$$

where $f$ and $g$ are non-linear reaction kinetics. The activator $u$ catalyzes its own production and that of $v$; the inhibitor $v$ suppresses $u$.

**Step 2 — Linear stability analysis.** Consider a small perturbation $(u,v) = (u_0, v_0) + (\delta u, \delta v)\cdot e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{x} + \lambda t}$ around the homogeneous steady state $(u_0, v_0)$. Substituting and keeping only first-order terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda\begin{pmatrix}\delta u\\ \delta v\end{pmatrix} &= \begin{pmatrix} f_u - D_u k^2 & f_v \\ g_u & g_v - D_v k^2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix}\delta u\\ \delta v\end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

where $f_u = \partial f/\partial u$, etc., evaluated at the steady state.

**Step 3 — Characteristic equation.** Setting the determinant to zero:

$$\lambda^2 - \lambda\bigl(f_u + g_v - (D_u + D_v)k^2\bigr) + \bigl(D_u D_v k^4 - (D_v f_u + D_u g_v)k^2 + f_u g_v - f_v g_u\bigr) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Turing instability condition.** A homogeneous steady state is *stable* to homogeneous perturbations ($k=0$) but *unstable* to a finite wavenumber $k_c$ if:

$$\boxed{\;D_v f_u + D_u g_v > 2\sqrt{D_u D_v (f_u g_v - f_v g_u)}\;}$$

and $D_v \gg D_u$ (inhibitor diffuses much faster than activator).

**Step 5 — Pattern wavelength.** The most-unstable wavenumber $k_c$ sets the characteristic *wavelength* of the resulting pattern:

$$\lambda_{pattern} = \frac{2\pi}{k_c}$$

This wavelength determines the *spacing* of stripes or spots — explaining why zebras have similar-width stripes and leopards have characteristic spot sizes, regardless of the individual's body size.

**Step 6 — Mapping to real biology.** The activator-inhibitor pair is realized by molecules like **Wnt vs. Dkk**, **FGF vs. Sprouty**, or **BMP vs. Noggin** in vertebrate limb development; **Edn3 vs. Mitf** in zebra fish stripes; and **agouti vs. Mc1r** in mammalian coat color.

**Conclusion:** The Turing mechanism is a *mathematically rigorous* BSO model demonstrating how a **uniform, symmetric initial state** can self-organize into a **structured, asymmetric biological pattern** purely from local reaction-diffusion rules.

*(7 marks — model statement 1, linearization 2, characteristic equation 2, instability condition 1, biological mapping 1)*

---

> **Q.B. (b) Derive the velocity and position update equations of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and explicitly identify how the four BSO ingredients are present in the PSO algorithm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the swarm state.** Let there be $N$ particles in a $D$-dimensional search space. Particle $i$ has:
- Current position: $\mathbf{x}_i(t) = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{iD})^T$.
- Current velocity: $\mathbf{v}_i(t) = (v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \dots, v_{iD})^T$.
- Personal best position: $\mathbf{p}_i = (p_{i1}, p_{i2}, \dots, p_{iD})^T$.
- Global best position (broadcast to all): $\mathbf{g} = (g_1, g_2, \dots, g_D)^T$.

**Step 2 — Construct the velocity update equation** by combining three forces:

$$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \omega\,\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1\bigl(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\bigr) + c_2 r_2\bigl(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\bigr)$$

where:
- $\omega$ = **inertia weight** (controls momentum).
- $c_1$ = **cognitive coefficient** (self-confidence).
- $c_2$ = **social coefficient** (swarm influence).
- $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ = **stochasticity** random scalars.

**Step 3 — Update the position:**

$$\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$$

**Step 4 — Clamp velocity to prevent explosion:**

$$v_{i,d} = \text{clamp}(v_{i,d}, -v_{max}, v_{max})$$

**Step 5 — Identify the four BSO ingredients:**

| BSO Ingredient | PSO Realization |
|---|---|
| **Positive feedback** | $c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ — particles attracted to global best, reinforcing the best region with more sampling. |
| **Negative feedback** | $\omega \mathbf{v}_i$ — inertia decay over time (if $\omega < 1$) and clamping to $v_{max}$ prevent runaway acceleration. |
| **Randomness** | $r_1, r_2$ — uniform random numbers in $[0,1]$ prevent premature convergence and explore. |
| **Multiple interactions (stigmergy)** | $\mathbf{p}_i$ and $\mathbf{g}$ — particles share information indirectly via broadcast of best-known positions. The "environment" of the swarm is the *fitness landscape memory*. |

**Step 6 — Convergence.** Under standard conditions $0 < \omega < 1$, $0 < c_1 + c_2 < 4$, the swarm converges to a region around $\mathbf{g}$. Convergence is *not* guaranteed to a global optimum but to a *stable attractor* in the search space — the hallmark of BSO convergence.

**Conclusion:** PSO is a *minimalist* BSO algorithm: two update equations, three parameters, one stochastic ingredient — yet it produces globally optimal search behavior through the self-organization of the swarm's collective memory.

*(7 marks — state definitions 1, velocity update 1.5, position update 0.5, clamping 0.5, BSO mapping table 2.5, conclusion 1)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Walking into the KTU ESE Hall**

- [ ] **Definition (verbatim):** BSO is the emergence of global order from local interactions of decentralized agents, *without* external control.
- [ ] **Four Ingredients (Bonabeau, Dorigo, Theraulaz 1997):** Positive feedback · Negative feedback · Randomness · Multiple interactions (stigmergy).
- [ ] **Stigmergy:** *Indirect* communication via environment modification. Coined by **Grassé (1959)** for termites.
- [ ] **Emergence vs. Stigmergy:** Emergence is the *consequence* (global pattern); stigmergy is the *mechanism* (indirect comm).
- [ ] **ACO update:** $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$. Memorize: $\rho$ is the evaporation coefficient.
- [ ] **Pheromone decay law:** $\tau(t) = \tau_0 e^{-\rho t}$. Half-life $= \ln 2 / \rho$.
- [ ] **ACO transition probability:** $P_{ij}^{k} = \tau_{ij}^{\alpha}\eta_{ij}^{\beta} / \sum_l \tau_{il}^{\alpha}\eta_{il}^{\beta}$. $\alpha$ = pheromone weight, $\beta$ = heuristic weight.
- [ ] **PSO velocity update:** $\mathbf{v}_i \leftarrow \omega \mathbf{v}_i + c_1 r_1(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$.
- [ ] **PSO position update:** $\mathbf{x}_i \leftarrow \mathbf{x}_i + \mathbf{v}_i$.
- [ ] **Turing model:** $\partial_t u = D_u \nabla^2 u + f(u,v)$; $\partial_t v = D_v \nabla^2 v + g(u,v)$; condition $D_v \gg D_u$.
- [ ] **Bridge Experiment (Goss et al. 1989):** Empirical proof of BSO shortest-path discovery via asymmetric pheromone decay.
- [ ] **Properties of BSO systems:** Decentralization, local information, emergence, robustness, adaptivity, autopoiesis.
- [ ] **Standard ranges to remember:** $\rho \in (0, 1]$; $\alpha, \beta > 0$; $\omega \in [0.4, 0.9]$; $c_1, c_2 \in [0, 2.5]$.
- [ ] **Inertia weight interpretation:** $\omega > 1$ → exploration (BSO), $\omega < 1$ → exploitation (convergence).
- [ ] **Common KTU map:** Pheromone stigmergy → ACO; Flocking → PSO; Cellular differentiation → Cellular Automata; Bacterial chemotaxis → BFO.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Swarm Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Particle Swarm Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Soft Computing)**
> Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a population-based, stochastic, meta-heuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. It belongs to the family of **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** techniques and is a foundational topic under **Evolutionary Computation / Nature-Inspired Computing**.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995):**
> *Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a computational method that optimizes a problem by iteratively trying to improve a candidate solution with regard to a given measure of quality. It solves a problem by having a population (called a **swarm**) of candidate solutions (called **particles**), and moving these particles around in the **search-space** according to simple mathematical formulae over the particle's **position** and **velocity**.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PSO is classified as:
- **Type:** Population-based meta-heuristic
- **Paradigm:** Swarm Intelligence (SI)
- **Inspiration:** Sociocognitive behavior of organisms
- **Strategy:** Stochastic global optimization
- **Memory:** Each particle retains its own best historical position

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Bird Flocking Analogy (The "Flocking Birds" Picture):**
> Imagine a flock of birds searching for a hidden piece of food in a vast field. **No bird knows where the food is**, but each bird can sense (estimate) how far it is from the food at its current location. The strategy that emerges naturally is:
> 1. Each bird (particle) keeps flying in a direction, remembers **the closest it personally got** to the food (`pbest`).
> 2. The flock as a whole shares information — **the closest any bird in the flock got** to the food is known to all (`gbest`).
> 3. Each bird adjusts its flight direction and speed by combining three influences:
>    - Its **current momentum** (inertia — keeps going the way it was going).
>    - A pull toward **its own best past location** (cognitive — its memory).
>    - A pull toward **the flock's best known location** (social — cooperation).
> 4. Over time, the flock converges toward the food.
>
> In PSO, "birds" → **particles**, "flight" → **search**, "food" → the **global optimum**, and "field" → the **objective function landscape**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU Definition | Symbol |
|------|----------------|--------|
| Swarm | The entire population of candidate solutions | $S$ |
| Particle | A single candidate solution (a point in search space) | $i$ |
| Position | The current location of a particle in the $D$-dimensional search space | $\vec{x}_i$ |
| Velocity | The rate of change of position; direction and speed of search | $\vec{v}_i$ |
| Personal Best | The best position a particle has visited so far | $\vec{p}_i$ |
| Global Best | The best position found by any particle in the swarm | $\vec{g}$ |
| Inertia Weight | Controls the influence of the previous velocity | $w$ |
| Cognitive Coefficient | Pull toward the particle's own best | $c_1$ |
| Social Coefficient | Pull toward the swarm's best | $c_2$ |
| Search Space Bounds | Lower and upper limits of the problem domain | $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ |
| Velocity Clamping | Limiting $|v_{ij}|$ to $[-V_{\max}, +V_{\max}]$ | $V_{\max}$ |

### 1.4 Why PSO is Important in KTU Soft Computing

> [!NOTE]
> PSO is a **mandatory algorithm** in the KTU Soft Computing syllabus because it:
> - Has **fewer parameters** than Genetic Algorithm (GA).
> - Is **easier to implement** and computationally cheaper.
> - Demonstrates the **convergence of swarm intelligence** to a global optimum.
> - Serves as a **direct comparison tool** against GA, Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Artificial Bee Colony (ABC).
> - Has **real-time applications** in neural network training, antenna design, and economic load dispatch.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PSO Algorithm — Conceptual Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Idea:** A particle moves in the search space by adjusting its **velocity** based on (i) its own past experience and (ii) the experience of its neighbors (or the whole swarm). After each iteration, the particle updates its **position** and the swarm collectively moves toward the best-known region.

### 2.2 The Three Influences on a Particle's Movement

Each particle's next velocity is a **weighted sum of three vectors**:

1. **Inertia Component (Momentum) — $w \cdot \vec{v}_i(t)$**
   - Represents the **tendency of the particle to continue in its current direction**.
   - Large $w$ → **global exploration** (searching broadly).
   - Small $w$ → **local exploitation** (refining current region).

2. **Cognitive Component (Memory) — $c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i(t))$**
   - Represents the **pull toward the particle's own best historical position** ($\vec{p}_i$).
   - Simulates the bird's "private memory".
   - Coefficient $c_1$ controls the strength of self-confidence.

3. **Social Component (Cooperation) — $c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i(t))$**
   - Represents the **pull toward the best position found by the entire swarm** ($\vec{g}$).
   - Simulates the bird's "social knowledge".
   - Coefficient $c_2$ controls the strength of group influence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why randomness $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0, 1)$?**
> Uniformly distributed random numbers in $[0,1]$ are introduced to give particles **stochastic exploration ability**, preventing premature convergence to local optima. Without $r_1, r_2$, the swarm would move deterministically and lose diversity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is the MOST critical reference for solving KTU PSO numerical problems. Memorize it.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|--------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\vec{v}_i(t+1) = w\vec{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\vec{p}_i - \vec{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2 (\vec{g} - \vec{x}_i(t))$ | **Velocity update** (the heart of PSO) | All terms in same units as $\vec{x}$ |
| 2 | $\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1)$ | **Position update** | Position vector |
| 3 | $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0, 1)$ | Random numbers | Dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ (typical) | Inertia weight | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $c_1, c_2 \in [0, 2]$ (typical) | Acceleration coefficients | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $c_1 = c_2 = 2$ (Shi & Eberhart default) | Equal cognitive and social influence | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $v_{ij} = \text{clamp}(v_{ij}, -V_{\max}, +V_{\max})$ | Velocity clamping | $V_{\max} = k \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ |
| 8 | $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}$ | **Constriction coefficient** (Clerc) | $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ |
| 9 | $\vec{p}_i = \arg\min_{t} f(\vec{x}_i(t))$ | Personal best update rule | — |
| 10 | $\vec{g} = \arg\min_{i} f(\vec{p}_i)$ | Global best selection | — |
| 11 | $w(t) = w_{\max} - \dfrac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t}{T_{\max}}$ | Linearly decreasing inertia (LDIW) | $w_{\max}=0.9, w_{\min}=0.4$ |
| 12 | $f(\vec{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{D} x_i^2$ | Sphere function (test problem) | Minimization |

### 2.4 Topology Variants

| Topology | Definition | Convergence | Diversity |
|----------|------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Global Best (gbest)** | All particles connected to one global best | Fast convergence | Low diversity |
| **Local Best (lbest)** | Each particle connected to $k$ neighbors | Slow convergence | High diversity |
| **Ring Topology** | Each particle connected to 2 neighbors | Moderate | High |
| **Star Topology** | All particles connected to one central hub | Same as gbest | Moderate |

### 2.5 Stopping Criteria (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PSO iteration terminates when **any one** of the following conditions is met:
> 1. **Maximum number of iterations** ($T_{\max}$) is reached.
> 2. **Acceptable error threshold** is achieved: $\vert f(\vec{g}) - f^* \vert \le \epsilon$.
> 3. **No improvement** in $\vec{g}$ over a specified number of consecutive iterations.
> 4. **Computational budget** (time / function evaluations) is exhausted.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| Electrical Power Systems | Economic load dispatch, unit commitment |
| Antenna Engineering | Array antenna pattern synthesis |
| Control Systems | PID controller tuning |
| Neural Networks | Weight optimization for training |
| Image Processing | Image segmentation, feature selection |
| Manufacturing | Job-shop scheduling, tool path optimization |
| Biomedical | ECG/EEG signal classification parameter tuning |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the PSO Velocity Update Equation

The classical PSO update equations are derived from two first-order difference equations governing the **position** and **velocity** of each particle $i$ in dimension $j$:

**Position Update (Newtonian Discretization):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ij}(t+1) &= x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1) \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

> Setting the time step $\Delta t = 1$ (a standard PSO convention adopted by Kennedy & Eberhart):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ij}(t+1) &= x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Velocity Update (Three-Force Synthesis):**

The new velocity is the linear superposition of the previous velocity and two acceleration terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ij}(t+1) &= w \cdot v_{ij}(t) \\
&\quad + c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot \left( p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t) \right) \\
&\quad + c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot \left( g_j - x_{ij}(t) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each term:**

- **$w \cdot v_{ij}(t)$** — The **inertial (momentum) component**. The particle continues along its current trajectory scaled by $w$. Setting $w = 0$ removes memory of the prior direction.

- **$c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t))$** — The **cognitive component**. The vector $(p_{ij} - x_{ij})$ points from the particle's current position toward its personal best. Multiplication by $c_1$ tunes self-confidence; $r_1$ introduces stochasticity.

- **$c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (g_j - x_{ij}(t))$** — The **social component**. The vector $(g_j - x_{ij})$ points toward the global best. Multiplication by $c_2$ tunes social influence; $r_2$ introduces stochasticity.

### 3.2 Constriction Coefficient Derivation (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002)

> [!NOTE]
> Clerc and Kennedy (2002) proved that **PSO convergence is guaranteed** if a multiplicative constriction factor $\chi$ is applied to the velocity update.

The recurrence is rewritten as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ij}(t+1) &= \chi \cdot \left[ v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij}(t)) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where the constriction coefficient is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi &= \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2,\ \phi > 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard stable choice:** $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05 \Rightarrow \phi = 4.1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi &= \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.1 - \sqrt{(4.1)^2 - 4(4.1)} \vert} \\
&= \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - \sqrt{16.81 - 16.4} \vert} \\
&= \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - \sqrt{0.41} \vert} \\
&= \frac{2}{2.1 + 0.6403} \\
&= \frac{2}{2.7403} \\
&\approx 0.7298
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often incorrectly compute $\sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}$ as $\phi - 2$ instead of $\sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)}$. The correct expansion is $\sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}$, **not** $\phi - 2$.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical PSO Iteration

> [!TIP]
> **Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Format):**
> Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ on a 1-D search space. Initialize two particles as follows:
> - Particle 1: $x_1 = -3$, $v_1 = +1$, $p_1 = -2$
> - Particle 2: $x_2 = +4$, $v_2 = -1$, $p_2 = +1$
> - Global best: $g = -2$
> - Parameters: $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1$, $r_1 = 0.4$, $r_2 = 0.6$
> - Compute the new velocity and position of Particle 1.

**Step 1 — Evaluate the objective function at the global best for reference:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(g) = f(-2) = (-2)^2 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the velocity update formula for Particle 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1(t+1) &= w \cdot v_1(t) + c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (p_1 - x_1(t)) + c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (g - x_1(t))
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_1(t+1) &= (0.5)(1) + (1)(0.4)\bigl((-2) - (-3)\bigr) + (1)(0.6)\bigl((-2) - (-3)\bigr) \\
&= 0.5 + (0.4)(1) + (0.6)(1) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.6 \\
&= 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the position update formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1(t+1) &= x_1(t) + v_1(t+1) \\
&= -3 + 1.5 \\
&= -1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the objective at the new position:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x_1(t+1)) = f(-1.5) = (-1.5)^2 = 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update personal best (since $f(-1.5) = 2.25 < f(p_1) = f(-2) = 4$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 \leftarrow x_1(t+1) = -1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** New velocity $= +1.5$, new position $= -1.5$, updated personal best $p_1 = -1.5$. The particle has moved closer to the true minimum ($x^* = 0$).

### 3.4 Convergence Behavior of PSO

| Phase | Behavior | $w$ setting | Cognitive vs Social |
|-------|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Exploration (early)** | Wide search, large velocity changes | $w \approx 0.9$ | Either balanced |
| **Exploitation (late)** | Fine search, small velocity changes | $w \approx 0.4$ | Either balanced |
| **Premature convergence** | Particles cluster prematurely at a non-optimum | $w$ too small, $c_2 \gg c_1$ | Social dominates |
| **Divergence** | Particles scatter, no convergence | $w$ too large, $c_1, c_2$ too large | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** assume $c_1$ is always the cognitive term and $c_2$ is always the social term. The notation can be reversed in some textbooks. Always check the formula in your answer script against the formula sheet provided in the KTU module.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — PSO Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize Swarm]
    B --> C["Random x_i in bounds"]
    B --> D["Random v_i in -Vmax +Vmax"]
    B --> E["p_i = x_i initially"]
    B --> F["g = best of all p_i"]
    C --> G[Loop t = 1 to Tmax]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["For each particle i"]
    H --> I["Generate r1, r2 in U 0 1"]
    I --> J["Compute v_i t+1"]
    J --> J1["w v_i t"]
    J --> J2["c1 r1 p_i - x_i t"]
    J --> J3["c2 r2 g - x_i t"]
    J1 --> K["v_i t+1 sum of three"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L["Clamp v_ij to -Vmax +Vmax"]
    L --> M["x_i t+1 = x_i t + v_i t+1"]
    M --> N["Compute f x_i t+1"]
    N --> O["If f new less than f p_i then p_i = x_i t+1"]
    O --> P["Update g = best of all p_i"]
    P --> Q{"Stop criterion met"}
    Q -->|No| G
    Q -->|Yes| R["Return g and f g"]
    R --> S([End])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — PSO Algorithm Pseudocode as Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Input Objective f] --> I2[Set w c1 c2 Tmax]
    I2 --> I3[Set Vmax Xmin Xmax]
    I3 --> INIT[Initialize Swarm]
    INIT --> LOOP{Iterate t = 1 to Tmax}
    LOOP --> EVAL[Evaluate f x_i for all i]
    EVAL --> UPB[Update p_i and g]
    UPB --> VEL["Update v_i t+1"]
    VEL --> POS["Update x_i t+1"]
    POS --> BCON[Check Boundary Constraints]
    BCON --> CHK{Stop Criterion}
    CHK -->|Not Met| LOOP
    CHK -->|Met| OUT[Output Global Best g]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Influence of the Three PSO Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Previous Velocity v_i t"] --> M1["w v_i t"]
    P1["Personal Best p_i"] --> M2["c1 r1 p_i - x_i t"]
    G1["Global Best g"] --> M3["c2 r2 g - x_i t"]
    X1["Current Position x_i t"] --> M2
    X1 --> M3
    M1 --> SUM["+"]
    M2 --> SUM
    M3 --> SUM
    SUM --> CLAMP["Clamp to -Vmax +Vmax"]
    CLAMP --> VN["New Velocity v_i t+1"]
    X1 --> ADD["+"]
    VN --> ADD
    ADD --> XN["New Position x_i t+1"]
```

### 4.4 Particle Trajectory Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Search Space 2D"]
        O[("Optimum target")]
        P1C["Particle 1 trajectory"]
        P2C["Particle 2 trajectory"]
        P3C["Particle 3 trajectory"]
    end
    P1C -->|"Converges"| O
    P2C -->|"Converges"| O
    P3C -->|"Converges"| O
```

> [!TIP]
> In an exam, you can sketch the trajectories as three dashed curves starting at the initial particle positions and spiraling toward the global best location, with arrows showing the velocity vectors at a few discrete time-steps.
