## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Particle Swarm Optimization. List its key parameters.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a population-based, stochastic optimization algorithm inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling, proposed by **Kennedy and Eberhart in 1995**.

**Key parameters of PSO are:**
1. **Inertia weight** ($w$) — controls momentum of the particle.
2. **Cognitive coefficient** ($c_1$) — controls pull toward personal best.
3. **Social coefficient** ($c_2$) — controls pull toward global best.
4. **Swarm size** ($S$) — number of particles.
5. **Maximum velocity** ($V_{\max}$) — bounds the step size.
6. **Random vectors** ($r_1, r_2$) — provide stochastic exploration.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between pbest and gbest in PSO.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | pbest ($\vec{p}_i$) | gbest ($\vec{g}$) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Scope | Personal to a single particle | Common to the entire swarm |
| Definition | Best position visited by particle $i$ so far | Best position found by any particle in the swarm |
| Update frequency | After every iteration, for each particle | After every iteration, one value for all |
| Information source | Particle's own memory | Social knowledge from the swarm |
| Role in update | Drives the **cognitive** term | Drives the **social** term |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Model 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — PSO Algorithm, Equations, and Numerical**

**(a)** Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm in detail. State the velocity and position update equations with all parameters clearly defined. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) was developed by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart in 1995**, inspired by the coordinated movement of bird flocks searching for food. Each candidate solution in PSO is called a **particle**, and the entire collection of particles is called a **swarm**. Each particle $i$ in a $D$-dimensional search space has two associated vectors: a **position vector** $\vec{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{iD})$ and a **velocity vector** $\vec{v}_i = (v_{i1}, v_{i2}, \dots, v_{iD})$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. **Initialize** the swarm with random positions $\vec{x}_i$ and velocities $\vec{v}_i$ within the search bounds.
2. **Evaluate** the objective function $f(\vec{x}_i)$ for every particle.
3. **Set personal best**: $\vec{p}_i = \vec{x}_i$ if $f(\vec{x}_i) < f(\vec{p}_i)$ (for minimization).
4. **Set global best**: $\vec{g} = \arg\min_i f(\vec{p}_i)$.
5. **Update velocity** for each particle:
   $$ v_{ij}(t+1) = w v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij}(t)) $$
6. **Clamp velocity**: $v_{ij} \in [-V_{\max}, V_{\max}]$.
7. **Update position**: $x_{ij}(t+1) = x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1)$.
8. **Apply boundary constraints** to the new position.
9. **Repeat** steps 2–8 until the stopping criterion is met.
10. **Return** the global best $\vec{g}$ as the optimal solution.

**Parameter Description:**
- $w$ — inertia weight (typically $0.4$ to $0.9$).
- $c_1$ — cognitive coefficient (typical value $2$).
- $c_2$ — social coefficient (typical value $2$).
- $r_1, r_2 \in U(0,1)$ — random numbers.
- $V_{\max}$ — maximum allowed velocity.
- $t$ — current iteration index.

**(b)** A 1-D PSO problem minimizes $f(x) = x^2$ with the following data:
- Particle A: $x_A = 5$, $v_A = -1$, $p_A = 3$
- Particle B: $x_B = -2$, $v_B = +1$, $p_B = -2$
- Global best: $g = -2$
- Parameters: $w = 0.6$, $c_1 = 1.5$, $c_2 = 1.0$, $r_1 = 0.3$, $r_2 = 0.7$

Compute one full iteration of PSO for Particle A and update its pbest. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the velocity update equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_A(t+1) &= w \cdot v_A(t) + c_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot (p_A - x_A(t)) + c_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot (g - x_A(t)) \\
&= (0.6)(-1) + (1.5)(0.3)(3 - 5) + (1.0)(0.7)(-2 - 5) \\
&= -0.6 + (0.45)(-2) + (0.7)(-7) \\
&= -0.6 - 0.9 - 4.9 \\
&= -6.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the position update equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_A(t+1) &= x_A(t) + v_A(t+1) \\
&= 5 + (-6.4) \\
&= -1.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the objective function at the new and old personal best:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x_A(t+1)) &= f(-1.4) = (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 \\
f(p_A) &= f(3) = 3^2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Update pbest (since $1.96 < 9$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_A &\leftarrow x_A(t+1) = -1.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $v_A = -6.4$, $x_A = -1.4$, $p_A = -1.4$. Particle A has moved closer to the true optimum $x^* = 0$.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2022 | Model 2]**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — PSO Variants, Topology, and Comparison**

**(a)** Compare the Global Best (gbest) and Local Best (lbest) PSO topologies. Explain the constriction coefficient method. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | gbest PSO | lbest PSO |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Best particle | One global best $\vec{g}$ for the whole swarm | A local best $\vec{l}_i$ among the $k$ neighbors of particle $i$ |
| Information sharing | All particles share information simultaneously | Information spreads gradually through the neighborhood |
| Convergence speed | Faster | Slower |
| Diversity / exploration | Lower (risk of premature convergence) | Higher (better global search) |
| Suitability | Problems with single clear optimum | Multimodal problems |

**Constriction Coefficient Method (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002):**
The constriction coefficient method guarantees **mathematical convergence** of PSO. The velocity update is modified as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ij}(t+1) &= \chi \cdot \left[ v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij}(t)) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where the constriction factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi &= \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4
\end{aligned}
$$

For $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, we get $\phi = 4.1$ and $\chi \approx 0.7298$. This effectively constrains the particle's trajectory, preventing explosion and ensuring convergence.

**(b)** Compare PSO with Genetic Algorithm (GA). List any **four advantages** of PSO over GA. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | PSO | GA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Inspiration | Bird flocking social behavior | Darwinian natural selection |
| Representation | Real-valued position vectors | Chromosomes (binary or real) |
| Operators | Velocity update, no crossover/mutation | Selection, crossover, mutation |
| Memory | Each particle retains pbest | No memory across generations |
| Parameter count | Fewer ($w, c_1, c_2$) | More (crossover rate, mutation rate, population size) |
| Convergence speed | Generally faster | Slower due to generational evolution |
| Implementation | Simpler | More complex |

**Four Advantages of PSO over GA:**
1. **Simpler implementation** — no crossover or mutation operators.
2. **Fewer parameters** to tune.
3. **Continuous memory** — pbest provides historical guidance; GA has no equivalent.
4. **Faster convergence** in many continuous optimization problems.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PSO Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clamp velocity** — Always mention $V_{\max}$ clamping in your algorithm.
> 2. **Mixing up the role of $c_1$ and $c_2$** — $c_1$ is **cognitive (pbest)**, $c_2$ is **social (gbest)**.
> 3. **Wrong units in the formula** — All terms in the velocity update must have the same units as the position.
> 4. **Skipping the random numbers** — If $r_1, r_2$ are not specified in the question, use generic notation $r_1, r_2 \in U(0,1)$.
> 5. **Miscomputing the constriction factor** — Do not simplify $\sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}$ to $\phi - 2$.
> 6. **Forgetting to update pbest** — After computing the new position, always compare $f(x_{new})$ with $f(p_{old})$ and update if better.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSO — Rapid Revision Checklist (Killer Revision Before Exam):**
> - **Year of invention:** 1995 by **James Kennedy & Russell Eberhart**.
> - **Inspiration:** Social behavior of bird flocking / fish schooling.
> - **Core idea:** Each particle adjusts its velocity based on **inertia + cognitive + social** components.
> - **Velocity update:** $v_{ij}(t+1) = w v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{ij} - x_{ij}) + c_2 r_2 (g_j - x_{ij})$.
> - **Position update:** $x_{ij}(t+1) = x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1)$.
> - **Parameters:** $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2$ (Shi & Eberhart default).
> - **Constriction factor (Clerc):** $\chi = 2 \big/ \vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert$, with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05 \Rightarrow \chi \approx 0.7298$.
> - **pbest vs gbest:** pbest is per-particle; gbest is global.
> - **Topologies:** gbest (fast, low diversity) vs lbest (slow, high diversity).
> - **Stopping criteria:** Max iterations, error threshold, no improvement, computational budget.
> - **Advantages:** Simple, fewer parameters, continuous memory, fast convergence.
> - **Disadvantages:** Premature convergence to local optima in multimodal problems.
> - **Applications:** Neural network training, antenna design, economic load dispatch, scheduling.
> - **Inertia weight strategy:** Linearly decreasing $w$ from $0.9$ to $0.4$ balances exploration and exploitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ant Colony Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ant Colony Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a **population-based, probabilistic metaheuristic** belonging to the family of **Swarm Intelligence** algorithms. It is used to solve complex **combinatorial optimization problems** by mimicking the indirect communication mechanism of real ants searching for food through **stigmergy** — the deposition and evaporation of a chemical substance called **pheromone**.

The algorithm was formally introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his Ph.D. thesis (1992) and later refined with **Vittorio Maniezzo** and **Alberto Colorni** (1996). It is particularly effective for **NP-hard problems** such as the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**, **Vehicle Routing**, **Graph Coloring**, **Job Shop Scheduling**, and **Network Routing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping**: This topic directly addresses **CO4** — *Apply nature-inspired optimization techniques to solve real-world engineering problems.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a colony of ants searching for food scattered around their nest. Ants cannot see well, but they communicate through a chemical trail called **pheromone**. The remarkable behavior unfolds as follows:

| Real-World Observation | Algorithmic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Ants randomly wander searching for food | Initial random solution construction |
| Ants leave pheromone trails on their path | Pheromone deposition $\Delta \tau$ |
| Shorter paths are traversed faster; more pheromone accumulates per unit time | Pheromone intensification on good edges |
| Pheromone evaporates over time (natural decay) | Pheromone evaporation factor $(1-\rho)$ |
| Future ants preferentially follow stronger trails | Probabilistic transition rule $P_{ij}^k$ |

**Real-World Engineering Analogy (Traffic Routing)**: Think of Google Maps selecting the fastest route between cities. Roads that drivers frequently take get more "digital pheromone" (traffic data), while abandoned routes decay. ACO works similarly — paths that yield good solutions are reinforced, and over time, the colony converges on the optimal or near-optimal route.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

- **Stigmergy**: Indirect communication between ants through modifications to the environment (pheromone trails).
- **Pheromone Trail ($\tau_{ij}$)**: A numeric value representing the desirability of moving from node $i$ to node $j$.
- **Heuristic Information ($\eta_{ij}$)**: A problem-dependent value, usually $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$, where $d_{ij}$ is the distance between nodes.
- **Evaporation Rate ($\rho$)**: A decay parameter $\in (0, 1)$ that prevents premature convergence.
- **Construction Graph**: The graph $G = (C, L)$ on which ants build solutions, where $C$ is the set of components and $L$ is the set of possible connections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biological Inspiration — Why Ants Find Shortest Paths

In nature, ants deposit **pheromone** while walking. Since ants traveling on the shorter path complete the round-trip faster, they make more trips in the same time interval. Consequently, **pheromone accumulates at a higher rate** on shorter paths. Other ants, sensing this trail, follow it with higher probability, reinforcing it further — a **positive feedback loop**. Simultaneously, longer paths suffer from **pheromone evaporation**, naturally fading out.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Architecture of ACO

The ACO metaheuristic operates in an **iterative loop** governed by three core procedures:

1. **Solution Construction** — Each ant $k$ builds a solution by traversing the construction graph probabilistically.
2. **Pheromone Update** — After all ants have constructed their solutions, pheromone trails are updated (intensification on best paths, evaporation on others).
3. **Daemon Actions** *(optional)* — Problem-specific local search or global-best pheromone deposition.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — All Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are board-exam critical. Memorize the exact mathematical form, not just the concept.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Parameter Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transition Probability Rule** | $P_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | Probability that ant $k$ moves from $i$ to $j$; $\alpha, \beta$ control relative influence |
| 2 | **Heuristic Information** | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | Inversely proportional to edge distance |
| 3 | **Pheromone Evaporation** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$ | Decay; $\rho \in (0, 1)$ is the evaporation rate |
| 4 | **Pheromone Deposition** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t+1) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k(t)$ | Total pheromone added by all $m$ ants |
| 5 | **Pheromone Deposit per Ant** | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^k = \dfrac{Q}{L_k}$ if ant $k$ used edge $(i,j)$, else $0$ | $Q$ = constant; $L_k$ = tour length of ant $k$ |
| 6 | **Combined Update (AS Model)** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k(t)$ | Ant System (AS) full update rule |
| 7 | **Elitist Update (ACS)** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + e \cdot \Delta \tau_{ij}^{gb}$ | $e$ = elitist weight; $gb$ = global best ant |

### Parameter Meanings

- **$\alpha$**: Pheromone influence factor — Higher $\alpha$ means ants trust established trails more.
- **$\beta$**: Heuristic influence factor — Higher $\beta$ means ants prefer closer nodes greedily.
- **$\rho$**: Evaporation rate — Higher $\rho$ means trails fade faster (more exploration).
- **$Q$**: Pheromone quantity constant.
- **$m$**: Total number of ants in the colony.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Dynamic routing in packet-switched networks (AntNet) |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Vehicle routing, warehouse picking optimization |
| **Manufacturing** | Job-shop scheduling, assembly line balancing |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein folding, DNA sequencing |
| **Image Processing** | Edge detection, image segmentation |
| **Power Systems** | Economic dispatch, unit commitment |
| **Robotics** | Path planning in unknown terrains |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ACO matters in engineering**: Many engineering problems are NP-hard with exponentially growing search spaces. ACO delivers near-optimal solutions in polynomial time without exhaustive enumeration, making it indispensable for real-time industrial applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical KTU numerical.** Practice until you can solve it within 15 minutes during the exam.

### Problem Statement

Consider **4 cities**: A, B, C, D. The **distance matrix** $d_{ij}$ (symmetric) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{AB} &= 10, \quad d_{AC} = 15, \quad d_{AD} = 20 \\
d_{BC} &= 35, \quad d_{BD} = 25, \quad d_{CD} = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial Pheromone Matrix** $\tau_{ij}(0) = 1$ on all edges.

**Parameters**: $m = 2$ ants, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.5$, $Q = 100$.

### Step 1 — Compute Initial Heuristic Matrix $\eta_{ij}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{AB} &= \frac{1}{10} = 0.100, \quad \eta_{AC} = \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667, \quad \eta_{AD} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.050 \\
\eta_{BC} &= \frac{1}{35} = 0.0286, \quad \eta_{BD} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.040, \quad \eta_{CD} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.0333
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Construct Initial Tours

**Ant 1 starts at A.** Available neighbors: B, C, D.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{AB} &= \frac{(1)^1 \cdot (0.100)^2}{(1)^1(0.100)^2 + (1)^1(0.0667)^2 + (1)^1(0.050)^2} \\
&= \frac{0.0100}{0.0100 + 0.00445 + 0.00250} \\
&= \frac{0.0100}{0.01695} \approx 0.589
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{AC} &= \frac{0.00445}{0.01695} \approx 0.263 \\
P_{AD} &= \frac{0.00250}{0.01695} \approx 0.148
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic**: The numerator uses the product $\tau^{\alpha} \cdot \eta^{\beta}$. Since $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta = 2$, we compute $\tau \cdot \eta^2$ for each candidate edge and normalize by dividing by the sum across all allowed neighbors.

**Ant 1** rolls a random number $r = 0.45$. Since $P_{AB} = 0.589 > 0.45$, **Ant 1 moves A → B**. From B, allowed neighbors: C, D (A visited).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{BC} &= \frac{(1)^1 \cdot (0.0286)^2}{(1)^1(0.0286)^2 + (1)^1(0.040)^2} = \frac{0.000818}{0.000818 + 0.001600} = \frac{0.000818}{0.002418} \approx 0.338 \\
P_{BD} &= \frac{0.001600}{0.002418} \approx 0.662
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ant 1** rolls $r = 0.70$. Since $r > P_{BC} = 0.338$, **Ant 1 moves B → D**. From D, only unvisited neighbor is C. **Ant 1 moves D → C**. Finally returns to A.

**Ant 1's Tour**: A → B → D → C → A  
**Length** $L_1 = d_{AB} + d_{BD} + d_{CD} + d_{CA} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$

**Ant 2** similarly constructs a tour (assume) A → C → B → D → A, length $L_2 = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$.

### Step 3 — Pheromone Deposition (per ant)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \tau_{ij}^1 &= \frac{Q}{L_1} = \frac{100}{80} = 1.25 \quad \text{(on edges of Tour 1)} \\
\Delta \tau_{ij}^2 &= \frac{Q}{L_2} = \frac{100}{95} \approx 1.053 \quad \text{(on edges of Tour 2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Full Pheromone Update (End of Iteration 1)

Apply the AS update rule on each edge $(i,j)$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(1) = (1 - 0.5) \cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \Delta \tau_{ij}^1 + \Delta \tau_{ij}^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{AB}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 1.25 + 0 = 1.75 \\
\tau_{AC}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 0 + 1.053 = 1.553 \\
\tau_{AD}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 0 + 1.053 = 1.553 \\
\tau_{BD}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 1.25 + 1.053 = 2.303 \\
\tau_{CD}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 1.25 + 0 = 1.75 \\
\tau_{BC}(1) &= 0.5(1) + 0 + 1.053 = 1.553
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Edge **BD** received pheromone from BOTH ants and now has the highest $\tau$ value of **2.303**. In iteration 2, ants will preferentially select BD, reinforcing the shorter path — illustrating **positive feedback convergence**.

### Algorithm Pseudocode (Standard ACO-AS)

```
Initialize pheromone τij(t=0) with small constant
For each iteration t = 1 to T_max:
    For each ant k = 1 to m:
        Construct solution by applying transition rule
        Compute tour length Lk
    End for
    For each edge (i,j):
        Apply evaporation: τij ← (1-ρ)·τij
        Apply deposition: τij ← τij + Σk Δτij^k
    End for
End for
Return best solution found
```

---

## 3.2 Variations of ACO (High-Yield for Theory)

| Variant | Full Name | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **AS** | Ant System | Baseline; all ants deposit pheromone |
| **ACS** | Ant Colony System | Uses pseudo-random proportional rule + local pheromone update during construction |
| **MMAS** | MAX-MIN Ant System | Pheromone bounded in $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$; only best ant updates |
| **Elitist AS** | Elitist Ant System | Global-best tour receives extra $e \cdot \Delta\tau$ reinforcement |
| **Rank-based AS** | Rank-Based Ant System | Top $(w-1)$ ants deposit proportionally to rank; weighted sum |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level ACO Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Parameters alpha beta rho Q m] --> B[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau_ij to constant c]
    B --> C[Set iteration t = 1]
    C --> D{t less than T_max}
    D -- No --> Z[Return Best Solution Found]
    D -- Yes --> E[Place each ant k on a random starting node]
    E --> F[For each ant k construct solution using transition probability rule]
    F --> G[Compute tour length Lk for each ant]
    G --> H[Identify iteration best and global best tour]
    H --> I[Apply pheromone evaporation tau_ij = 1 minus rho times tau_ij]
    I --> J[Apply pheromone deposition tau_ij = tau_ij plus sum of delta tau_ij]
    J --> K[t = t + 1]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Ant Construction Step — Detailed View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ant k at node i] --> B[Determine allowed unvisited neighbors N_i_k]
    B --> C[Compute attractiveness for each edge ij as tau_ij to alpha times eta_ij to beta]
    C --> D[Normalize to get transition probabilities P_ij_k]
    D --> E[Generate random number r in 0 1]
    E --> F{r less than q0}
    F -- Yes --> G[Choose best edge greedily]
    F -- No --> H[Select edge via roulette wheel selection]
    G --> I[Move ant k to next node]
    H --> I
    I --> J{All cities visited}
    J -- No --> A
    J -- Yes --> K[Return to start node and complete tour]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of ACO System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Layer
        A1[Problem Instance e.g. distance matrix]
        A2[ACO Parameters alpha beta rho Q m]
    end
    subgraph Construction_Engine
        B1[Pheromone Memory tau_ij]
        B2[Heuristic Engine eta_ij]
        B3[Probabilistic Transition Rule]
        B4[Solution Builder ants construct tours]
    end
    subgraph Feedback_Engine
        C1[Tour Evaluator compute Lk]
        C2[Best Solution Tracker]
        C3[Evaporation Module 1 minus rho]
        C4[Deposition Module delta tau]
    end
    subgraph Output_Layer
        D1[Best Tour Found]
        D2[Convergence Curve]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> B1
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Convergence Behavior Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Iteration 1: Random Exploration] --> B[Pheromone Reinforces Better Edges]
    B --> C[Iteration 2 to 10: Guided Exploration]
    C --> D[Iteration 10 to 50: Convergence to Near Optimal]
    D --> E[Iteration 50+: Stable Optimal or Near Optimal Tour]
    E --> F[Termination: Return Best Tour]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the early iterations, ants explore broadly due to nearly uniform pheromone. As $\tau$ accumulates on short edges, exploration narrows. Eventually, the colony **converges** — though not always to the true global optimum (a known limitation of metaheuristics).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Ant Colony Optimization. List its key biological inspirations.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a **probabilistic, population-based metaheuristic** proposed by Marco Dorigo (1992) for solving combinatorial optimization problems. It is inspired by the **foraging behavior of real ants**, particularly their ability to find **shortest paths** between nest and food using **pheromone-mediated indirect communication (stigmergy)**.

**Key Biological Inspirations:**
1. **Pheromone trail laying** — ants deposit chemical substance on traversed paths.
2. **Trail following** — ants probabilistically follow stronger pheromone trails.
3. **Pheromone evaporation** — trails decay over time, allowing path forgetting.
4. **Positive feedback** — shorter paths get reinforced faster, leading to collective intelligence.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Explain the role of the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\rho$ in the ACO transition probability formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The transition probability $P_{ij}^k = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ uses three critical parameters:

- **$\alpha$ (Pheromone Influence)**: Controls how much an ant trusts accumulated pheromone. **High $\alpha$** $\Rightarrow$ exploitation of known good paths. **$\alpha = 0$** $\Rightarrow$ pure heuristic search.
- **$\beta$ (Heuristic Influence)**: Controls greediness based on local cost (e.g., distance). **High $\beta$** $\Rightarrow$ ants behave like a greedy heuristic. **$\beta = 0$** $\Rightarrow$ only pheromone matters.
- **$\rho$ (Evaporation Rate)**: Determines how fast old pheromone fades. **High $\rho$** $\Rightarrow$ faster forgetting, more exploration. **Low $\rho$** $\Rightarrow$ longer memory, more exploitation. Range: $0 < \rho < 1$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Ant Colony Optimization algorithm** with all its components: **transition rule, pheromone update rule, and evaporation mechanism**. Explain how positive feedback leads to convergence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following **4-city TSP** with distance matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{12} &= 5, \quad d_{13} = 10, \quad d_{14} = 8 \\
d_{23} &= 7, \quad d_{24} = 12, \quad d_{34} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Initial pheromone $\tau_{ij}(0) = 1$ on all edges. Using $m = 2$ ants, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 1$, $\rho = 0.2$, $Q = 10$, compute the **transition probabilities** for Ant 1 starting at city 1, and determine **Ant 1's first move**. Assume a random number $r = 0.42$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed Algorithm Description:**

**Step 1 — Initialization**: Pheromone trails $\tau_{ij}(0)$ are set to a small constant $c$ on every edge. Distance matrix $d_{ij}$ is given as input. Parameters $\alpha, \beta, \rho, Q$ are configured.

**Step 2 — Solution Construction**: Each ant $k$ starts at a randomly chosen node. At node $i$, it selects the next node $j$ using the **transition probability rule**:

$$
P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

where $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ is the heuristic visibility, and $N_i^k$ is the set of unvisited neighbors.

**Step 3 — Tour Completion**: Each ant constructs a complete Hamiltonian tour and computes its length $L_k$.

**Step 4 — Pheromone Update**: After all $m$ ants complete their tours, two operations occur on each edge $(i,j)$:

- **Evaporation** (prevents stagnation): $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}$
- **Deposition** (reinforces good solutions): $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow \tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k$, where $\Delta \tau_{ij}^k = \frac{Q}{L_k}$ if ant $k$ traversed $(i,j)$, else $0$.

**Step 5 — Convergence via Positive Feedback**: Shorter tours deposit more pheromone per edge (since $\Delta \tau \propto \frac{1}{L_k}$). These edges become more attractive in subsequent iterations, attracting more ants, which in turn deposit more pheromone. This **autocatalytic process** drives the colony toward the best discovered solution. Evaporation simultaneously prevents premature convergence on suboptimal paths.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heuristic values**:

$$
\eta_{12} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200, \quad \eta_{13} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.100, \quad \eta_{14} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator products** $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$ (with $\alpha = \beta = 1$, all $\tau_{ij} = 1$):

$$
\tau_{12} \cdot \eta_{12} = 1 \cdot 0.200 = 0.200
$$

$$
\tau_{13} \cdot \eta_{13} = 1 \cdot 0.100 = 0.100
$$

$$
\tau_{14} \cdot \eta_{14} = 1 \cdot 0.125 = 0.125
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator (sum)**:

$$
\sum = 0.200 + 0.100 + 0.125 = 0.425
$$

**Step 4 — Transition probabilities**:

$$
P_{12} = \frac{0.200}{0.425} = 0.471, \quad P_{13} = \frac{0.100}{0.425} = 0.235, \quad P_{14} = \frac{0.125}{0.425} = 0.294
$$

**Step 5 — Decision**: Random number $r = 0.42 < P_{12} = 0.471$. Therefore, **Ant 1 moves from City 1 → City 2**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: Students often forget to (1) **normalize** the probabilities properly, (2) **square/raise** $\eta$ to power $\beta$ when $\beta \neq 1$, or (3) include the **roulette-wheel cumulative range** when comparing with random $r$. **Always show the full numerator and denominator explicitly** to earn full method marks.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Compare the major **ACO variants** — Ant System (AS), Ant Colony System (ACS), and MAX-MIN Ant System (MMAS) — in a tabular form with respect to **pheromone update rule, exploration mechanism, and stagnation prevention**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of ACO. Explain with a real-world example how ACO outperforms a brute-force or greedy approach for an NP-hard routing problem. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of ACO Variants:**

| Feature | **Ant System (AS)** | **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | **MAX-MIN Ant System (MMAS)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pheromone Update** | All ants deposit after full tour | Only global-best ant updates; local update during construction | Only iteration-best or global-best updates |
| **Update Formula** | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{gb}$ | $\tau_{ij} = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$ |
| **Exploration Mechanism** | Probabilistic, $\alpha$ & $\beta$ balanced | Pseudo-random proportional rule with parameter $q_0$ | Probabilistic with bounded pheromone |
| **Pheromone Bounds** | None | None | $\tau_{ij} \in [\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ |
| **Stagnation Prevention** | Evaporation only | Evaporation + local update | Strict bounds + pheromone reinitialization |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow | Fast | Moderate to Fast |
| **Solution Quality** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Best Use Case** | Educational baseline | Real-time applications | High-quality optimization |

**(b) Advantages, Limitations & Real-World Superiority:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Parallelism** — Multiple ants construct solutions simultaneously.
2. **Positive feedback** — Drives rapid discovery of good solutions.
3. **Distributed computation** — Avoids single-point-of-failure.
4. **Adaptability** — Works for dynamic problems (e.g., routing in changing networks).
5. **Greedy heuristic integration** — Through $\eta_{ij}$ for informed search.

**Limitations:**
1. **No formal convergence guarantee** to global optimum.
2. **Parameter sensitivity** — Performance depends heavily on $\alpha, \beta, \rho$.
3. **Slower than greedy** for very small problems.
4. **Stagnation behavior** — Colony may converge prematurely to a local optimum.

**Real-World Example — Vehicle Routing with Time Windows (VRPTW)**:
A logistics company needs to deliver to **50 customers** within specific time windows. Brute-force evaluates $50! / (2 \cdot 50) \approx 3 \times 10^{62}$ tours — **impossible**. A greedy nearest-neighbor heuristic gives a solution quickly but typically **20–30% suboptimal**. ACO, however, uses pheromone memory from past good routes and heuristic distance to **iteratively improve solutions**, achieving near-optimal results within minutes — a 5–10% improvement over greedy, translating to **lakhs of rupees saved annually** in fuel and time costs for a mid-sized fleet.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: When asked to "explain advantages," students often list generic points without **mathematical/engineering justification** (e.g., "ACO is good because it uses ants"). Tie every advantage to a **specific formula or mechanism** to secure full marks. Use keywords like *pheromone intensification*, *stigmergy*, *NP-hard*, *polynomial complexity*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read before every exam!**

- ✅ ACO was invented by **Marco Dorigo (1992)** — remember the year and the researcher's name.
- ✅ ACO belongs to the **Swarm Intelligence** family of algorithms (along with PSO, ABC, Firefly).
- ✅ **Stigmergy** is the indirect communication mechanism via pheromone.
- ✅ The transition probability combines **pheromone ($\tau^\alpha$) and heuristic ($\eta^\beta$)** components multiplicatively.
- ✅ **Heuristic visibility** $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ — always invert the distance.
- ✅ **Evaporation rate** $\rho \in (0, 1)$ — higher $\rho$ = more exploration.
- ✅ **Pheromone deposit** $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \frac{Q}{L_k}$ — shorter tours contribute more.
- ✅ **ACS** uses a pseudo-random rule with parameter $q_0$ for the next-city choice.
- ✅ **MMAS** bounds pheromone in $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to avoid stagnation.
- ✅ ACO is best suited for **discrete combinatorial optimization** (TSP, VRP, scheduling).
- ✅ Convergence is achieved through **positive feedback**, not mathematical proof.
- ✅ The **3-marker** always tests definitions, $\alpha$/$\beta$/$\rho$ roles, or biological inspiration.
- ✅ The **14-marker** typically combines algorithm description + a TSP numerical or variant comparison.
- ✅ **Show full transition probability calculation** with explicit numerator and denominator in numericals.
- ✅ Common applications: **AntNet** (network routing), **vehicle routing**, **scheduling**, **bioinformatics**.
- ✅ ACO does **NOT guarantee global optimum** — only near-optimal in polynomial time.
- ✅ Always **normalize** probabilities so they sum to 1; verify your calculations sum to $\approx 1.000$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swarm Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST417 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swarm Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Swarm Robotics** is the study of how to coordinate groups of relatively simple, physically embodied robots (the *swarm*) to achieve a collective behaviour that emerges from the local interactions among the robots and between the robots and their environment. It is a sub-field of robotics that draws direct inspiration from **swarm intelligence** — the collective behaviour observed in natural biological systems such as ant colonies, bee hives, fish schools, and bird flocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard 2-Mark Answer):**
> *"Swarm Robotics is a decentralized approach to the coordination of large numbers of simple physical robots, where the collective behaviour emerges from local sensing, local communication, and local interactions, without any centralized controller."*

## 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Swarm** | A group of $\geq 10$ (typically hundreds/thousands) homogeneous robots |
| **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** | The collective, self-organized behaviour of decentralized systems |
| **Agent / Robot** | An autonomous, physically embodied entity with limited sensing/computation |
| **Emergence** | Complex global behaviour arising from simple local rules |
| **Stigmergy** | Indirect coordination through modification of the environment (e.g., pheromones) |
| **Locality** | Each robot can only sense/communicate within a finite neighbourhood |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a colony of ants carrying a large leaf back to the nest.**
> No single ant knows the full path. No ant is the "leader." Each ant follows 3 simple rules: (1) Follow the strongest pheromone trail, (2) Drop pheromone when carrying food, (3) Wander randomly if no trail found. From these 3 trivial rules, the *entire colony* solves complex problems like shortest-path routing, traffic management, and dead-end avoidance. **Swarm robotics replicates this exact principle with robots.**

## 1.4 Properties That Define a Swarm Robotic System (Mandatory for KTU)

1. **Autonomy** — Each robot operates without external supervision.
2. **Decentralization** — No central controller dictates behaviour.
3. **Homogeneity** (mostly) — Robots have similar hardware/software.
4. **Local Sensing & Communication** — Robots interact only within a limited range.
5. **Scalability** — Adding/removing robots does not break the system.
6. **Robustness / Fault Tolerance** — Loss of some robots does not stop the mission.
7. **Emergent Behaviour** — Global intelligence emerges from local rules.
8. **Flexibility** — The swarm can adapt to dynamic environments.

---

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for Exams — "ASDLORS"**
> **A**utonomy, **S**calability, **D**ecentralization, **L**ocal sensing, **O**rganization (self), **R**obustness, **S**implicity (of individuals).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Theoretical Principles (The "Why" Behind Swarm Robotics)

### A. Inspiration from Biological Swarms

| Biological System | Behaviour Studied | Engineering Analogue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ant Colony** | Pheromone-based shortest path | Network routing, exploration |
| **Bee Hive** | Waggle dance, swarm optimization | Task allocation, site selection |
| **Fish School / Bird Flock** | Reynolds Boids (cohesion, alignment, separation) | Flocking, formation control |
| **Bacterial Colony** | Chemotaxis, quorum sensing | Coverage, area patrolling |

### B. The Three Foundational Rules of Boids (Reynolds 1987) — *Frequently asked in KTU exams*

1. **Separation** — Steer to avoid crowding neighbours (collision avoidance).
2. **Alignment** — Steer towards the average heading of local neighbours.
3. **Cohesion** — Steer towards the average position of local neighbours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Statement of Boids:**
> Each rule produces a desired velocity vector $\vec{v}_d$. The final velocity is a weighted sum:
> $$\vec{v}_{\text{final}} = w_S \vec{v}_{\text{sep}} + w_A \vec{v}_{\text{aln}} + w_C \vec{v}_{\text{coh}} + w_O \vec{v}_{\text{obs}}$$
> where $w_S, w_A, w_C, w_O \geq 0$ are tunable weights, and $\vec{v}_{\text{obs}}$ represents obstacle avoidance.

### C. Communication Topologies in Swarm Robotics

1. **Explicit (Direct) Communication** — Robots broadcast/receive messages (e.g., Wi-Fi, infrared).
2. **Implicit (Stigmergic) Communication** — Robots modify the environment (e.g., leave markers, change lighting).
3. **No Communication** — Purely reactive behaviour using only local sensing (e.g., vision, proximity).

### D. Mathematical Foundations (PSO — Particle Swarm Optimization)

Particle Swarm Optimization is the most relevant algorithm from swarm intelligence applied within swarm robotic contexts (e.g., for path planning, foraging). The update equations are:

$$v_i(t+1) = w \cdot v_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \left[p_{\text{best}} - x_i(t)\right] + c_2 r_2 \left[g_{\text{best}} - x_i(t)\right]$$

$$x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + v_i(t+1)$$

where:
- $v_i(t)$ = velocity of particle $i$ at iteration $t$
- $x_i(t)$ = position of particle $i$
- $w$ = **inertia weight** (typically $0.4 \leq w \leq 0.9$)
- $c_1, c_2$ = cognitive and social acceleration constants
- $r_1, r_2$ = uniform random numbers in $[0, 1]$
- $p_{\text{best}}$ = particle's personal best position
- $g_{\text{best}}$ = swarm's global best position

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Description | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reynolds Boids Rule | $\vec{v}_{\text{final}} = w_S \vec{v}_{\text{sep}} + w_A \vec{v}_{\text{aln}} + w_C \vec{v}_{\text{coh}}$ | Flocking, formation control |
| PSO Velocity Update | $v_{id} = w v_{id} + c_1 r_1 (p_{id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2 (g_{d} - x_{id})$ | Path planning, optimization |
| PSO Position Update | $x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)$ | Updating robot position |
| Pheromone Evaporation | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho) \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij}$ | ACO for routing |
| Constriction Coefficient | $\chi = \frac{2k}{\mid \phi - 2 \mid + \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)}}$ | Clerc's PSO stability |
| Communication Range | $R_c = 2R_s$ (where $R_s$ is sensing range) | Network connectivity |
| Swarm Coverage | $A_{\text{covered}} \leq N \cdot \pi R_s^2$ | Area surveillance estimate |
| Mean Inter-Robot Distance | $d_{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{\frac{A}{N \cdot \pi}}$ | Density analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Replacement:** In the KTU formula table, the absolute value `|φ - 2|` is written as `\mid \phi - 2 \mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. This is per the **vertical pipe restriction rule**.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility (Where Swarm Robotics Is Applied)

1. **Search and Rescue (SAR)** — Post-disaster rubble exploration.
2. **Precision Agriculture** — Coordinated sowing, weeding, harvesting.
3. **Military Reconnaissance** — Surveillance in hostile zones.
4. **Warehouse Automation** — Amazon-style Kiva robot swarms.
5. **Planetary Exploration** — NASA swarms for Mars cave/terrain mapping.
6. **Pollution Monitoring** — Distributed water/air quality sampling.
7. **Medical Micro-Swimmers** — Targeted drug delivery inside the human body.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Rule for PSO (Clerc & Kennedy):** For convergence, the parameter $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ must satisfy $\phi > 4$, and the constriction coefficient is applied to the velocity term to prevent explosion. **This is a guaranteed 5-mark KTU question almost every year.**

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Constriction Coefficient of PSO (Clerc's Method)

The PSO recurrence relation can be rewritten in matrix form for analysis. Consider a single particle in 1-D, with no stochasticity ($r_1 = r_2 = 1$) and assuming $p_{\text{best}} = g_{\text{best}} = p$ (a fixed point).

$$\begin{aligned}
v(t+1) &= w \cdot v(t) + c_1 [p - x(t)] + c_2 [p - x(t)] \\
v(t+1) &= w \cdot v(t) + (c_1 + c_2)[p - x(t)] \\
v(t+1) &= w \cdot v(t) + \phi [p - x(t)]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1:** We define the deviation from the optimum: $y(t) = p - x(t)$. Then $y(t+1) = p - x(t+1) = p - [x(t) + v(t+1)]$.

**Step 2:** Substituting $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$:
$$y(t+1) = p - x(t) - v(t+1) = y(t) - v(t+1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the velocity update:
$$\begin{aligned}
v(t+1) &= w \cdot v(t) + \phi \cdot y(t) \\
y(t+1) &= y(t) - [w \cdot v(t) + \phi \cdot y(t)] \\
y(t+1) &= (1 - \phi) y(t) - w \cdot v(t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Write the system in matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} y(t+1) \\ v(t+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \phi & -w \\ \phi & w \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} y(t) \\ v(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** For convergence, the eigenvalues $\lambda$ of this matrix must lie inside the unit circle ($\mid \lambda \mid < 1$). The characteristic equation is:
$$\lambda^2 - (w + 1 - \phi)\lambda + w = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solving the quadratic:
$$\lambda = \frac{(w + 1 - \phi) \pm \sqrt{(w + 1 - \phi)^2 - 4w}}{2}$$

**Step 7:** For the system to be critically damped and converge fast, the discriminant is set to zero (boundary of the stability region):
$$(w + 1 - \phi)^2 = 4w$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $w$ yields the maximum stable inertia weight:
$$w_{\max} = \frac{(\phi - 1) + \sqrt{\phi^2 - 2\phi + 1 - 4w_{\max}}}{2}$$
which after standard algebraic manipulation gives Clerc's result. With $w = \chi$ as a constriction factor and $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\phi = 4.1$:

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\mid 2 - \phi \mid + \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}}$$

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\mid 2 - 4.1 \mid + \sqrt{4.1^2 - 4(4.1)}}$$

**Step 9:** Numerical evaluation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mid 2 - 4.1 \mid &= 2.1 \\
4.1^2 - 4(4.1) &= 16.81 - 16.4 = 0.41 \\
\sqrt{0.41} &\approx 0.6403 \\
\chi &= \frac{2}{2.1 + 0.6403} = \frac{2}{2.7403} \approx 0.7298
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\chi \approx 0.7298$, which is the canonical constriction coefficient. **This guarantees convergence.**

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Reynolds Boids Weighted Vector Sum

Starting from the three Reynolds rules applied to a robot $i$ in a swarm of $N$ robots.

**Step 1: Separation vector** — Sum of vectors pointing *away* from robots within distance $d_{\text{sep}}$:
$$\vec{v}_{\text{sep}} = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_{\text{sep}}} \frac{\vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j}{\Vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j \Vert}$$

**Step 2: Alignment vector** — Average heading of neighbours within $d_{\text{aln}}$:
$$\vec{v}_{\text{aln}} = \frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{N}_{\text{aln}} \mid} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_{\text{aln}}} \vec{v}_j$$

**Step 3: Cohesion vector** — Vector towards the centre of mass of neighbours within $d_{\text{coh}}$:
$$\vec{v}_{\text{coh}} = \frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{N}_{\text{coh}} \mid} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_{\text{coh}}} \vec{x}_j - \vec{x}_i$$

**Step 4: Total steering command**:
$$\vec{a}_i = w_S \vec{v}_{\text{sep}} + w_A \vec{v}_{\text{aln}} + w_C \vec{v}_{\text{coh}}$$

**Step 5: Normalize to max speed $v_{\max}$**:
$$\vec{v}_i(t+1) = \frac{\vec{a}_i}{\Vert \vec{a}_i \Vert} \cdot v_{\max}$$

**Step 6: Update position**:
$$\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1) \cdot \Delta t$$

---

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hint:** When asked "Show that PSO can converge," you must derive up to the eigenvalues step. Examiners give full 7 marks only if the characteristic equation is reached.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define swarm robotics. List any four key properties that distinguish a swarm robotic system from a multi-robot system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Swarm robotics is the study of coordinating large numbers (typically $\geq 10$) of simple, homogeneous, physically embodied robots using decentralized control, where the collective behaviour emerges from local interactions.
- **Four Properties (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
  1. **Decentralized control** — no central leader.
  2. **Local sensing and communication** — robots only sense within a finite range $R_c$.
  3. **Scalability** — performance is invariant to the number of robots.
  4. **Robustness** — failure of some robots does not halt the mission.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of stigmergy in swarm robotics with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Stigmergy is a form of *indirect communication* in which robots coordinate by modifying the environment rather than by explicit message passing.
- **Example (2 marks):** A swarm of foraging robots in a warehouse leaves virtual "pheromone" markers (e.g., gradient of LED brightness on the floor) to indicate the path to a target. Other robots follow the strongest gradient, reinforcing it, while unused paths fade (evaporate). This emulates ant pheromone behaviour.
- **Significance (1 mark):** Stigmergy enables coordination even when robots cannot directly communicate, supporting massive scalability.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss the biological inspiration for swarm robotics. With neat diagrams, explain the **Reynolds Boids model** and derive the steering rule for flocking. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Biological Inspiration (2 Marks):**
- **Ants** use pheromone trails → Ant Colony Optimization (ACO).
- **Bees** perform waggle dances → Artificial Bee Colony (ABC).
- **Birds and fish** exhibit coordinated flocking → Reynolds Boids.
- **Bacteria** perform chemotaxis → Bacterial Foraging Optimization.

**(ii) Reynolds Boids (3 Marks):**
Craig Reynolds (1987) proposed that flocking emerges from three local rules:

| Rule | Description | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Separation | Avoid crowding neighbours | $\vec{v}_S = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_S} \frac{\vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j}{\Vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j \Vert}$ |
| Alignment | Match neighbours' velocity | $\vec{v}_A = \frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{N}_A \mid} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_A} \vec{v}_j$ |
| Cohesion | Move toward local centre | $\vec{v}_C = \frac{1}{\mid \mathcal{N}_C \mid} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_C} \vec{x}_j - \vec{x}_i$ |

**(iii) Derivation of Steering Rule (2 Marks):**
$$\vec{a}_i = w_S \vec{v}_S + w_A \vec{v}_A + w_C \vec{v}_C$$
The robot's velocity is normalized and scaled to $v_{\max}$, and its position updates as:
$$\vec{x}_i(t+1) = \vec{x}_i(t) + \vec{v}_i(t+1) \cdot \Delta t$$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** algorithm. Derive the velocity and position update equations. Briefly mention the role of the **inertia weight** $w$ and the **constriction coefficient** $\chi$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) PSO Overview (2 Marks):**
PSO, proposed by Kennedy and Eberhart (1995), is a population-based stochastic optimization technique inspired by bird flocking. Each "particle" represents a candidate solution moving through a search space.

**(ii) Update Equations (3 Marks):**
$$v_{id}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 [p_{id} - x_{id}(t)] + c_2 r_2 [g_d - x_{id}(t)]$$
$$x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)$$

**(iii) Inertia Weight $w$ (1 Mark):** Controls exploration vs exploitation. Higher $w$ encourages global search; lower $w$ encourages local refinement. Typical range: $0.4 \leq w \leq 0.9$.

**(iv) Constriction Coefficient $\chi$ (1 Mark):** Introduced by Clerc & Kennedy to guarantee convergence:
$$\chi = \frac{2}{\mid 2 - \phi \mid + \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}}, \quad \phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$$
For $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$, $\chi \approx 0.7298$. The constriction prevents velocity explosion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a swarm robotic system**. Discuss the role of each layer in coordinating the swarm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
The swarm architecture has four layers (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):
1. **Sensor Layer** — LiDAR, IR, IMU, cameras for local perception.
2. **Local Perception Module** — Filters noise, identifies neighbours within $R_c$.
3. **Behaviour Engine** — Executes Boids / PSO / ACO rules.
4. **Actuator Layer** — Motors/wheels producing physical motion.

**(ii) Role of Each Layer (4 Marks):**
- **Sensor Layer:** Provides raw data $\rightarrow$ maps environment into local state.
- **Local Perception:** Converts raw data to neighbour positions $\vec{x}_j$ and velocities $\vec{v}_j$.
- **Behaviour Engine:** Computes the steering vector $\vec{a}_i$ using weighted rules.
- **Actuator Layer:** Executes motion command, closing the feedback loop with the environment.

---

**Q. (b)** List and explain **any five real-world applications** of swarm robotics. Discuss **three major challenges** faced in deploying swarm systems. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Five Applications (5 × 1 = 5 Marks):**
1. **Search and Rescue** — Rubble exploration post-earthquake.
2. **Precision Agriculture** — Coordinated seeding and weeding.
3. **Warehouse Automation** — Amazon Kiva robots for shelf transport.
4. **Military Reconnaissance** — Surveillance of hostile zones.
5. **Environmental Monitoring** — Distributed air/water quality sampling.

**(ii) Three Challenges (2 Marks):**
1. **Inter-robot communication bottlenecks** at large $N$ (e.g., RF interference).
2. **Limited on-board power and computation** in small robots.
3. **Verification of emergent behaviour** — hard to formally prove safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Swarm Robotics with Multi-Robot Systems (MRS).** MRS may have centralized planners; swarms are *strictly* decentralized. Students lose 1-2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT use Python code in soft-computing derivations.** Examiners expect mathematical derivations only. Code gets zero credit.
> 3. **Always mention the *biological inspiration* first** when explaining any swarm algorithm. Examiners allot 2 marks just for this preamble.
> 4. **In the PSO update equation,** always show all three terms (inertia + cognitive + social). Skipping any term loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Reynolds Boids requires mentioning "1987" and "Craig Reynolds"** explicitly for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Swarm Robotics = Decentralized + Local sensing + Emergent behaviour.**
- Key properties: **ASDLORS** (Autonomy, Scalability, Decentralization, Local sensing, self-Organization, Robustness, Simplicity).
- **Three Reynolds Boids Rules:** Separation, Alignment, Cohesion (must know the year 1987 and author).
- **PSO Update Equations:** Velocity has 3 components (inertia, cognitive, social); position is $x(t) + v(t+1)$.
- **Constriction Coefficient** $\chi \approx 0.7298$ for $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ (this exact number is asked).
- **Inertia weight** controls the exploration-exploitation trade-off; $0.4 \leq w \leq 0.9$.
- **Stigmergy = indirect communication** via environmental modification (e.g., pheromones).
- **Differences to remember:**
  - **Swarm vs MRS:** Swarm is decentralized; MRS may have a central planner.
  - **Explicit vs Implicit Communication:** Explicit = message passing; Implicit = stigmergy.
- **Communication topologies:** Direct RF, Infrared, Vision-based, Stigmergy.
- **Biological sources:** Ants → ACO, Bees → ABC, Birds/Fish → Boids, Bacteria → BFO.
- **Convergence condition:** $\phi = c_1 + c_2 > 4$ (Clerc-Kennedy stability theorem).
- **Key applications:** Search and Rescue, Agriculture, Warehousing, Military, Exploration, Medical.
- **Challenges:** Communication bottleneck, power limits, formal verification, sensor noise, fault tolerance.
- **Standard swarm size:** $\geq 10$ robots (defining threshold for KTU).
- **Typical sensing range** $R_s$, communication range $R_c \approx 2R_s$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Power Recall:** If you remember only 3 things — (1) Reynolds Boids = 3 rules, (2) PSO = inertia + cognitive + social, (3) Stigmergy = environment-based indirect comm — you will secure at least 10/14 in any swarm robotics question.
